saa-01-cloud-global-infrastructure./title: Global Infrastructure and the Shared Responsibility Model
saa-01-cloud-global-infrastructure./shortTitle: Global Infrastructure and the Shared Responsibility Model
saa-01-cloud-global-infrastructure./summary: Understand global infrastructure and the shared responsibility model through requirements, constraints, trade-offs, and an architectural decision that can be explained to the BlueRetail team.
saa-01-cloud-global-infrastructure./opening/0: BlueRetail is changing its architecture, and its website must serve multiple geographic areas, but the project team does not yet distinguish between AZ failures, Region failures, and its own configuration responsibilities. A diagram with many AWS icons is not necessarily a good design. What matters is which requirement each component addresses, which failure modes remain, and whether the operations team can manage it. Lesson 1 begins with this scenario and turns global infrastructure and the shared responsibility model into a sequence of evidence-based decisions rather than a list of features to memorize.
saa-01-cloud-global-infrastructure./opening/1: In SAA-C03, questions often present several technically feasible options, but only one best matches keywords such as “least operational effort,” “no data loss,” “low cost,” “multiple accounts,” or “no public internet.” Global infrastructure and the shared responsibility model should be evaluated from four perspectives: Region, Availability Zone, edge location, and shared responsibility. Each perspective eliminates a group of answers if they conflict with the requirements or introduce unacceptable responsibilities.
saa-01-cloud-global-infrastructure./opening/2: The scope of Lesson 1—Global Infrastructure and the Shared Responsibility Model—is aligned with objective SAA-C03 1.2 in the current AWS Exam Guide. The GitHub topic repository is used only to verify coverage and content organization. All explanations, cases, and diagrams here are original, while the technical conclusions are validated against AWS Documentation and the Well-Architected Framework. After this lesson, you should be able to explain not only what to choose, but also why the other options should not be chosen.
saa-01-cloud-global-infrastructure./sections/0/title: Thinking Model: Start with Requirements, Not Services
saa-01-cloud-global-infrastructure./sections/0/paragraphs/0: Think of an AWS architecture as a contract among business, application, and platform teams. The business defines what must remain operational and what level of loss is acceptable. The application describes traffic, data, and dependencies. The platform translates those requirements into boundaries, services, policies, and observability mechanisms. For global infrastructure and the shared responsibility model, the region is the starting point, the availability zone determines how the system responds, and the edge location and shared responsibility determine long-term operability. Going directly from requirements to service names often causes one of these four layers to be overlooked.
saa-01-cloud-global-infrastructure./sections/0/paragraphs/1: A simple model that works for every lesson is R-C-D-E: Requirement, Constraint, Decision, Evidence. A requirement describes the outcome; a constraint limits the scope through factors such as Region, budget, or skills; a decision is the selected architectural structure; and evidence is a metric, log, test, or document confirming that the decision remains valid. By applying R-C-D-E to global infrastructure and the shared responsibility model, learners can see that the same service may be appropriate for one workload but inappropriate for another. This skill matters more than memorizing the entire AWS catalog.
saa-01-cloud-global-infrastructure./sections/0/paragraphs/2: You have understood Lesson 1 when you can trace a request or operational event from beginning to end. Which boundaries does the request cross? Which identity is evaluated? Where is the data stored? Which components can fail, and which mechanisms restore them? If the story breaks at the region or availability zone, the diagram still contains an unverified assumption. Record that assumption beside the diagram before selecting another service.
saa-01-cloud-global-infrastructure./sections/1/title: Four Concepts That Must Be Connected
saa-01-cloud-global-infrastructure./sections/1/paragraphs/0: Region is the first component in a global infrastructure and shared responsibility decision, but its value becomes clear only when considered alongside business requirements. For SAA-C03, learners should not stop at asking, “What is this service used for?” Identify the input the region receives, the state or policy it manages, the failure mode it addresses, and the operational cost it creates. At BlueRetail, the website must serve multiple geographic areas, but the project team does not yet distinguish among AZ failures, Region failures, and its own configuration responsibilities. Connecting region with availability zone reveals where the responsibility boundary lies and why two similarly named options can produce different outcomes. A good solution must trace each technical choice back to latency, availability, security, data, or the operations team’s capabilities.
saa-01-cloud-global-infrastructure./sections/1/paragraphs/1: Availability Zone is the second component in a global infrastructure and shared responsibility decision, but its value becomes clear only when considered alongside business requirements. For SAA-C03, learners should not stop at asking, “What is this service used for?” Identify the input the availability zone receives, the state or policy it manages, the failure mode it addresses, and the operational cost it creates. At BlueRetail, the website must serve multiple geographic areas, but the project team does not yet distinguish among AZ failures, Region failures, and its own configuration responsibilities. Connecting availability zone with edge location reveals where the responsibility boundary lies and why two similarly named options can produce different outcomes. A good solution must trace each technical choice back to latency, availability, security, data, or the operations team’s capabilities.
saa-01-cloud-global-infrastructure./sections/1/paragraphs/2: edge location is the third component in a global infrastructure and shared responsibility decision, but its value becomes clear only when considered alongside business requirements. For SAA-C03, learners should not stop at asking, “What is this service used for?” Identify the input the edge location receives, the state or policy it manages, the failure mode it addresses, and the operational cost it creates. At BlueRetail, the website must serve multiple geographic areas, but the project team does not yet distinguish among AZ failures, Region failures, and its own configuration responsibilities. Connecting edge location with shared responsibility reveals where the responsibility boundary lies and why two similarly named options can produce different outcomes. A good solution must trace each technical choice back to latency, availability, security, data, or the operations team’s capabilities.
saa-01-cloud-global-infrastructure./sections/1/paragraphs/3: shared responsibility is the fourth component in a global infrastructure and shared responsibility decision, but its value becomes clear only when considered alongside business requirements. For SAA-C03, learners should not stop at asking, “What is this service used for?” Identify the input shared responsibility receives, the state or policy it manages, the failure mode it addresses, and the operational cost it creates. At BlueRetail, the website must serve multiple geographic areas, but the project team does not yet distinguish among AZ failures, Region failures, and its own configuration responsibilities. Connecting shared responsibility with region reveals where the responsibility boundary lies and why two similarly named options can produce different outcomes. A good solution must trace each technical choice back to latency, availability, security, data, or the operations team’s capabilities.
saa-01-cloud-global-infrastructure./sections/2/title: Make Decisions Step by Step and Record the Trade-offs
saa-01-cloud-global-infrastructure./sections/2/paragraphs/0: A decision involving region should be documented as a chain of reasoning, not as an isolated service name. First, confirm which requirements are mandatory. Then check quota limits, Region, consistency, network path, and the responsibility model. Next, compare region with edge location using the same criteria; do not compare one feature of option A with the total cost of option B. Finally, state the remaining trade-off and how it will be observed after implementation. In Lesson 1, this approach helps BlueRetail understand why the selected option is good enough in the current context and which signals would indicate that the architecture needs to be reconsidered.
saa-01-cloud-global-infrastructure./sections/2/paragraphs/1: A decision involving availability zone should be documented as a chain of reasoning, not as an isolated service name. First, confirm which requirements are mandatory. Then check quota limits, Region, consistency, network path, and the responsibility model. Next, compare availability zone with shared responsibility using the same criteria; do not compare one feature of option A with the total cost of option B. Finally, state the remaining trade-off and how it will be observed after implementation. In Lesson 1, this approach helps BlueRetail understand why the selected option is good enough in the current context and which signals would indicate that the architecture needs to be reconsidered.
saa-01-cloud-global-infrastructure./sections/2/paragraphs/2: A decision involving edge location should be documented as a chain of reasoning, not as an isolated service name. First, confirm which requirements are mandatory. Then check quota limits, Region, consistency, network path, and the responsibility model. Next, compare edge location with region using the same criteria; do not compare one feature of option A with the total cost of option B. Finally, state the remaining trade-off and how it will be observed after implementation. In Lesson 1, this approach helps BlueRetail understand why the selected option is good enough in the current context and which signals would indicate that the architecture needs to be reconsidered.
saa-01-cloud-global-infrastructure./sections/2/paragraphs/3: A decision involving shared responsibility should be documented as a chain of reasoning, not as an isolated service name. First, confirm which requirements are mandatory. Then check quota limits, Region, consistency, network path, and the responsibility model. Next, compare shared responsibility with availability zone using the same criteria; do not compare one feature of option A with the total cost of option B. Finally, state the remaining trade-off and how it will be observed after implementation. In Lesson 1, this approach helps BlueRetail understand why the selected option is good enough in the current context and which signals would indicate that the architecture needs to be reconsidered.
saa-01-cloud-global-infrastructure./sections/3/title: Design for Failure Before It Occurs
saa-01-cloud-global-infrastructure./sections/3/paragraphs/0: Failure mode 1 for global infrastructure and the shared responsibility model occurs when the project team assumes that region automatically handles every related layer. In reality, service availability does not replace correct access permissions, replication does not replace backups, encryption does not automatically grant permission to use a key, and autoscaling does not fix a stateful dependency. For BlueRetail, map the blast radius of region and mark the areas for which AWS is responsible and those that the application team must still configure or test. Then select a metric, log, or event that can provide an early warning. Analyzing failure modes first ensures that the answer is not only correct while the system is healthy, but also provides a recovery path when an AZ, connection, credential, or operational process fails.
saa-01-cloud-global-infrastructure./sections/3/paragraphs/1: Failure mode 2 for global infrastructure and the shared responsibility model occurs when the project team assumes that availability zone automatically handles every related layer. In reality, service availability does not replace correct access permissions, replication does not replace backups, encryption does not automatically grant permission to use a key, and autoscaling does not fix a stateful dependency. For BlueRetail, map the blast radius of availability zone and mark the areas for which AWS is responsible and those that the application team must still configure or test. Then select a metric, log, or event that can provide an early warning. Analyzing failure modes first ensures that the answer is not only correct while the system is healthy, but also provides a recovery path when an AZ, connection, credential, or operational process fails.
saa-01-cloud-global-infrastructure./sections/3/paragraphs/2: Failure mode 3 for global infrastructure and the shared responsibility model occurs when the project team assumes that edge location automatically handles every related layer. In reality, service availability does not replace correct access permissions, replication does not replace backups, encryption does not automatically grant permission to use a key, and autoscaling does not fix a stateful dependency. For BlueRetail, map the blast radius of edge location and mark the areas for which AWS is responsible and those that the application team must still configure or test. Then select a metric, log, or event that can provide an early warning. Analyzing failure modes first ensures that the answer is not only correct while the system is healthy, but also provides a recovery path when an AZ, connection, credential, or operational process fails.
saa-01-cloud-global-infrastructure./sections/4/title: Apply the Decision to the BlueRetail Case
saa-01-cloud-global-infrastructure./sections/4/paragraphs/0: BlueRetail has stores, an e-commerce website, internal applications, and an operations team working across multiple accounts. In Lesson 1, the website must serve multiple geographic areas, but the project team does not yet distinguish among AZ failures, Region failures, and its own configuration responsibilities. The first step is to separate the stakeholders: users need a consistent experience, security needs boundaries and an audit trail, finance needs cost attribution, and the product team needs to make changes quickly. A design optimized for only one stakeholder creates operational debt for another.
saa-01-cloud-global-infrastructure./sections/4/paragraphs/1: The architecture team documents three options centered on Region, Availability Zone, and edge location. Each option is scored using the same matrix: fulfillment of mandatory requirements, blast radius, recovery path, operational burden, scalability, and cost per unit of usage. shared responsibility serves as the final validation condition. If an option wins only because the team is familiar with it, the decision lacks sufficient evidence and the constraints must be revisited.
saa-01-cloud-global-infrastructure./sections/4/paragraphs/2: The conclusion for global infrastructure and the shared responsibility model is written in four sentences: which structure is selected, which requirements it satisfies, which trade-off is accepted, and which metric or review will trigger a reassessment. This format helps developers, security, and operations understand the same decision. In exam questions, these four sentences also help eliminate distractors: an option that fails a mandatory requirement is eliminated first, even if it offers many appealing features.
saa-01-cloud-global-infrastructure./sections/5/title: Choosing the Right Action in SAA-C03 Questions
saa-01-cloud-global-infrastructure./sections/5/paragraphs/0: Read an SAA-C03 question in this order: the assigned role, the current state, the required outcome, the constraints, and the verb at the end of the question. For global infrastructure and the shared responsibility model, mark every detail related to region and availability zone. Then classify each answer as fully satisfying the requirements, partially satisfying them, creating additional risk, or being out of scope. Do not choose an answer simply because it names the correct service; AWS tests your ability to combine multiple requirements into the most appropriate solution.
saa-01-cloud-global-infrastructure./sections/5/paragraphs/1: Absolute phrases such as “no data loss,” “no downtime,” or “all traffic must remain private” require careful evaluation. They may require a very different design from phrases such as “minimize,” “acceptable,” or “prioritize cost.” In Lesson 1, use edge location to validate the required assurance level and shared responsibility to determine whether the operations team can sustain the option. An answer that provides the right feature but the wrong assurance level is still a distractor.
saa-01-cloud-global-infrastructure./sections/5/paragraphs/2: In Lesson 1—Global Infrastructure and the Shared Responsibility Model—objective SAA-C03 1.2 indicates the required depth. For design, you must balance quality attributes. For determine, you must interpret the facts and select a cause or option. For improve, you must preserve the existing constraints while reducing risk through an appropriate change. End each answer with evidence: a metric, log, policy evaluation, recovery test, or cost report. Evidence turns an answer from a guess into a defensible architectural decision.
saa-01-cloud-global-infrastructure./visuals/0/title: Decision Map: Global Infrastructure and the Shared Responsibility Model
saa-01-cloud-global-infrastructure./visuals/0/caption: An original diagram connecting requirements with Region, Availability Zone, edge location, and shared responsibility in the BlueRetail case.
saa-01-cloud-global-infrastructure./decisionWalkthroughs/0/title: Global Infrastructure and Shared Responsibility Decision Analysis
saa-01-cloud-global-infrastructure./decisionWalkthroughs/0/businessRequirem: BlueRetail must address the scenario in which “the website must serve multiple geographic areas, but the project team does not yet distinguish among AZ failures, Region failures, and its own configuration responsibilities,” while still being able to explain the effects on security, resilience, performance, and cost.
saa-01-cloud-global-infrastructure./decisionWalkthroughs/0/technicalConstra: The design must clarify the Region boundary and the responsibility of the Availability Zone.
The option must include a failure mode and a recovery path related to edge location.
The operations team must use real data to observe and reassess shared responsibility.
saa-01-cloud-global-infrastructure./decisionWalkthroughs/0/candidates/0/fit: Appropriate when the requirement prioritizes the region perspective in step 1.
saa-01-cloud-global-infrastructure./decisionWalkthroughs/0/candidates/0/lim: Does not automatically address availability zone and requires validation of the remaining responsibilities.
saa-01-cloud-global-infrastructure./decisionWalkthroughs/0/candidates/1/fit: Appropriate when the requirement prioritizes the availability zone perspective in step 2.
saa-01-cloud-global-infrastructure./decisionWalkthroughs/0/candidates/1/lim: Does not automatically address edge location and requires validation of the remaining responsibilities.
saa-01-cloud-global-infrastructure./decisionWalkthroughs/0/candidates/2/fit: Appropriate when the requirement prioritizes the edge location perspective in step 3.
saa-01-cloud-global-infrastructure./decisionWalkthroughs/0/candidates/2/lim: Does not automatically address shared responsibility and requires validation of the remaining responsibilities.
saa-01-cloud-global-infrastructure./decisionWalkthroughs/0/decision: Select an option that combines Region with Availability Zone, uses edge location as a guardrail, and uses shared responsibility as operational evidence.
saa-01-cloud-global-infrastructure./decisionWalkthroughs/0/reasoning/0: Eliminate options that do not satisfy mandatory requirements before comparing costs.
saa-01-cloud-global-infrastructure./decisionWalkthroughs/0/reasoning/1: Compare options using the same quality attributes and the same time horizon.
saa-01-cloud-global-infrastructure./decisionWalkthroughs/0/reasoning/2: Clearly document the trade-off, blast radius, and responsibilities that remain with the application team.
saa-01-cloud-global-infrastructure./decisionWalkthroughs/0/reasoning/3: Identify the metric or review that would require the decision to be reconsidered.
saa-01-cloud-global-infrastructure./comparison/0/bestFit: Prioritize region when the primary requirement is addressed in step 1 of the decision.
saa-01-cloud-global-infrastructure./comparison/0/blueRetailUse: BlueRetail uses region to address the scenario in which the website must serve multiple geographic areas, but the project team does not yet distinguish among AZ failures, Region failures, and its own configuration responsibilities.

saa-01-cloud-global-infrastructure./comparison/1/bestFit: Prioritize availability zone when the primary requirement is addressed in step 2 of the decision.
saa-01-cloud-global-infrastructure./comparison/1/blueRetailUse: BlueRetail uses availability zone to address the scenario in which the website must serve multiple geographic areas, but the project team does not yet distinguish among AZ failures, Region failures, and its own configuration responsibilities.
saa-01-cloud-global-infrastructure./comparison/1/tradeOff: edge location must be added or validated; do not treat this as a solution for every layer.
saa-01-cloud-global-infrastructure./comparison/2/bestFit: Prioritize edge location when the primary requirement is addressed in step 3 of the decision.
saa-01-cloud-global-infrastructure./comparison/2/blueRetailUse: BlueRetail uses edge location to address the scenario in which the website must serve multiple geographic areas, but the project team does not yet distinguish among AZ failures, Region failures, and its own configuration responsibilities.

saa-01-cloud-global-infrastructure./commonMistakes/0/wrong: Seeing the keyword Region and immediately choosing the answer with the matching service or feature name.
saa-01-cloud-global-infrastructure./commonMistakes/0/better: Compare region with the requirement, constraints, and availability zone before choosing.
saa-01-cloud-global-infrastructure./commonMistakes/0/reason: In Lesson 1, an option is correct only when the entire chain of responsibilities and failure modes is addressed.
saa-01-cloud-global-infrastructure./commonMistakes/1/wrong: Seeing the keyword Availability Zone and immediately choosing the answer with the matching service or feature name.
saa-01-cloud-global-infrastructure./commonMistakes/1/better: Compare availability zone with the requirement, constraints, and edge location before choosing.
saa-01-cloud-global-infrastructure./commonMistakes/1/reason: In Lesson 1, an option is correct only when the entire chain of responsibilities and failure modes is addressed.
saa-01-cloud-global-infrastructure./commonMistakes/2/wrong: Seeing the keyword edge location and immediately choosing the answer with the matching service or feature name.
saa-01-cloud-global-infrastructure./commonMistakes/2/better: Compare edge location with the requirement, constraints, and shared responsibility before choosing.
saa-01-cloud-global-infrastructure./commonMistakes/2/reason: In Lesson 1, an option is correct only when the entire chain of responsibilities and failure modes is addressed.
saa-01-cloud-global-infrastructure./examFocus/0: Read the verb in objective SAA-C03 1.2 to determine the required depth of the answer.
saa-01-cloud-global-infrastructure./examFocus/1: Distinguish mandatory requirements from background details related to Region.
saa-01-cloud-global-infrastructure./examFocus/2: Eliminate answers that transfer risk from Availability Zone to edge location without actually addressing it.
saa-01-cloud-global-infrastructure./examFocus/3: Choose the option with an appropriate operational burden and evidence through shared responsibility.
saa-01-cloud-global-infrastructure./activity/title: Architecture Review: Global Infrastructure and the Shared Responsibility Model
saa-01-cloud-global-infrastructure./activity/context: BlueRetail is preparing to approve a change because its website must serve multiple geographic areas, but the project team does not yet distinguish among AZ failures, Region failures, and its own configuration responsibilities. You must review the architecture before the team implements it or makes any cost commitments.
saa-01-cloud-global-infrastructure./activity/learnerRole: You are the Solutions Architect responsible for a workload. You must translate requirements into a decision and clearly communicate the trade-off to stakeholders.
saa-01-cloud-global-infrastructure./activity/currentArchitecture: Users → access layer → workload → data store; the review points are Region → Availability Zone → edge location → shared responsibility.
saa-01-cloud-global-infrastructure./activity/requirements/0: The solution must correctly address the requirement related to Region.
saa-01-cloud-global-infrastructure./activity/requirements/1: The recovery path must demonstrate the role of the Availability Zone.
saa-01-cloud-global-infrastructure./activity/requirements/2: Security and operations must be able to observe edge location without relying on assumptions.
saa-01-cloud-global-infrastructure./activity/constraints/0: Do not add a service merely to conceal a requirement that has not been clearly defined.
saa-01-cloud-global-infrastructure./activity/constraints/1: The trade-off involving shared responsibility must be accepted by the stakeholders.
saa-01-cloud-global-infrastructure./activity/constraints/2: Do not change the storage schema or account boundary without an architectural reason.
saa-01-cloud-global-infrastructure./activity/tasks/0: Rewrite the requirement as measurable criteria.
saa-01-cloud-global-infrastructure./activity/tasks/1: Compare at least two options based on Region, Availability Zone, and edge location.
saa-01-cloud-global-infrastructure./activity/tasks/2: Select an option, document the trade-off, and propose post-implementation evidence.
saa-01-cloud-global-infrastructure./activity/reasoningSteps/0: Separate the business outcome, technical constraints, and unverified assumptions.
saa-01-cloud-global-infrastructure./activity/reasoningSteps/1: Diagram the request/data flow together with trust boundaries and blast radius.
saa-01-cloud-global-infrastructure./activity/reasoningSteps/2: Eliminate options that fail mandatory requirements before optimizing.
saa-01-cloud-global-infrastructure./activity/reasoningSteps/3: Compare the remaining options across reliability, security, performance, cost, and operations.
saa-01-cloud-global-infrastructure./activity/reasoningSteps/4: Document the decision, trade-off, recovery path, and review metric.
saa-01-cloud-global-infrastructure./activity/deliverables/0: An architecture diagram showing boundaries and data-flow direction.
saa-01-cloud-global-infrastructure./activity/deliverables/1: A decision matrix with at least two options.
saa-01-cloud-global-infrastructure./activity/deliverables/2: A brief architecture decision record that includes the trade-off and evidence.
saa-01-cloud-global-infrastructure./activity/rubric/0/evidence: The decision can be traced back to Region and introduces no assumptions beyond the scenario.
saa-01-cloud-global-infrastructure./activity/rubric/0/importance: Prevents selecting services based on preference or a single keyword.
saa-01-cloud-global-infrastructure./activity/rubric/1/evidence: Compares Availability Zone, edge location, and shared responsibility using the same criteria.
saa-01-cloud-global-infrastructure./activity/rubric/1/importance: AWS architecture always involves balance; there is no universally best option.
saa-01-cloud-global-infrastructure./activity/rubric/2/evidence: The submission identifies a failure mode, recovery path, and validating metric or test.
saa-01-cloud-global-infrastructure./activity/rubric/2/importance: A design is trustworthy only if it can be operated and validated after implementation.
saa-01-cloud-global-infrastructure./activity/sampleAnalysis/0: The primary requirement is rewritten around Region; Availability Zone is a constraint that directly affects the choice.
saa-01-cloud-global-infrastructure./activity/sampleAnalysis/1: An option that prioritizes edge location is eliminated if it increases the operational burden of shared responsibility without adding value.
saa-01-cloud-global-infrastructure./activity/sampleAnalysis/2: The proposed architecture combines Region and Availability Zone and clearly documents the blast radius, recovery path, and review metric.
saa-01-cloud-global-infrastructure./activity/sampleAnalysis/3: The remaining trade-off is accepted because it fits the scenario in which the website must serve multiple geographic areas, but the project team does not yet distinguish among AZ failures, Region failures, and its own configuration responsibilities.
saa-01-cloud-global-infrastructure./activity/commonMistakes/0: Listing services without connecting them to the requirement.
saa-01-cloud-global-infrastructure./activity/commonMistakes/1: Describing only the happy path and ignoring failure modes.
saa-01-cloud-global-infrastructure./activity/commonMistakes/2: Saying “AWS manages it automatically” without identifying the customer’s responsibilities.
saa-01-cloud-global-infrastructure./activity/reflectionQuestions/0: Which detail in the scenario eliminated the most options?
saa-01-cloud-global-infrastructure./activity/reflectionQuestions/1: Which metric or event would cause you to reconsider the decision?
saa-01-cloud-global-infrastructure./activity/reflectionQuestions/2: If the budget or RTO changes, which part of the architecture must change first?
saa-01-cloud-global-infrastructure./recap/0: Global infrastructure and the shared responsibility model must begin with requirements and constraints.
saa-01-cloud-global-infrastructure./recap/1: Region, Availability Zone, edge location, and shared responsibility form a chain of responsibilities.
saa-01-cloud-global-infrastructure./recap/2: Every decision requires a trade-off, recovery path, and operational evidence.
saa-01-cloud-global-infrastructure./recap/3: In exam questions, eliminate options that fail mandatory requirements before comparing optimizations.
saa-01-cloud-global-infrastructure./selfCheck/0: Can you explain the difference between the roles of Region and Availability Zone?
saa-01-cloud-global-infrastructure./selfCheck/1: Which failure mode relates to edge location, and what is the corresponding recovery path?
saa-01-cloud-global-infrastructure./selfCheck/2: Why can shared responsibility change an architectural choice?
saa-01-cloud-global-infrastructure./selfCheck/3: Which metric, log, or test would you use to validate the decision?

saa-02-well-architected-requirements./title: From Business Requirements to AWS Well-Architected
saa-02-well-architected-requirements./shortTitle: From Business Requirements to AWS Well-Architected
saa-02-well-architected-requirements./summary: Understand the journey from business requirements to AWS Well-Architected through requirements, constraints, trade-offs, and an architecture decision that can be explained to the BlueRetail team.
saa-02-well-architected-requirements./opening/0: BlueRetail is changing its architecture, and all stakeholders say the system must improve, but each interprets security, reliability, and cost differently. A diagram filled with AWS icons is not necessarily a good design. What matters is which requirement each component addresses, which failure modes remain, and whether the operations team can manage it. Lesson 2 begins with this situation and turns the journey from business requirements to AWS Well-Architected into a sequence of evidence-based decisions rather than a list of features to memorize.
saa-02-well-architected-requirements./opening/1: In SAA-C03, questions often present several technically feasible options, but only one best matches keywords such as “least operational effort,” “no data loss,” “low cost,” “multiple accounts,” or “no use of the public internet.” The journey from business requirements to AWS Well-Architected must be examined from four perspectives: quality attribute, Well-Architected pillar, workload boundary, and architecture decision. Each perspective eliminates a group of answers that conflict with the requirements or introduce unacceptable responsibilities.
saa-02-well-architected-requirements./opening/2: The scope of Lesson 2—From Business Requirements to AWS Well-Architected—is aligned with objective SAA-C03 2.2 in the current AWS Exam Guide. The GitHub topic repository is used only to verify coverage and content organization. All explanations, cases, and diagrams here are newly written, while the technical conclusions are validated against AWS Documentation and the Well-Architected Framework. After this lesson, you should be able to explain not only “what to choose,” but also “why the other options should not be chosen.”
saa-02-well-architected-requirements./sections/0/title: Thinking Model: Start with Requirements, Not Services
saa-02-well-architected-requirements./sections/0/paragraphs/0: Treat an AWS architecture as a contract among business, application, and platform teams. The business identifies what must remain operational and the acceptable level of loss. The application defines traffic, data, and dependencies. The platform translates those requirements into boundaries, services, policies, and observability mechanisms. When moving from business requirements to AWS Well-Architected, the quality attribute is the entry point, the Well-Architected pillar indicates how the system should respond, and the workload boundary and architecture decision determine long-term operability. Moving directly from a requirement to a service name often omits one of these four layers.
saa-02-well-architected-requirements./sections/0/paragraphs/1: A simple model that works for every exercise is R-C-D-E: Requirement, Constraint, Decision, Evidence. A requirement describes the outcome. A constraint limits the scope through factors such as Region, budget, or skills. A decision is the selected architecture structure. Evidence consists of metrics, logs, tests, or documentation confirming that the decision remains valid. By applying R-C-D-E to From Business Requirements to AWS Well-Architected, learners can see that the same service may be correct for one workload but wrong for another. This skill is more important than memorizing the entire AWS catalog.
saa-02-well-architected-requirements./sections/0/paragraphs/2: You have understood Lesson 2 when you can trace a request or operational event from beginning to end. Which boundaries does the request cross? Which identity is evaluated? Where is the data retained? Which component can fail, and which mechanism restores it? If the narrative breaks at a quality attribute or Well-Architected pillar, the diagram still contains an unverified assumption. Record that assumption beside the diagram before selecting another service.
saa-02-well-architected-requirements./sections/1/title: Four Concepts That Must Be Connected
saa-02-well-architected-requirements./sections/1/paragraphs/0: The quality attribute is the first component of a decision leading from business requirements to AWS Well-Architected, but it provides value only when considered alongside the business requirement. For SAA-C03, learners should not stop at asking, “What is this service used for?” Identify the input received by the quality attribute, the state or policy it manages, the failure mode it addresses, and the resulting operational cost. At BlueRetail, all stakeholders say the system must improve, but each interprets security, reliability, and cost differently. Connecting the quality attribute to the Well-Architected pillar reveals where the responsibility boundary lies and why two similarly named options produce different outcomes. A strong solution must be traceable from the technical choice back to latency, availability, security, data, or the operations team's capabilities.
saa-02-well-architected-requirements./sections/1/paragraphs/1: The Well-Architected pillar is the second component of a decision leading from business requirements to AWS Well-Architected, but it provides value only when considered alongside the business requirement. For SAA-C03, learners should not stop at asking, “What is this service used for?” Identify the input received by the Well-Architected pillar, the state or policy it manages, the failure mode it addresses, and the resulting operational cost. At BlueRetail, all stakeholders say the system must improve, but each interprets security, reliability, and cost differently. Connecting the Well-Architected pillar to the workload boundary reveals where the responsibility boundary lies and why two similarly named options produce different outcomes. A strong solution must be traceable from the technical choice back to latency, availability, security, data, or the operations team's capabilities.
saa-02-well-architected-requirements./sections/1/paragraphs/2: The workload boundary is the third component of a decision leading from business requirements to AWS Well-Architected, but it provides value only when considered alongside the business requirement. For SAA-C03, learners should not stop at asking, “What is this service used for?” Identify the input received by the workload boundary, the state or policy it manages, the failure mode it addresses, and the resulting operational cost. At BlueRetail, all stakeholders say the system must improve, but each interprets security, reliability, and cost differently. Connecting the workload boundary to the architecture decision reveals where the responsibility boundary lies and why two similarly named options produce different outcomes. A strong solution must be traceable from the technical choice back to latency, availability, security, data, or the operations team's capabilities.
saa-02-well-architected-requirements./sections/1/paragraphs/3: The architecture decision is the fourth component of a decision leading from business requirements to AWS Well-Architected, but it provides value only when considered alongside the business requirement. For SAA-C03, learners should not stop at asking, “What is this service used for?” Identify the input received by the architecture decision, the state or policy it manages, the failure mode it addresses, and the resulting operational cost. At BlueRetail, all stakeholders say the system must improve, but each interprets security, reliability, and cost differently. Connecting the architecture decision to the quality attribute reveals where the responsibility boundary lies and why two similarly named options produce different outcomes. A strong solution must be traceable from the technical choice back to latency, availability, security, data, or the operations team's capabilities.
saa-02-well-architected-requirements./sections/2/title: Make Decisions Step by Step and Document the Trade-Offs
saa-02-well-architected-requirements./sections/2/paragraphs/0: A decision involving a quality attribute should be documented as a chain of reasoning, not as a standalone service name. First, confirm which requirements are mandatory, then check quota limits, Region, consistency, network path, and the responsibility model. Next, compare the quality attribute with the workload boundary using the same criteria; do not compare one feature of option A with the total cost of option B. Finally, state the remaining trade-off and how it will be observed after deployment. In Lesson 2, this approach helps BlueRetail understand why the selected option is good enough in the current context and which signals would trigger an architecture review.
saa-02-well-architected-requirements./sections/2/paragraphs/1: A decision involving a Well-Architected pillar should be documented as a chain of reasoning, not as a standalone service name. First, confirm which requirements are mandatory, then check quota limits, Region, consistency, network path, and the responsibility model. Next, compare the Well-Architected pillar with the architecture decision using the same criteria; do not compare one feature of option A with the total cost of option B. Finally, state the remaining trade-off and how it will be observed after deployment. In Lesson 2, this approach helps BlueRetail understand why the selected option is good enough in the current context and which signals would trigger an architecture review.
saa-02-well-architected-requirements./sections/2/paragraphs/2: A decision involving a workload boundary should be documented as a chain of reasoning, not as a standalone service name. First, confirm which requirements are mandatory, then check quota limits, Region, consistency, network path, and the responsibility model. Next, compare the workload boundary with the quality attribute using the same criteria; do not compare one feature of option A with the total cost of option B. Finally, state the remaining trade-off and how it will be observed after deployment. In Lesson 2, this approach helps BlueRetail understand why the selected option is good enough in the current context and which signals would trigger an architecture review.
saa-02-well-architected-requirements./sections/2/paragraphs/3: A decision involving an architecture decision should be documented as a chain of reasoning, not as a standalone service name. First, confirm which requirements are mandatory, then check quota limits, Region, consistency, network path, and the responsibility model. Next, compare the architecture decision with the Well-Architected pillar using the same criteria; do not compare one feature of option A with the total cost of option B. Finally, state the remaining trade-off and how it will be observed after deployment. In Lesson 2, this approach helps BlueRetail understand why the selected option is good enough in the current context and which signals would trigger an architecture review.
saa-02-well-architected-requirements./sections/3/title: Design for Failure Before Failure Occurs
saa-02-well-architected-requirements./sections/3/paragraphs/0: Failure mode 1 in the journey from business requirements to AWS Well-Architected occurs when the project team assumes that the quality attribute automatically addresses every related layer. In reality, service availability does not replace proper access controls, replication does not replace backup, encryption does not automatically grant permission to use a key, and autoscaling does not fix a stateful dependency. For BlueRetail, diagram the blast radius of the quality attribute and mark the portion for which AWS is responsible and the portion the application team must still configure or test. Then select a metric, log, or event that can provide an early warning. Analyzing the failure mode first ensures that the answer works not only when the system is healthy but also provides a recovery path when an AZ, connection, credential, or operational process fails.
saa-02-well-architected-requirements./sections/3/paragraphs/1: Failure mode 2 in the journey from business requirements to AWS Well-Architected occurs when the project team assumes that the Well-Architected pillar automatically addresses every related layer. In reality, service availability does not replace proper access controls, replication does not replace backup, encryption does not automatically grant permission to use a key, and autoscaling does not fix a stateful dependency. For BlueRetail, diagram the blast radius of the Well-Architected pillar and mark the portion for which AWS is responsible and the portion the application team must still configure or test. Then select a metric, log, or event that can provide an early warning. Analyzing the failure mode first ensures that the answer works not only when the system is healthy but also provides a recovery path when an AZ, connection, credential, or operational process fails.
saa-02-well-architected-requirements./sections/3/paragraphs/2: Failure mode 3 in the journey from business requirements to AWS Well-Architected occurs when the project team assumes that the workload boundary automatically addresses every related layer. In reality, service availability does not replace proper access controls, replication does not replace backup, encryption does not automatically grant permission to use a key, and autoscaling does not fix a stateful dependency. For BlueRetail, diagram the blast radius of the workload boundary and mark the portion for which AWS is responsible and the portion the application team must still configure or test. Then select a metric, log, or event that can provide an early warning. Analyzing the failure mode first ensures that the answer works not only when the system is healthy but also provides a recovery path when an AZ, connection, credential, or operational process fails.
saa-02-well-architected-requirements./sections/4/title: Apply the Decision to the BlueRetail Case
saa-02-well-architected-requirements./sections/4/paragraphs/0: BlueRetail has physical stores, an e-commerce website, internal applications, and an operations team working across multiple accounts. In Lesson 2, all stakeholders say the system must improve, but each interprets security, reliability, and cost differently. The first step is to separate the stakeholder needs: users require a consistent experience, security requires boundaries and an audit trail, finance requires cost attribution, and the product team needs to make changes quickly. A design optimized for only one stakeholder creates operational debt for another.
saa-02-well-architected-requirements./sections/4/paragraphs/1: The architecture team documents three options centered on the quality attribute, Well-Architected pillar, and workload boundary. Each option is scored using the same matrix: fulfillment of mandatory requirements, blast radius, recovery path, operational burden, scalability, and cost per unit of use. The architecture decision serves as the final validation condition. If an option wins only because the team is familiar with it, the decision lacks sufficient evidence and the team must revisit the constraint.
saa-02-well-architected-requirements./sections/4/paragraphs/2: The conclusion for the journey from business requirements to AWS Well-Architected is written in four sentences: which structure is selected, which requirements it meets, which trade-off is accepted, and which metric or review will trigger reevaluation. This format helps developers, security, and operations understand the same decision. In exam questions, the four sentences also help eliminate distractors: an option that fails to meet a mandatory requirement is eliminated first, even if it offers many attractive features.
saa-02-well-architected-requirements./sections/5/title: How to Select an Action in SAA-C03 Questions
saa-02-well-architected-requirements./sections/5/paragraphs/0: Read an SAA-C03 question in this order: the assigned role, current state, mandatory outcome, constraint, and the verb in the final sentence. For the journey from business requirements to AWS Well-Architected, mark every detail related to the quality attribute and Well-Architected pillar. Then classify each answer as fully meeting the requirements, partially meeting them, introducing additional risk, or being out of scope. Do not choose an answer merely because it names the correct service; AWS tests your ability to combine multiple requirements into the most appropriate solution.
saa-02-well-architected-requirements./sections/5/paragraphs/1: Absolute phrases such as “no data loss,” “no downtime,” or “all traffic must remain private” require careful examination. They may call for a very different design from phrases such as “minimize,” “acceptable,” or “prioritize cost.” In Lesson 2, use the workload boundary to validate the assurance level and the architecture decision to determine whether the operations team can sustain the option. An answer with the correct feature but the wrong assurance level is still a distractor.
saa-02-well-architected-requirements./sections/5/paragraphs/2: In Lesson 2—From Business Requirements to AWS Well-Architected—objective SAA-C03 2.2 indicates the required depth. For design, you must balance quality attributes. For determine, you must interpret the facts and select a cause or option. For improve, you must retain existing constraints and reduce risk through an appropriate change. Conclude each answer with evidence: a metric, log, policy evaluation, recovery test, or cost report. Evidence turns an answer from a guess into a defensible architecture decision.
saa-02-well-architected-requirements./visuals/0/title: Decision Map: From Business Requirements to AWS Well-Architected
saa-02-well-architected-requirements./visuals/0/caption: An original diagram connecting the requirement to the quality attribute, Well-Architected pillar, workload boundary, and architecture decision in the BlueRetail case.
saa-02-well-architected-requirements./decisionWalkthroughs/0/title: Decision Analysis: From Business Requirements to AWS Well-Architected
saa-02-well-architected-requirements./decisionWalkthroughs/0/businessRequirem: BlueRetail must address the situation in which “all stakeholders say the system must improve, but each interprets security, reliability, and cost differently,” while still being able to explain the effects on security, resilience, performance, and cost.
saa-02-well-architected-requirements./decisionWalkthroughs/0/technicalConstra: The design must clarify the boundary of the quality attribute and the responsibility of the Well-Architected pillar.
The option must include a failure mode and a recovery path related to the workload boundary.
The operations team must be able to observe and reevaluate the architecture decision using real data.
saa-02-well-architected-requirements./decisionWalkthroughs/0/candidates/0/fit: Suitable when the requirement prioritizes the quality attribute perspective in step 1.
saa-02-well-architected-requirements./decisionWalkthroughs/0/candidates/0/lim: Does not address the Well-Architected pillar by itself, and the remaining responsibilities must be verified.
saa-02-well-architected-requirements./decisionWalkthroughs/0/candidates/1/fit: Suitable when the requirement prioritizes the Well-Architected pillar perspective in step 2.
saa-02-well-architected-requirements./decisionWalkthroughs/0/candidates/1/lim: Does not address the workload boundary by itself, and the remaining responsibilities must be verified.
saa-02-well-architected-requirements./decisionWalkthroughs/0/candidates/2/fit: Suitable when the requirement prioritizes the workload boundary perspective in step 3.
saa-02-well-architected-requirements./decisionWalkthroughs/0/candidates/2/lim: Does not address the architecture decision by itself, and the remaining responsibilities must be verified.
saa-02-well-architected-requirements./decisionWalkthroughs/0/decision: Select an option that combines the quality attribute with the Well-Architected pillar, uses the workload boundary as a guardrail, and uses the architecture decision as operational evidence.
saa-02-well-architected-requirements./decisionWalkthroughs/0/reasoning/0: Eliminate options that do not meet mandatory requirements before comparing costs.
saa-02-well-architected-requirements./decisionWalkthroughs/0/reasoning/1: Compare options using the same quality attributes and the same time horizon.
saa-02-well-architected-requirements./decisionWalkthroughs/0/reasoning/2: Clearly document the trade-off, blast radius, and responsibilities that remain with the application team.
saa-02-well-architected-requirements./decisionWalkthroughs/0/reasoning/3: Identify the metric or review that would trigger reconsideration of the decision.
saa-02-well-architected-requirements./comparison/0/bestFit: Prioritize the quality attribute when the main requirement is associated with step 1 of the decision.
saa-02-well-architected-requirements./comparison/0/blueRetailUse: BlueRetail uses the quality attribute to address the situation in which all stakeholders say the system must improve, but each interprets security, reliability, and cost differently.
saa-02-well-architected-requirements./comparison/0/tradeOff: The Well-Architected pillar must be added or verified; do not treat this as a solution for every layer.
saa-02-well-architected-requirements./comparison/1/bestFit: Prioritize the Well-Architected pillar when the main requirement is associated with step 2 of the decision.
saa-02-well-architected-requirements./comparison/1/blueRetailUse: BlueRetail uses the Well-Architected pillar to address the situation in which all stakeholders say the system must improve, but each interprets security, reliability, and cost differently.
saa-02-well-architected-requirements./comparison/1/tradeOff: The workload boundary must be added or verified; do not treat this as a solution for every layer.
saa-02-well-architected-requirements./comparison/2/bestFit: Prioritize the workload boundary when the main requirement is associated with step 3 of the decision.
saa-02-well-architected-requirements./comparison/2/blueRetailUse: BlueRetail uses the workload boundary to address the situation in which all stakeholders say the system must improve, but each interprets security, reliability, and cost differently.
saa-02-well-architected-requirements./comparison/2/tradeOff: The architecture decision must be added or verified; do not treat this as a solution for every layer.
saa-02-well-architected-requirements./commonMistakes/0/wrong: Seeing the keyword quality attribute and immediately selecting an answer with the same service or feature name.
saa-02-well-architected-requirements./commonMistakes/0/better: Compare the quality attribute against the requirement, constraint, and Well-Architected pillar before making a selection.
saa-02-well-architected-requirements./commonMistakes/0/reason: In Lesson 2, a choice is correct only when the entire chain of responsibilities and failure modes is addressed.
saa-02-well-architected-requirements./commonMistakes/1/wrong: Seeing the keyword Well-Architected pillar and immediately selecting an answer with the same service or feature name.
saa-02-well-architected-requirements./commonMistakes/1/better: Compare the Well-Architected pillar against the requirement, constraint, and workload boundary before making a selection.
saa-02-well-architected-requirements./commonMistakes/1/reason: In Lesson 2, a choice is correct only when the entire chain of responsibilities and failure modes is addressed.
saa-02-well-architected-requirements./commonMistakes/2/wrong: Seeing the keyword workload boundary and immediately selecting an answer with the same service or feature name.
saa-02-well-architected-requirements./commonMistakes/2/better: Compare the workload boundary against the requirement, constraint, and architecture decision before making a selection.
saa-02-well-architected-requirements./commonMistakes/2/reason: In Lesson 2, a choice is correct only when the entire chain of responsibilities and failure modes is addressed.
saa-02-well-architected-requirements./examFocus/0: Read the verb in objective SAA-C03 2.2 to determine the required depth of the answer.
saa-02-well-architected-requirements./examFocus/1: Distinguish mandatory requirements from background details related to the quality attribute.
saa-02-well-architected-requirements./examFocus/2: Eliminate answers that transfer risk from the Well-Architected pillar to the workload boundary without actually addressing it.
saa-02-well-architected-requirements./examFocus/3: Select an option with an appropriate operational burden and evidence provided through the architecture decision.
saa-02-well-architected-requirements./activity/title: Architecture Review: From Business Requirements to AWS Well-Architected
saa-02-well-architected-requirements./activity/context: BlueRetail is preparing to approve a change because all stakeholders say the system must improve, but each interprets security, reliability, and cost differently. You must review the architecture before the team deploys it or incurs a cost commitment.
saa-02-well-architected-requirements./activity/learnerRole: You are the Solutions Architect responsible for a workload, translating requirements into decisions and clearly communicating trade-offs to stakeholders.
saa-02-well-architected-requirements./activity/currentArchitecture: Users → access layer → workload → data store; the review points are quality attribute → Well-Architected pillar → workload boundary → architecture decision.
saa-02-well-architected-requirements./activity/requirements/0: The solution must correctly address the requirement related to the quality attribute.
saa-02-well-architected-requirements./activity/requirements/1: The recovery path must demonstrate the role of the Well-Architected pillar.
saa-02-well-architected-requirements./activity/requirements/2: Security and operations must be able to observe the workload boundary without relying on assumptions.
saa-02-well-architected-requirements./activity/constraints/0: Do not add a service merely to conceal a requirement that has not been clearly defined.
saa-02-well-architected-requirements./activity/constraints/1: The trade-off associated with the architecture decision must be accepted by the stakeholder.
saa-02-well-architected-requirements./activity/constraints/2: Do not change the storage schema or account boundary without an architectural justification.
saa-02-well-architected-requirements./activity/tasks/0: Rewrite the requirement as measurable criteria.
saa-02-well-architected-requirements./activity/tasks/1: Compare at least two options based on the quality attribute, Well-Architected pillar, and workload boundary.
saa-02-well-architected-requirements./activity/tasks/2: Select an option, document the trade-off, and propose post-deployment evidence.
saa-02-well-architected-requirements./activity/reasoningSteps/0: Separate the business outcome, technical constraint, and unverified assumptions.
saa-02-well-architected-requirements./activity/reasoningSteps/1: Diagram the request and data flow, including the trust boundary and blast radius.
saa-02-well-architected-requirements./activity/reasoningSteps/2: Eliminate options that do not meet mandatory requirements before optimizing.
saa-02-well-architected-requirements./activity/reasoningSteps/3: Compare the remaining options across reliability, security, performance, cost, and operations.
saa-02-well-architected-requirements./activity/reasoningSteps/4: Document the decision, trade-off, recovery path, and review metric.
saa-02-well-architected-requirements./activity/deliverables/0: An architecture diagram showing boundaries and data flow direction.
saa-02-well-architected-requirements./activity/deliverables/1: A decision matrix comparing at least two options.
saa-02-well-architected-requirements./activity/deliverables/2: A concise architecture decision record containing the trade-off and evidence.
saa-02-well-architected-requirements./activity/rubric/0/evidence: The decision can be traced back to the quality attribute and introduces no assumptions beyond the scenario.
saa-02-well-architected-requirements./activity/rubric/0/importance: Prevents services from being selected based on preference or a single keyword.
saa-02-well-architected-requirements./activity/rubric/1/evidence: The Well-Architected pillar, workload boundary, and architecture decision are compared using the same criteria.
saa-02-well-architected-requirements./activity/rubric/1/importance: AWS architecture always involves balance; there is no universally best option.
saa-02-well-architected-requirements./activity/rubric/2/evidence: The submission identifies a failure mode, recovery path, metric, or validation test.
saa-02-well-architected-requirements./activity/rubric/2/importance: A design is trustworthy only when it can be operated and validated after deployment.
saa-02-well-architected-requirements./activity/sampleAnalysis/0: The main requirement is rewritten around the quality attribute; the Well-Architected pillar is a constraint that directly affects the choice.
saa-02-well-architected-requirements./activity/sampleAnalysis/1: An option that prioritizes the workload boundary is eliminated if it increases the operational burden of the architecture decision without adding value.
saa-02-well-architected-requirements./activity/sampleAnalysis/2: The proposed architecture combines the quality attribute and Well-Architected pillar and clearly documents the blast radius, recovery path, and review metric.
saa-02-well-architected-requirements./activity/sampleAnalysis/3: The remaining trade-off is accepted because it fits the situation in which all stakeholders say the system must improve, but each interprets security, reliability, and cost differently.
saa-02-well-architected-requirements./activity/commonMistakes/0: Listing services without connecting them to the requirement.
saa-02-well-architected-requirements./activity/commonMistakes/1: Describing only the happy path and ignoring failure modes.
saa-02-well-architected-requirements./activity/commonMistakes/2: Saying that “AWS manages it automatically” without identifying the customer's responsibilities.
saa-02-well-architected-requirements./activity/reflectionQuestions/0: Which detail in the scenario eliminated the most options?
saa-02-well-architected-requirements./activity/reflectionQuestions/1: Which metric or event would cause you to reconsider the decision?
saa-02-well-architected-requirements./activity/reflectionQuestions/2: If the budget or RTO changes, which part of the architecture must change first?
saa-02-well-architected-requirements./recap/0: The journey from business requirements to AWS Well-Architected must begin with the requirement and constraint.
saa-02-well-architected-requirements./recap/1: The quality attribute, Well-Architected pillar, workload boundary, and architecture decision form a chain of responsibilities.
saa-02-well-architected-requirements./recap/2: Every decision requires a trade-off, recovery path, and operational evidence.
saa-02-well-architected-requirements./recap/3: In exam questions, eliminate options that fail to meet mandatory requirements before comparing optimizations.
saa-02-well-architected-requirements./selfCheck/0: Can you explain the difference between the roles of the quality attribute and the Well-Architected pillar?
