saa-05-organizations-scp-account-boundar./activity/title: Architecture Review: Organizations, SCP, and Multi-Account Boundaries
saa-05-organizations-scp-account-boundar./activity/context: BlueRetail is preparing to approve a change that separates production from development while retaining shared guardrails without granting direct access from the management account. You must review the architecture before the team implements it or incurs a cost commitment.
saa-05-organizations-scp-account-boundar./activity/learnerRole: You are the Solutions Architect responsible for a workload. You must translate requirements into a decision and clearly explain the trade-off to stakeholders.
saa-05-organizations-scp-account-boundar./activity/currentArchitecture: Users → access layer → workload → data store; the review points are organization → organizational unit → service control policy → account boundary.
saa-05-organizations-scp-account-boundar./activity/requirements/0: The solution must correctly address the requirement related to the organization.
saa-05-organizations-scp-account-boundar./activity/requirements/1: The recovery path must reflect the role of the organizational unit.
saa-05-organizations-scp-account-boundar./activity/requirements/2: Security and operations must be able to observe the service control policy without relying on assumptions.
saa-05-organizations-scp-account-boundar./activity/constraints/0: Do not add a service merely to conceal a requirement that has not been clearly defined.
saa-05-organizations-scp-account-boundar./activity/constraints/1: Stakeholders must accept the trade-off involving the account boundary.
saa-05-organizations-scp-account-boundar./activity/constraints/2: Do not change the storage schema or account boundary without an architectural justification.
saa-05-organizations-scp-account-boundar./activity/tasks/0: Rewrite the requirement as measurable criteria.
saa-05-organizations-scp-account-boundar./activity/tasks/1: Compare at least two options based on the organization, organizational unit, and service control policy.
saa-05-organizations-scp-account-boundar./activity/tasks/2: Select an option, document the trade-off, and propose post-implementation evidence.
saa-05-organizations-scp-account-boundar./activity/reasoningSteps/0: Separate the business outcome, technical constraints, and unconfirmed assumptions.
saa-05-organizations-scp-account-boundar./activity/reasoningSteps/1: Diagram the request and data flows together with trust boundaries and blast radii.
saa-05-organizations-scp-account-boundar./activity/reasoningSteps/2: Eliminate options that fail mandatory requirements before optimizing.
saa-05-organizations-scp-account-boundar./activity/reasoningSteps/3: Compare the remaining options across reliability, security, performance, cost, and operations.
saa-05-organizations-scp-account-boundar./activity/reasoningSteps/4: Document the decision, trade-off, recovery path, and review metric.
saa-05-organizations-scp-account-boundar./activity/deliverables/0: An architecture diagram showing boundaries and data-flow directions.
saa-05-organizations-scp-account-boundar./activity/deliverables/1: A decision matrix containing at least two options.
saa-05-organizations-scp-account-boundar./activity/deliverables/2: A brief architecture decision record containing the trade-off and evidence.
saa-05-organizations-scp-account-boundar./activity/rubric/0/evidence: The decision can be traced back to the organization and introduces no assumptions beyond the scenario.
saa-05-organizations-scp-account-boundar./activity/rubric/0/importance: Prevents services from being selected based on preference or an isolated keyword.
saa-05-organizations-scp-account-boundar./activity/rubric/1/evidence: The organizational unit, service control policy, and account boundary are compared using the same criteria.
saa-05-organizations-scp-account-boundar./activity/rubric/1/importance: AWS architecture always involves balancing priorities; no option is universally best.
saa-05-organizations-scp-account-boundar./activity/rubric/2/evidence: The submission identifies a failure mode, recovery path, and validating metric or test.
saa-05-organizations-scp-account-boundar./activity/rubric/2/importance: A design is trustworthy only if it can be operated and validated after implementation.
saa-05-organizations-scp-account-boundar./activity/sampleAnalysis/0: The primary requirement is rewritten around the organization; the organizational unit is a constraint that directly affects the choice.
saa-05-organizations-scp-account-boundar./activity/sampleAnalysis/1: The option that prioritizes the service control policy is eliminated if it increases the operational burden of the account boundary without adding value.
saa-05-organizations-scp-account-boundar./activity/sampleAnalysis/2: The proposed architecture combines the organization and organizational unit and clearly documents the blast radius, recovery path, and review metric.
saa-05-organizations-scp-account-boundar./activity/sampleAnalysis/3: The remaining trade-off is accepted because it fits the scenario in which production is separated from development but shared guardrails are still required without granting direct access from the management account.
saa-05-organizations-scp-account-boundar./activity/commonMistakes/0: Listing services without connecting them to the requirement.
saa-05-organizations-scp-account-boundar./activity/commonMistakes/1: Describing only the happy path and ignoring failure modes.
saa-05-organizations-scp-account-boundar./activity/commonMistakes/2: Saying “AWS manages it” without identifying the customer's responsibilities.
saa-05-organizations-scp-account-boundar./activity/reflectionQuestions/0: Which detail in the scenario eliminated the most options?
saa-05-organizations-scp-account-boundar./activity/reflectionQuestions/1: Which metric or event would cause you to reassess the decision?
saa-05-organizations-scp-account-boundar./activity/reflectionQuestions/2: If the budget or RTO changes, which part of the architecture must change first?
saa-05-organizations-scp-account-boundar./recap/0: Organizations, SCP, and multi-account boundaries must begin with requirements and constraints.
saa-05-organizations-scp-account-boundar./recap/1: The organization, organizational unit, service control policy, and account boundary form a chain of responsibilities.
saa-05-organizations-scp-account-boundar./recap/2: Every decision requires a trade-off, recovery path, and operational evidence.
saa-05-organizations-scp-account-boundar./recap/3: In exam questions, eliminate options that fail mandatory requirements before comparing optimizations.

saa-05-organizations-scp-account-boundar./selfCheck/1: Which failure mode is related to the service control policy, and what is the corresponding recovery path?
saa-05-organizations-scp-account-boundar./selfCheck/2: Why can the account boundary change the architectural choice?

saa-06-ec2-architecture-instance-familie./title: EC2 architecture and instance family selection
saa-06-ec2-architecture-instance-familie./shortTitle: EC2 architecture and instance family selection
saa-06-ec2-architecture-instance-familie./summary: Understand EC2 architecture and instance family selection through requirements, constraints, trade-offs, and an architectural decision that can be explained to the BlueRetail team.
saa-06-ec2-architecture-instance-familie./opening/0: BlueRetail is changing its architecture, and its order service slows down during peak hours. The team plans to increase the instance size without first identifying the bottleneck. A diagram with many AWS icons is not necessarily a good design: what matters is which requirement each component addresses, which failure modes remain, and whether the operations team can manage it. Lesson 6 starts with this scenario and turns EC2 architecture and instance family selection into a sequence of evidence-based decisions rather than a list of features to memorize.
saa-06-ec2-architecture-instance-familie./opening/1: In SAA-C03, questions often present several technically viable options, but only one best matches keywords such as “least operational effort,” “no data loss,” “low cost,” “multiple accounts,” or “no use of the public internet.” EC2 architecture and instance family selection must be evaluated from four perspectives: instance family, vCPU and memory, network performance, and workload profile. Each perspective eliminates a group of answers that conflicts with the requirements or introduces unacceptable responsibilities.
saa-06-ec2-architecture-instance-familie./opening/2: The scope of Lesson 6—EC2 architecture and instance family selection—is aligned with objective SAA-C03 3.2 in the current AWS Exam Guide. The GitHub topic repository is used only to verify coverage and content organization; all explanations, cases, and diagrams here are newly written, while the technical conclusions are validated against AWS Documentation and the Well-Architected Framework. After this lesson, you should be able to explain not only “what to choose,” but also “why the other options were not chosen.”
saa-06-ec2-architecture-instance-familie./sections/0/title: Mental model: start with requirements, not services
saa-06-ec2-architecture-instance-familie./sections/0/paragraphs/0: Treat an AWS architecture as a contract among the business, application, and platform. The business states what must remain operational and what level of loss is acceptable; the application describes traffic, data, and dependencies; the platform translates those requirements into boundaries, services, policies, and observability mechanisms. For EC2 architecture and instance family selection, instance family is the entry point, vCPU and memory indicate how the system responds, and network performance and workload profile determine long-term operability. Moving directly from a requirement to a service name often causes one of these four layers to be overlooked.
saa-06-ec2-architecture-instance-familie./sections/0/paragraphs/1: A compact model that can be used for every lesson is R-C-D-E: Requirement, Constraint, Decision, Evidence. A requirement describes the outcome; a constraint limits the scope, such as Region, budget, or skills; a decision is the selected architectural structure; and evidence is a metric, log, test, or document confirming that the decision remains valid. By applying R-C-D-E to EC2 architecture and instance family selection, learners can see that the same service may be appropriate for one workload but unsuitable for another. This skill is more important than memorizing the entire AWS catalog.
saa-06-ec2-architecture-instance-familie./sections/0/paragraphs/2: You have understood Lesson 6 when you can trace a request or operational event from beginning to end. Which boundary does the request cross, which identity is evaluated, where is the data retained, which component can fail, and which mechanism provides recovery? If the narrative breaks at instance family or vCPU and memory, the diagram still contains an unverified assumption. Record that assumption next to the diagram before selecting another service.
saa-06-ec2-architecture-instance-familie./sections/1/title: Four concepts that must be connected
saa-06-ec2-architecture-instance-familie./sections/1/paragraphs/0: instance family is the first part of the EC2 architecture and instance family selection decision, but its value becomes clear only when considered alongside business requirements. For SAA-C03, learners should not stop at asking, “What is this service used for?” Identify the input that instance family receives, the state or policy it manages, the failure mode it addresses, and the resulting operational cost. At BlueRetail, the order service slows down during peak hours, and the team plans to increase the instance size without first identifying the bottleneck. Connecting instance family with vCPU and memory reveals where the responsibility boundary lies and why two similarly named options can produce different outcomes. A strong solution must trace the technical choice back to latency, availability, security, data, or the operations team's capabilities.
saa-06-ec2-architecture-instance-familie./sections/1/paragraphs/1: vCPU and memory is the second part of the EC2 architecture and instance family selection decision, but its value becomes clear only when considered alongside business requirements. For SAA-C03, learners should not stop at asking, “What is this service used for?” Identify the input that vCPU and memory receives, the state or policy it manages, the failure mode it addresses, and the resulting operational cost. saa-06-ec2-architecture-instance-familie./sections/2/title: Make decisions step by step and record the trade-offs
saa-06-ec2-architecture-instance-familie./sections/2/paragraphs/0: A decision involving instance family should be documented as a chain of reasoning, not as a standalone service name. First, confirm which requirements are mandatory, then check quota limits, Region, consistency, network path, and the responsibility model. Next, compare instance family with network performance using the same criteria; do not compare one feature of option A with the total cost of option B. Finally, state the remaining trade-off and how it will be observed after implementation. In Lesson 6, this approach helps BlueRetail understand why the selected option is good enough in the current context and which signals would trigger an architecture review.
saa-06-ec2-architecture-instance-familie./sections/2/paragraphs/1: A decision involving vCPU and memory should be documented as a chain of reasoning, not as a standalone service name. First, confirm which requirements are mandatory, then check quota limits, Region, consistency, network path, and the responsibility model. Next, compare vCPU and memory with workload profile using the same criteria; do not compare one feature of option A with the total cost of option B. Finally, state the remaining trade-off and how it will be observed after implementation. In Lesson 6, this approach helps BlueRetail understand why the selected option is good enough in the current context and which signals would trigger an architecture review.
saa-06-ec2-architecture-instance-familie./sections/2/paragraphs/2: A decision involving network performance should be documented as a chain of reasoning, not as a standalone service name. First, confirm which requirements are mandatory, then check quota limits, Region, consistency, network path, and the responsibility model. Next, compare network performance with instance family using the same criteria; do not compare one feature of option A with the total cost of option B. Finally, state the remaining trade-off and how it will be observed after implementation. In Lesson 6, this approach helps BlueRetail understand why the selected option is good enough in the current context and which signals would trigger an architecture review.
saa-06-ec2-architecture-instance-familie./sections/2/paragraphs/3: A decision involving workload profile should be documented as a chain of reasoning, not as a standalone service name. First, confirm which requirements are mandatory, then check quota limits, Region, consistency, network path, and the responsibility model. Next, compare workload profile with vCPU and memory using the same criteria; do not compare one feature of option A with the total cost of option B. Finally, state the remaining trade-off and how it will be observed after implementation. In Lesson 6, this approach helps BlueRetail understand why the selected option is good enough in the current context and which signals would trigger an architecture review.
saa-06-ec2-architecture-instance-familie./sections/3/title: Design for failure before it occurs
saa-06-ec2-architecture-instance-familie./sections/3/paragraphs/0: Failure mode 1 in EC2 architecture and instance family selection occurs when the project team assumes that instance family automatically addresses every related layer. In practice, service availability does not replace appropriate access permissions, replication does not replace backups, encryption does not automatically grant permission to use a key, and autoscaling cannot fix a stateful dependency. At BlueRetail, map the blast radius of instance family, identify the areas for which AWS is responsible, and identify what the application team must still configure or test. Then select a metric, log, or event that can provide an early warning. Analyzing failure modes first ensures that an answer is valid not only while the system is healthy but also provides a recovery path when an AZ, connection, credential, or operational process fails.
saa-06-ec2-architecture-instance-familie./sections/3/paragraphs/1: Failure mode 2 in EC2 architecture and instance family selection occurs when the project team assumes that vCPU and memory automatically addresses every related layer. In practice, service availability does not replace appropriate access permissions, replication does not replace backups, encryption does not automatically grant permission to use a key, and autoscaling cannot fix a stateful dependency. At BlueRetail, map the blast radius of vCPU and memory, identify the areas for which AWS is responsible, and identify what the application team must still configure or test. Then select a metric, log, or event that can provide an early warning. Analyzing failure modes first ensures that an answer is valid not only while the system is healthy but also provides a recovery path when an AZ, connection, credential, or operational process fails.
saa-06-ec2-architecture-instance-familie./sections/3/paragraphs/2: Failure mode 3 in EC2 architecture and instance family selection occurs when the project team assumes that network performance automatically addresses every related layer. In practice, service availability does not replace appropriate access permissions, replication does not replace backups, encryption does not automatically grant permission to use a key, and autoscaling cannot fix a stateful dependency. At BlueRetail, map the blast radius of network performance, identify the areas for which AWS is responsible, and identify what the application team must still configure or test. Then select a metric, log, or event that can provide an early warning. Analyzing failure modes first ensures that an answer is valid not only while the system is healthy but also provides a recovery path when an AZ, connection, credential, or operational process fails.
saa-06-ec2-architecture-instance-familie./sections/4/title: Apply the decision to the BlueRetail case
saa-06-ec2-architecture-instance-familie./sections/4/paragraphs/0: BlueRetail has stores, an e-commerce website, internal applications, and an operations team working across multiple accounts. In Lesson 6, the order service slows down during peak hours, and the team plans to increase the instance size without first identifying the bottleneck. The first step is to separate the stakeholders: users need a consistent experience, security needs boundaries and an audit trail, finance needs cost attribution, and the product team needs to make changes quickly. A design optimized for only one stakeholder creates operational debt for another.
saa-06-ec2-architecture-instance-familie./sections/4/paragraphs/1: The architecture team documents three options centered on instance family, vCPU and memory, and network performance. Each option is scored against the same matrix: satisfaction of mandatory requirements, blast radius, recovery path, operational burden, scalability, and cost per unit of use. workload profile serves as the final validation condition. If an option wins only because the team is familiar with it, the decision lacks sufficient evidence and the constraints must be revisited.
saa-06-ec2-architecture-instance-familie./sections/4/paragraphs/2: The conclusion for EC2 architecture and instance family selection is written in four sentences: which structure is selected; which requirements it satisfies; which trade-off is accepted; and which metric or review will trigger reassessment. This format helps developers, security, and operations understand the same decision. In exam questions, these four sentences also help eliminate distractors: an option that fails to meet a mandatory requirement is eliminated first, even if it offers many attractive features.
saa-06-ec2-architecture-instance-familie./sections/5/title: Choosing the right action in SAA-C03 questions
saa-06-ec2-architecture-instance-familie./sections/5/paragraphs/0: Read an SAA-C03 question in this order: the assigned role, current state, required outcome, constraints, and the verb at the end of the question. For EC2 architecture and instance family selection, mark every detail related to instance family and vCPU and memory. Then classify each answer as fully meeting the requirements, partially meeting them, introducing additional risk, or being out of scope. Do not choose an answer simply because it mentions the correct service; AWS tests your ability to combine multiple requirements into the most appropriate solution.
saa-06-ec2-architecture-instance-familie./sections/5/paragraphs/1: Absolute terms such as “no data loss,” “no downtime,” or “all traffic must remain private” require careful evaluation. They may demand a fundamentally different design from terms such as “minimize,” “acceptable,” or “prioritize cost.” In Lesson 6, use network performance to verify the required assurance level and workload profile to determine whether the operations team can sustain the option. An answer that provides the right feature but the wrong assurance level is still a distractor.
saa-06-ec2-architecture-instance-familie./sections/5/paragraphs/2: In Lesson 6—EC2 architecture and instance family selection—objective SAA-C03 3.2 indicates the required depth. For design, you must balance quality attributes; for determine, you must interpret the evidence and select a cause or option; for improve, you must preserve existing constraints while reducing risk through an appropriate change. End each answer with evidence: a metric, log, policy evaluation, recovery test, or cost report. Evidence turns an answer from a guess into a defensible architectural decision.

saa-06-ec2-architecture-instance-familie./activity/title: Architecture review: EC2 architecture and instance family selection
saa-06-ec2-architecture-instance-familie./activity/context: BlueRetail is preparing to approve a change because the order service slows down during peak hours, and the team plans to increase the instance size without first identifying the bottleneck. You must review the architecture before the team implements the change or incurs a cost commitment.
saa-06-ec2-architecture-instance-familie./activity/learnerRole: You are the Solutions Architect responsible for a workload. You must translate requirements into a decision and clearly explain the trade-offs to stakeholders.
saa-06-ec2-architecture-instance-familie./activity/currentArchitecture: Users → access layer → workload → data store; the review points are instance family → vCPU and memory → network performance → workload profile.
saa-06-ec2-architecture-instance-familie./activity/requirements/0: The solution must correctly address the requirement related to instance family.
saa-06-ec2-architecture-instance-familie./activity/requirements/1: The recovery path must show the role of vCPU and memory.
saa-06-ec2-architecture-instance-familie./activity/requirements/2: Security and operations must be able to observe network performance without relying on assumptions.
saa-06-ec2-architecture-instance-familie./activity/constraints/0: Do not add a service merely to conceal a requirement that has not been clearly defined.
saa-06-ec2-architecture-instance-familie./activity/constraints/1: The trade-off related to workload profile must be accepted by stakeholders.
saa-06-ec2-architecture-instance-familie./activity/constraints/2: Do not change the storage schema or account boundary without an architectural justification.
saa-06-ec2-architecture-instance-familie./activity/tasks/0: Rewrite the requirement as measurable criteria.
saa-06-ec2-architecture-instance-familie./activity/tasks/1: Compare at least two options based on instance family, vCPU and memory, and network performance.
saa-06-ec2-architecture-instance-familie./activity/tasks/2: Select an option, document the trade-off, and propose post-implementation evidence.
saa-06-ec2-architecture-instance-familie./activity/reasoningSteps/0: Separate the business outcome, technical constraints, and unverified assumptions.
saa-06-ec2-architecture-instance-familie./activity/reasoningSteps/1: Diagram the request and data flows, including trust boundaries and the blast radius.
saa-06-ec2-architecture-instance-familie./activity/reasoningSteps/2: Eliminate options that fail to meet mandatory requirements before optimizing.
saa-06-ec2-architecture-instance-familie./activity/reasoningSteps/3: Compare the remaining options across reliability, security, performance, cost, and operations.
saa-06-ec2-architecture-instance-familie./activity/reasoningSteps/4: Document the decision, trade-off, recovery path, and review metric.
saa-06-ec2-architecture-instance-familie./activity/deliverables/0: An architecture diagram showing boundaries and data flow directions.
saa-06-ec2-architecture-instance-familie./activity/deliverables/1: A decision matrix with at least two options.
saa-06-ec2-architecture-instance-familie./activity/deliverables/2: A brief architecture decision record containing trade-offs and evidence.
saa-06-ec2-architecture-instance-familie./activity/rubric/0/evidence: The decision can be traced back to instance family and does not introduce assumptions beyond the question.
saa-06-ec2-architecture-instance-familie./activity/rubric/0/importance: Prevents services from being selected based on preference or a single keyword.
saa-06-ec2-architecture-instance-familie./activity/rubric/1/evidence: Compares vCPU and memory, network performance, and workload profile using the same criteria.
saa-06-ec2-architecture-instance-familie./activity/rubric/1/importance: AWS architecture always involves balancing priorities; there is no universally best option.
saa-06-ec2-architecture-instance-familie./activity/rubric/2/evidence: The submission identifies a failure mode, recovery path, and validating metric or test.
saa-06-ec2-architecture-instance-familie./activity/rubric/2/importance: A design is trustworthy only when it can be operated and validated after implementation.
saa-06-ec2-architecture-instance-familie./activity/sampleAnalysis/0: The primary requirement is rewritten around instance family; vCPU and memory is a constraint that directly affects the selection.
saa-06-ec2-architecture-instance-familie./activity/sampleAnalysis/1: The option prioritizing network performance is eliminated if it increases the operational burden of workload profile without adding value.

saa-06-ec2-architecture-instance-familie./sources/2/label: GitHub topic inventory used to verify coverage—Lesson 6

saa-07-ec2-purchase-options./title: On-Demand, Savings Plans, Reserved, and Spot
saa-07-ec2-purchase-options./shortTitle: On-Demand, Savings Plans, Reserved, and Spot
saa-07-ec2-purchase-options./summary: Understand On-Demand, Savings Plans, Reserved, and Spot through requirements, constraints, trade-offs, and an architectural decision that can be explained to the BlueRetail team.
saa-07-ec2-purchase-options./opening/0: BlueRetail is changing its architecture, and finance wants to reduce compute costs, but its workloads include a stable API, flexible batch processing, and test environments. A diagram with many AWS icons is not necessarily a good design. What matters is which requirement each component addresses, which failure modes remain, and whether the operations team can manage it. Lesson 7 begins with this scenario and turns On-Demand, Savings Plans, Reserved, and Spot into a sequence of evidence-based decisions rather than a list of features to memorize.
saa-07-ec2-purchase-options./opening/1: In SAA-C03, questions often present several technically viable options, but only one best matches keywords such as “least operational effort,” “no data loss,” “low cost,” “multiple accounts,” or “no public internet.” On-Demand, Savings Plans, Reserved, and Spot must be evaluated from four perspectives: On-Demand, Savings Plans, Reserved Instance, and Spot interruption. Each perspective eliminates a group of answers that conflict with the requirements or introduce unacceptable responsibilities.
saa-07-ec2-purchase-options./opening/2: The scope of Lesson 7—On-Demand, Savings Plans, Reserved, and Spot—is aligned with objective SAA-C03 4.2 in the current AWS Exam Guide. The GitHub topic inventory is used only to check coverage and content organization. All explanations, cases, and diagrams here are original, while the technical conclusions are validated against AWS Documentation and the Well-Architected Framework. After this lesson, you should be able to explain not only what to choose, but also why the other options should not be chosen.
saa-07-ec2-purchase-options./sections/0/title: Thinking model: start with requirements, not services
saa-07-ec2-purchase-options./sections/0/paragraphs/0: Treat an AWS architecture as a contract among the business, application, and platform. The business states what must remain operational and what level of loss is acceptable; the application defines traffic, data, and dependencies; and the platform translates those requirements into boundaries, services, policies, and observability mechanisms. For On-Demand, Savings Plans, Reserved, and Spot, On-Demand is the entry point, Savings Plans describe how the system responds, and Reserved Instance and Spot interruption determine long-term operability. Moving directly from a requirement to a service name often causes one of these four layers to be overlooked.
saa-07-ec2-purchase-options./sections/0/paragraphs/1: A compact model that works for every lesson is R-C-D-E: Requirement, Constraint, Decision, Evidence. A requirement describes the outcome; a constraint limits the scope through factors such as Region, budget, or skills; a decision is the selected architectural structure; and evidence is a metric, log, test, or document confirming that the decision remains valid. By applying R-C-D-E to On-Demand, Savings Plans, Reserved, and Spot, learners can see that the same service may be right for one workload but wrong for another. This skill is more important than memorizing the entire AWS catalog.
saa-07-ec2-purchase-options./sections/0/paragraphs/2: You understand Lesson 7 when you can trace a request or operational event from beginning to end. Which boundary does the request cross, which identity is evaluated, where is the data retained, which component can fail, and what mechanism provides recovery? If the story breaks at On-Demand or Savings Plans, the diagram still contains an unverified assumption. Record that assumption beside the diagram before selecting another service.
saa-07-ec2-purchase-options./sections/1/title: Four concepts that must be connected
saa-07-ec2-purchase-options./sections/1/paragraphs/0: On-Demand is the 1st component in the On-Demand, Savings Plans, Reserved, and Spot decision, but its value becomes clear only when considered alongside business requirements. For SAA-C03, learners should not stop at asking, “What is this service used for?” Identify the input On-Demand receives, the state or policy it manages, the failure mode it addresses, and the operational costs it creates. At BlueRetail, finance wants to reduce compute costs, but the workloads include a stable API, flexible batch processing, and test environments. Connecting On-Demand to Savings Plans clarifies where responsibility boundaries lie and why two similarly named options can produce different outcomes. A good solution must trace the technical choice back to latency, availability, security, data, or the operations team's capabilities.
saa-07-ec2-purchase-options./sections/1/paragraphs/1: Savings Plans is the 2nd component in the On-Demand, Savings Plans, Reserved, and Spot decision, but its value becomes clear only when considered alongside business requirements. For SAA-C03, learners should not stop at asking, “What is this service used for?” Identify the input Savings Plans receives, the state or policy it manages, the failure mode it addresses, and the operational costs it creates. At BlueRetail, finance wants to reduce compute costs, but the workloads include a stable API, flexible batch processing, and test environments. Connecting Savings Plans to Reserved Instance clarifies where responsibility boundaries lie and why two similarly named options can produce different outcomes. A good solution must trace the technical choice back to latency, availability, security, data, or the operations team's capabilities.
saa-07-ec2-purchase-options./sections/1/paragraphs/2: Reserved Instance is the 3rd component in the On-Demand, Savings Plans, Reserved, and Spot decision, but its value becomes clear only when considered alongside business requirements. For SAA-C03, learners should not stop at asking, “What is this service used for?” Identify the input Reserved Instance receives, the state or policy it manages, the failure mode it addresses, and the operational costs it creates. At BlueRetail, finance wants to reduce compute costs, but the workloads include a stable API, flexible batch processing, and test environments. Connecting Reserved Instance to Spot interruption clarifies where responsibility boundaries lie and why two similarly named options can produce different outcomes. A good solution must trace the technical choice back to latency, availability, security, data, or the operations team's capabilities.
saa-07-ec2-purchase-options./sections/1/paragraphs/3: Spot interruption is the 4th component in the On-Demand, Savings Plans, Reserved, and Spot decision, but its value becomes clear only when considered alongside business requirements. For SAA-C03, learners should not stop at asking, “What is this service used for?” Identify the input Spot interruption receives, the state or policy it manages, the failure mode it addresses, and the operational costs it creates. At BlueRetail, finance wants to reduce compute costs, but the workloads include a stable API, flexible batch processing, and test environments. Connecting Spot interruption to On-Demand clarifies where responsibility boundaries lie and why two similarly named options can produce different outcomes. A good solution must trace the technical choice back to latency, availability, security, data, or the operations team's capabilities.
saa-07-ec2-purchase-options./sections/2/title: Make decisions step by step and document the trade-offs
saa-07-ec2-purchase-options./sections/2/paragraphs/0: A decision involving On-Demand should be documented as a chain of reasoning, not as a standalone service name. First, confirm which requirements are mandatory, then check quota limits, Region, consistency, network path, and the responsibility model. Next, compare On-Demand with Reserved Instance using the same criteria; do not compare one feature of option A with the total cost of option B. Finally, state the remaining trade-off and how it will be observed after implementation. In Lesson 7, this approach helps BlueRetail understand why the selected option is good enough for the current context and which signals would trigger an architecture review.
saa-07-ec2-purchase-options./sections/2/paragraphs/1: A decision involving Savings Plans should be documented as a chain of reasoning, not as a standalone service name. First, confirm which requirements are mandatory, then check quota limits, Region, consistency, network path, and the responsibility model. Next, compare Savings Plans with Spot interruption using the same criteria; do not compare one feature of option A with the total cost of option B. Finally, state the remaining trade-off and how it will be observed after implementation. In Lesson 7, this approach helps BlueRetail understand why the selected option is good enough for the current context and which signals would trigger an architecture review.
saa-07-ec2-purchase-options./sections/2/paragraphs/2: A decision involving Reserved Instance should be documented as a chain of reasoning, not as a standalone service name. First, confirm which requirements are mandatory, then check quota limits, Region, consistency, network path, and the responsibility model. Next, compare Reserved Instance with On-Demand using the same criteria; do not compare one feature of option A with the total cost of option B. Finally, state the remaining trade-off and how it will be observed after implementation. In Lesson 7, this approach helps BlueRetail understand why the selected option is good enough for the current context and which signals would trigger an architecture review.
saa-07-ec2-purchase-options./sections/2/paragraphs/3: A decision involving Spot interruption should be documented as a chain of reasoning, not as a standalone service name. First, confirm which requirements are mandatory, then check quota limits, Region, consistency, network path, and the responsibility model. Next, compare Spot interruption with Savings Plans using the same criteria; do not compare one feature of option A with the total cost of option B. Finally, state the remaining trade-off and how it will be observed after implementation. In Lesson 7, this approach helps BlueRetail understand why the selected option is good enough for the current context and which signals would trigger an architecture review.
saa-07-ec2-purchase-options./sections/3/title: Design for failure before it occurs
saa-07-ec2-purchase-options./sections/3/paragraphs/0: Failure mode 1 for On-Demand, Savings Plans, Reserved, and Spot occurs when the project team assumes that On-Demand automatically addresses every related layer. In reality, service availability does not replace correct access permissions, replication does not replace backups, encryption does not automatically grant permission to use a key, and autoscaling cannot fix a stateful dependency. For BlueRetail, diagram the blast radius of On-Demand, marking the parts for which AWS is responsible and those the application team must still configure or test. Then select a metric, log, or event that can provide an early warning. Analyzing failure modes first ensures that the answer is valid not only while the system is healthy, but also provides a recovery path when an AZ, connection, credential, or operational process fails.
saa-07-ec2-purchase-options./sections/3/paragraphs/1: Failure mode 2 for On-Demand, Savings Plans, Reserved, and Spot occurs when the project team assumes that Savings Plans automatically addresses every related layer. In reality, service availability does not replace correct access permissions, replication does not replace backups, encryption does not automatically grant permission to use a key, and autoscaling cannot fix a stateful dependency. For BlueRetail, diagram the blast radius of Savings Plans, marking the parts for which AWS is responsible and those the application team must still configure or test. Then select a metric, log, or event that can provide an early warning. Analyzing failure modes first ensures that the answer is valid not only while the system is healthy, but also provides a recovery path when an AZ, connection, credential, or operational process fails.
saa-07-ec2-purchase-options./sections/3/paragraphs/2: Failure mode 3 for On-Demand, Savings Plans, Reserved, and Spot occurs when the project team assumes that Reserved Instance automatically addresses every related layer. In reality, service availability does not replace correct access permissions, replication does not replace backups, encryption does not automatically grant permission to use a key, and autoscaling cannot fix a stateful dependency. For BlueRetail, diagram the blast radius of Reserved Instance, marking the parts for which AWS is responsible and those the application team must still configure or test. Then select a metric, log, or event that can provide an early warning. Analyzing failure modes first ensures that the answer is valid not only while the system is healthy, but also provides a recovery path when an AZ, connection, credential, or operational process fails.
saa-07-ec2-purchase-options./sections/4/title: Apply the decision to the BlueRetail case
saa-07-ec2-purchase-options./sections/4/paragraphs/0: BlueRetail has stores, an e-commerce website, internal applications, and an operations team working across multiple accounts. In Lesson 7, finance wants to reduce compute costs, but the workloads include a stable API, flexible batch processing, and test environments. The first step is to separate the stakeholders: users need a stable experience, security needs boundaries and an audit trail, finance needs cost attribution, and the product team needs to make changes quickly. A design optimized for only one stakeholder will create operational debt for another.
saa-07-ec2-purchase-options./sections/4/paragraphs/1: The architecture team documents three options involving On-Demand, Savings Plans, and Reserved Instance. Each option is scored using the same table: mandatory requirement coverage, blast radius, recovery path, operational burden, scalability, and cost per unit of usage. Spot interruption serves as the final validation condition. If an option wins only because the team is familiar with it, the decision lacks sufficient evidence and the constraints must be revisited.
saa-07-ec2-purchase-options./sections/4/paragraphs/2: The conclusion for On-Demand, Savings Plans, Reserved, and Spot is written in four sentences: which structure is selected; which requirements it satisfies; which trade-off is accepted; and which metric or review will trigger reevaluation. This format helps developers, security, and operations understand the same decision. In exam questions, these four sentences also help eliminate distractors: an option that fails a mandatory requirement is eliminated first, even if it offers many attractive features.
saa-07-ec2-purchase-options./sections/5/title: Choosing the right action in SAA-C03 questions
saa-07-ec2-purchase-options./sections/5/paragraphs/0: Read an SAA-C03 question in this order: the assigned role, the current state, the required outcome, the constraints, and the verb in the final sentence. For On-Demand, Savings Plans, Reserved, and Spot, mark every detail related to On-Demand and Savings Plans. Then classify each answer as fully meeting the requirements, partially meeting them, introducing additional risk, or being out of scope. Do not select an answer merely because it mentions the correct service; AWS tests your ability to combine multiple requirements into the most appropriate solution.
saa-07-ec2-purchase-options./sections/5/paragraphs/1: Absolute phrases such as “no data loss,” “no downtime,” or “all traffic must remain private” require careful evaluation. They may demand a very different design from phrases such as “minimize,” “acceptable,” or “prioritize cost.” In Lesson 7, use Reserved Instance to assess the level of assurance and Spot interruption to determine whether the operations team can sustain the option. An answer that identifies the right feature but provides the wrong level of assurance is still a distractor.
saa-07-ec2-purchase-options./sections/5/paragraphs/2: In Lesson 7—On-Demand, Savings Plans, Reserved, and Spot—objective SAA-C03 4.2 indicates the required depth. For design, you must balance quality attributes; for determine, you must interpret the facts and select the cause or option; for improve, you must preserve existing constraints while reducing risk through an appropriate change. End each answer with evidence: a metric, log, policy evaluation, recovery test, or cost report. Evidence turns an answer from a guess into a defensible architectural decision.

saa-07-ec2-purchase-options./sources/2/label: GitHub topic inventory used to check coverage—Lesson 7

saa-08-ami-bootstrap-metadata./title: Secure AMI, bootstrap, and instance metadata
saa-08-ami-bootstrap-metadata./shortTitle: Secure AMI, bootstrap, and instance metadata
saa-08-ami-bootstrap-metadata./summary: Understand secure AMI, bootstrap, and instance metadata through requirements, constraints, trade-offs, and an architectural decision that can be explained to the BlueRetail team.
saa-08-ami-bootstrap-metadata./opening/0: BlueRetail is changing its architecture, and auto scaling is creating new instances with inconsistent configurations while the bootstrap script contains sensitive information. A diagram with many AWS icons is not necessarily a good design: what matters is which requirement each component addresses, which failure modes remain, and whether the operations team can manage it effectively. Lesson 8 begins with this scenario and turns secure AMI, bootstrap, and instance metadata into a sequence of evidence-based decisions rather than a list of features to memorize.
saa-08-ami-bootstrap-metadata./opening/1: In SAA-C03, questions often present several technically viable options, but only one best matches keywords such as “least operational effort,” “no data loss,” “low cost,” “multiple accounts,” or “no public internet.” Secure AMI, bootstrap, and instance metadata should be examined from four perspectives: Amazon Machine Image, user data, instance metadata service, and immutable server. Each perspective eliminates a group of answers that conflict with the requirements or introduce unacceptable responsibilities.
saa-08-ami-bootstrap-metadata./opening/2: The scope of Lesson 8—secure AMI, bootstrap, and instance metadata—is aligned with objective SAA-C03 1.2 in the current AWS Exam Guide. The GitHub topic repository is used only to verify coverage and content organization; all explanations, cases, and diagrams here are newly written, while the technical conclusions are cross-checked against AWS Documentation and the Well-Architected Framework. After this lesson, you should be able to explain not only what to choose, but also why the other options should not be chosen.
saa-08-ami-bootstrap-metadata./sections/0/title: Mental model: start with requirements, not services
saa-08-ami-bootstrap-metadata./sections/0/paragraphs/0: Treat an AWS architecture as a contract among business, application, and platform. The business defines what must remain operational and what level of loss is acceptable; the application defines traffic, data, and dependencies; and the platform translates those requirements into boundaries, services, policies, and observability mechanisms. For secure AMI, bootstrap, and instance metadata, the Amazon Machine Image is the entry point, user data defines how the system responds, and the instance metadata service and immutable server determine long-term operability. Moving directly from requirements to service names often causes one of these four layers to be overlooked.
saa-08-ami-bootstrap-metadata./sections/0/paragraphs/1: A compact model that works for every lesson is R-C-D-E: Requirement, Constraint, Decision, Evidence. A requirement describes the desired outcome; a constraint limits the scope through factors such as Region, budget, or skills; a decision is the selected architectural structure; and evidence is a metric, log, test, or document confirming that the decision remains valid. By applying R-C-D-E to secure AMI, bootstrap, and instance metadata, learners can see that the same service may be appropriate for one workload but unsuitable for another. This skill is more important than memorizing the entire AWS catalog.
saa-08-ami-bootstrap-metadata./sections/0/paragraphs/2: You have understood Lesson 8 when you can trace a request or operational event from beginning to end. Which boundary does the request cross, which identity is evaluated, where is the data retained, which component can fail, and which mechanism restores service? If the story stops at the Amazon Machine Image or user data, the diagram still contains an unverified assumption. Record that assumption next to the diagram before selecting another service.
saa-08-ami-bootstrap-metadata./sections/1/title: Four concepts that must be connected
saa-08-ami-bootstrap-metadata./sections/1/paragraphs/0: Amazon Machine Image is the first component in a decision about secure AMI, bootstrap, and instance metadata, but its value becomes clear only when considered alongside business requirements. For SAA-C03, learners should not stop at asking what the service does. Identify the input that Amazon Machine Image receives, the state or policy it manages, the failure modes it addresses, and the resulting operational cost. At BlueRetail, auto scaling is creating new instances with inconsistent configurations while the bootstrap script contains sensitive information. Connecting Amazon Machine Image with user data reveals where the responsibility boundary lies and why two similarly named options can produce different outcomes. A good solution must trace the technical choice back to latency, availability, security, data, or the operations team's capabilities.
saa-08-ami-bootstrap-metadata./sections/1/paragraphs/1: user data is the second component in a decision about secure AMI, bootstrap, and instance metadata, but its value becomes clear only when considered alongside business requirements. For SAA-C03, learners should not stop at asking what the service does. Identify the input that user data receives, the state or policy it manages, the failure modes it addresses, and the resulting operational cost. At BlueRetail, auto scaling is creating new instances with inconsistent configurations while the bootstrap script contains sensitive information. Connecting user data with the instance metadata service reveals where the responsibility boundary lies and why two similarly named options can produce different outcomes. A good solution must trace the technical choice back to latency, availability, security, data, or the operations team's capabilities.

saa-08-ami-bootstrap-metadata./sections/2/title: Make decisions step by step and document the trade-offs
saa-08-ami-bootstrap-metadata./sections/2/paragraphs/0: A decision involving an Amazon Machine Image should be documented as a chain of reasoning, not as a standalone service name. First, confirm which requirements are mandatory, then check quota limits, Region, consistency, network path, and the responsibility model. Next, compare the Amazon Machine Image with the instance metadata service using the same criteria; do not compare one feature of option A with the total cost of option B. Finally, state the remaining trade-off and how it will be observed after implementation. In Lesson 8, this approach helps BlueRetail understand why the selected option is good enough in the current context and identify the signals that would trigger an architecture review.
saa-08-ami-bootstrap-metadata./sections/2/paragraphs/1: A decision involving user data should be documented as a chain of reasoning, not as a standalone service name. First, confirm which requirements are mandatory, then check quota limits, Region, consistency, network path, and the responsibility model. Next, compare user data with the immutable server using the same criteria; do not compare one feature of option A with the total cost of option B. Finally, state the remaining trade-off and how it will be observed after implementation. In Lesson 8, this approach helps BlueRetail understand why the selected option is good enough in the current context and identify the signals that would trigger an architecture review.

Next, compare the instance metadata service with the Amazon Machine Image using the same criteria; do not compare one feature of option A with the total cost of option B. Finally, state the remaining trade-off and how it will be observed after implementation. First, confirm which requirements are mandatory, then check quota limits, Region, consistency, network path, and the responsibility model. Next, compare the immutable server with user data using the same criteria; do not compare one feature of option A with the total cost of option B. Finally, state the remaining trade-off and how it will be observed after implementation. saa-08-ami-bootstrap-metadata./sections/3/title: Design for failure before it occurs
saa-08-ami-bootstrap-metadata./sections/3/paragraphs/0: Failure mode 1 for secure AMI, bootstrap, and instance metadata occurs when the project team assumes that the Amazon Machine Image automatically addresses every related layer. In practice, service availability does not replace correct access permissions, replication does not replace backup, encryption does not automatically grant permission to use a key, and autoscaling does not fix a stateful dependency. At BlueRetail, map the blast radius of the Amazon Machine Image and identify what AWS is responsible for and what the application team must still configure or test. Then select a metric, log, or event that can provide an early warning. Analyzing failure modes first ensures that the answer is valid not only when the system is healthy, but also provides a recovery path when an AZ, connection, credential, or operational process fails.
saa-08-ami-bootstrap-metadata./sections/3/paragraphs/1: Failure mode 2 for secure AMI, bootstrap, and instance metadata occurs when the project team assumes that user data automatically addresses every related layer. In practice, service availability does not replace correct access permissions, replication does not replace backup, encryption does not automatically grant permission to use a key, and autoscaling does not fix a stateful dependency. At BlueRetail, map the blast radius of user data and identify what AWS is responsible for and what the application team must still configure or test. Then select a metric, log, or event that can provide an early warning. Analyzing failure modes first ensures that the answer is valid not only when the system is healthy, but also provides a recovery path when an AZ, connection, credential, or operational process fails.
saa-08-ami-bootstrap-metadata./sections/3/paragraphs/2: Failure mode 3 for secure AMI, bootstrap, and instance metadata occurs when the project team assumes that the instance metadata service automatically addresses every related layer. In practice, service availability does not replace correct access permissions, replication does not replace backup, encryption does not automatically grant permission to use a key, and autoscaling does not fix a stateful dependency. At BlueRetail, map the blast radius of the instance metadata service and identify what AWS is responsible for and what the application team must still configure or test. Then select a metric, log, or event that can provide an early warning. Analyzing failure modes first ensures that the answer is valid not only when the system is healthy, but also provides a recovery path when an AZ, connection, credential, or operational process fails.
saa-08-ami-bootstrap-metadata./sections/4/title: Apply the decision to the BlueRetail case
saa-08-ami-bootstrap-metadata./sections/4/paragraphs/0: BlueRetail has stores, an e-commerce website, internal applications, and an operations team working across multiple accounts. In Lesson 8, Auto Scaling creates new instances with inconsistent configurations while the bootstrap script contains sensitive information. The first step is to separate the stakeholders: users need a consistent experience, security needs boundaries and an audit trail, finance needs cost attribution, and the product team needs to make changes quickly. A design optimized for only one stakeholder creates operational debt for another.
saa-08-ami-bootstrap-metadata./sections/4/paragraphs/1: The architecture team documents three options centered on Amazon Machine Image, user data, and the instance metadata service. Each option is evaluated using the same scorecard: fulfillment of mandatory requirements, blast radius, recovery path, operational burden, scalability, and cost per unit of usage. The immutable server serves as the final validation criterion. If an option wins only because the team is familiar with it, the decision lacks sufficient evidence and the constraints must be revisited.
saa-08-ami-bootstrap-metadata./sections/4/paragraphs/2: The conclusion for secure AMI, bootstrap, and instance metadata is written in four sentences: which structure is selected; which requirements it satisfies; which trade-off is accepted; and which metric or review will trigger reevaluation. This format helps developers, security, and operations understand the same decision. In exam questions, the four sentences also help eliminate distractors: an option that fails a mandatory requirement is eliminated first, even if it offers many attractive features.
saa-08-ami-bootstrap-metadata./sections/5/paragraphs/0: Read an SAA-C03 question in this order: the assigned role, the current state, the required outcome, the constraints, and the final verb in the question. For secure AMI, bootstrap, and instance metadata, mark every detail related to the Amazon Machine Image and user data. Then classify each answer as fully satisfying the requirements, partially satisfying them, introducing additional risk, or being out of scope. Do not choose an answer merely because it names the correct service; AWS tests your ability to combine multiple requirements into the most appropriate solution.
saa-08-ami-bootstrap-metadata./sections/5/paragraphs/1: Absolute phrases such as “no data loss,” “no downtime,” or “all traffic must remain private” require careful examination. They may require a completely different design from phrases such as “minimize,” “acceptable,” or “prioritize cost.” In Lesson 8, use the instance metadata service to evaluate the assurance level and the immutable server to determine whether the operations team can sustain the option. An answer that has the right feature but the wrong assurance level is still a distractor.
saa-08-ami-bootstrap-metadata./sections/5/paragraphs/2: In Lesson 8—secure AMI, bootstrap, and instance metadata—objective SAA-C03 1.2 indicates the required depth. For design, you must balance quality attributes; for determine, you must interpret the facts and select a cause or option; for improve, you must preserve existing constraints and reduce risk through an appropriate change. End each answer with evidence: a metric, log, policy evaluation, recovery test, or cost report. Evidence turns an answer from a guess into a defensible architectural decision.

saa-08-ami-bootstrap-metadata./activity/title: Architecture review: secure AMI, bootstrap, and instance metadata
saa-08-ami-bootstrap-metadata./activity/context: BlueRetail is preparing to approve a change because auto scaling creates new instances with inconsistent configurations while the bootstrap script contains sensitive information. You must review the architecture before the team implements it or incurs a cost commitment.
saa-08-ami-bootstrap-metadata./activity/learnerRole: You are the Solutions Architect responsible for a workload, translating requirements into decisions and clearly communicating trade-offs to stakeholders.
saa-08-ami-bootstrap-metadata./activity/currentArchitecture: Users → access layer → workload → data store; the review points are Amazon Machine Image → user data → instance metadata service → immutable server.
saa-08-ami-bootstrap-metadata./activity/requirements/0: The solution must correctly address the requirement related to the Amazon Machine Image.
saa-08-ami-bootstrap-metadata./activity/requirements/1: The recovery path must demonstrate the role of user data.
saa-08-ami-bootstrap-metadata./activity/requirements/2: Security and operations must be able to observe the instance metadata service without relying on assumptions.
saa-08-ami-bootstrap-metadata./activity/constraints/0: Do not add a service merely to conceal a requirement that has not been clearly defined.
saa-08-ami-bootstrap-metadata./activity/constraints/1: The trade-off concerning the immutable server must be accepted by the stakeholders.
saa-08-ami-bootstrap-metadata./activity/constraints/2: Do not change the storage schema or account boundary without an architectural justification.
saa-08-ami-bootstrap-metadata./activity/tasks/0: Rewrite the requirement as measurable criteria.
saa-08-ami-bootstrap-metadata./activity/tasks/1: Compare at least two options based on Amazon Machine Image, user data, and the instance metadata service.
saa-08-ami-bootstrap-metadata./activity/tasks/2: Select an option, document the trade-off, and propose post-implementation evidence.
saa-08-ami-bootstrap-metadata./activity/reasoningSteps/0: Separate the business outcome, technical constraints, and unverified assumptions.
saa-08-ami-bootstrap-metadata./activity/reasoningSteps/1: Draw the request and data flow, including the trust boundary and blast radius.
saa-08-ami-bootstrap-metadata./activity/reasoningSteps/2: Eliminate options that fail mandatory requirements before optimizing.
saa-08-ami-bootstrap-metadata./activity/reasoningSteps/3: Compare the remaining options across reliability, security, performance, cost, and operations.
saa-08-ami-bootstrap-metadata./activity/reasoningSteps/4: Document the decision, trade-off, recovery path, and review metric.
saa-08-ami-bootstrap-metadata./activity/deliverables/0: An architecture diagram showing boundaries and data flow direction.
saa-08-ami-bootstrap-metadata./activity/deliverables/1: A decision matrix with at least two options.
saa-08-ami-bootstrap-metadata./activity/deliverables/2: A concise architecture decision record containing trade-offs and evidence.
saa-08-ami-bootstrap-metadata./activity/rubric/0/evidence: The decision can be traced back to the Amazon Machine Image and introduces no assumptions beyond the question.
saa-08-ami-bootstrap-metadata./activity/rubric/0/importance: Prevents selecting a service based on preference or an isolated keyword.
saa-08-ami-bootstrap-metadata./activity/rubric/1/evidence: Compares user data, the instance metadata service, and the immutable server using the same criteria.
saa-08-ami-bootstrap-metadata./activity/rubric/1/importance: AWS architecture always involves balance; there is no universally best option.
saa-08-ami-bootstrap-metadata./activity/rubric/2/evidence: The submission identifies a failure mode, recovery path, and validating metric or test.
saa-08-ami-bootstrap-metadata./activity/rubric/2/importance: A design is trustworthy only when it can be operated and validated after implementation.
saa-08-ami-bootstrap-metadata./activity/sampleAnalysis/0: The primary requirement is rewritten around the Amazon Machine Image; user data is a constraint that directly affects the choice.
saa-08-ami-bootstrap-metadata./activity/sampleAnalysis/1: An option prioritizing the instance metadata service is eliminated if it increases the operational burden of the immutable server without adding value.
saa-08-ami-bootstrap-metadata./activity/sampleAnalysis/2: The proposed architecture combines Amazon Machine Image and user data, with the blast radius, recovery path, and review metric clearly documented.
saa-08-ami-bootstrap-metadata./activity/sampleAnalysis/3: The remaining trade-off is accepted because it fits the scenario in which auto scaling creates new instances with inconsistent configurations while the bootstrap script contains sensitive information.
saa-08-ami-bootstrap-metadata./activity/commonMistakes/0: Listing services without connecting them to the requirement.
saa-08-ami-bootstrap-metadata./activity/commonMistakes/1: Describing only the happy path and ignoring failure modes.
saa-08-ami-bootstrap-metadata./activity/commonMistakes/2: Saying “AWS manages it” without identifying the customer's responsibilities.

saa-09-ebs-instance-store./title: EBS, instance store, and block storage performance
saa-09-ebs-instance-store./shortTitle: EBS, instance store, and block storage performance
saa-09-ebs-instance-store./summary: Understand EBS, instance store, and block storage performance through requirements, constraints, trade-offs, and an architectural decision that can be explained to the BlueRetail team.
saa-09-ebs-instance-store./opening/0: BlueRetail is changing its architecture, and its image-processing cluster needs a very fast scratch disk, but the output data must persist after an instance is replaced. A diagram filled with AWS icons is not necessarily a good design: what matters is which requirement each component addresses, which failure modes remain, and whether the operations team can manage it. Lesson 9 begins with this scenario and turns EBS, instance store, and block storage performance into a sequence of evidence-based decisions rather than a list of features to memorize.
saa-09-ebs-instance-store./opening/1: In SAA-C03, questions often present several technically viable options, but only one best matches keywords such as “least operational effort,” “no data loss,” “low cost,” “multiple accounts,” or “no public internet.” EBS, instance store, and block storage performance should be evaluated from four perspectives: EBS volume, instance store, IOPS, and throughput. Each perspective eliminates a group of answers that conflict with the requirements or introduce unacceptable responsibilities.
saa-09-ebs-instance-store./opening/2: The scope of Lesson 9—EBS, instance store, and block storage performance—is aligned with objective SAA-C03 3.1 in the current AWS Exam Guide. The GitHub topic repository is used only to verify coverage and content structure; all explanations, cases, and diagrams here are original, while the technical conclusions are validated against AWS Documentation and the Well-Architected Framework. After this lesson, you should be able to explain not only “what to choose,” but also “why the other options should not be chosen.”
saa-09-ebs-instance-store./sections/0/title: Mental model: start with requirements, not services
saa-09-ebs-instance-store./sections/0/paragraphs/0: Think of an AWS architecture as a contract among business, application, and platform teams. The business defines what must remain operational and what level of loss is acceptable; the application defines traffic, data, and dependencies; the platform translates those requirements into boundaries, services, policies, and observability mechanisms. For EBS, instance store, and block storage performance, the EBS volume is the entry point, instance store indicates how the system responds, and IOPS and throughput determine long-term operational viability. Going directly from a requirement to a service name often causes one of these four layers to be overlooked.
saa-09-ebs-instance-store./sections/0/paragraphs/1: A compact model that works for every lesson is R-C-D-E: Requirement, Constraint, Decision, Evidence. A requirement describes the outcome; a constraint limits the scope through factors such as Region, budget, or skills; a decision is the selected architectural structure; and evidence is the metric, log, test, or document confirming that the decision remains valid. By applying R-C-D-E to EBS, instance store, and block storage performance, learners can see that the same service may be right for one workload but wrong for another. This skill is more important than memorizing the entire AWS catalog.
saa-09-ebs-instance-store./sections/0/paragraphs/2: You have understood Lesson 9 when you can trace a request or operational event from beginning to end. Which boundary does the request cross, which identity is evaluated, where is the data retained, which component can fail, and which mechanism performs recovery? If the story breaks at the EBS volume or instance store, the diagram still contains an unverified assumption. Record that assumption next to the diagram before selecting another service.
saa-09-ebs-instance-store./sections/1/title: Four concepts that must be connected
saa-09-ebs-instance-store./sections/1/paragraphs/0: EBS volume is the first element in decisions about EBS, instance store, and block storage performance, but its value becomes clear only when considered alongside business requirements. For SAA-C03, learners should not stop at asking, “What is this service used for?” Identify the input received by the EBS volume, the state or policy it manages, the failure mode it addresses, and the operational cost it introduces. At BlueRetail, the image-processing cluster needs a very fast scratch disk, but the output data must persist after an instance is replaced. Connecting EBS volume with instance store reveals where the responsibility boundary lies and why two similarly named options can produce different outcomes. A good solution must trace each technical choice back to latency, availability, security, data, or the operations team's capabilities.
saa-09-ebs-instance-store./sections/1/paragraphs/1: instance store is the second element in decisions about EBS, instance store, and block storage performance, but its value becomes clear only when considered alongside business requirements. Connecting instance store with IOPS reveals where the responsibility boundary lies and why two similarly named options can produce different outcomes. A good solution must trace each technical choice back to latency, availability, security, data, or the operations team's capabilities.

saa-09-ebs-instance-store./sections/2/title: Make decisions step by step and document the trade-offs
saa-09-ebs-instance-store./sections/2/paragraphs/0: A decision involving an EBS volume should be documented as a chain of reasoning, not as a standalone service name. First, confirm which requirements are mandatory, then check quota limits, Region, consistency, network path, and the responsibility model. Next, compare the EBS volume with IOPS using the same criteria; do not compare one feature of option A with the total cost of option B. Finally, state the remaining trade-off and how it will be observed after implementation. In Lesson 9, this approach helps BlueRetail understand why the selected option is good enough in the current context and which signals would trigger an architectural review.
saa-09-ebs-instance-store./sections/2/paragraphs/1: A decision involving instance store should be documented as a chain of reasoning, not as a standalone service name. First, confirm which requirements are mandatory, then check quota limits, Region, consistency, network path, and the responsibility model. Next, compare instance store with throughput using the same criteria; do not compare one feature of option A with the total cost of option B. Finally, state the remaining trade-off and how it will be observed after implementation. In Lesson 9, this approach helps BlueRetail understand why the selected option is good enough in the current context and which signals would trigger an architectural review.
saa-09-ebs-instance-store./sections/2/paragraphs/2: A decision involving IOPS should be documented as a chain of reasoning, not as a standalone service name. First, confirm which requirements are mandatory, then check quota limits, Region, consistency, network path, and the responsibility model. Next, compare IOPS with an EBS volume using the same criteria; do not compare one feature of option A with the total cost of option B. Finally, state the remaining trade-off and how it will be observed after implementation. In Lesson 9, this approach helps BlueRetail understand why the selected option is good enough in the current context and which signals would trigger an architectural review.
saa-09-ebs-instance-store./sections/2/paragraphs/3: A decision involving throughput should be documented as a chain of reasoning, not as a standalone service name. First, confirm which requirements are mandatory, then check quota limits, Region, consistency, network path, and the responsibility model. Next, compare throughput with instance store using the same criteria; do not compare one feature of option A with the total cost of option B. Finally, state the remaining trade-off and how it will be observed after implementation. In Lesson 9, this approach helps BlueRetail understand why the selected option is good enough in the current context and which signals would trigger an architectural review.
saa-09-ebs-instance-store./sections/3/title: Design for failure before it occurs
saa-09-ebs-instance-store./sections/3/paragraphs/0: Failure mode 1 for EBS, instance store, and block storage performance occurs when the project team assumes that an EBS volume automatically addresses every related layer. In reality, service availability does not replace proper access permissions, replication does not replace backups, encryption does not automatically grant permission to use a key, and autoscaling cannot fix a stateful dependency. At BlueRetail, map the blast radius of the EBS volume, marking what AWS is responsible for and what the application team must still configure or test. Then select a metric, log, or event that can provide an early warning. Analyzing failure modes first ensures that the answer is valid not only while the system is healthy, but also provides a recovery path when an AZ, connection, credential, or operational process fails.
saa-09-ebs-instance-store./sections/3/paragraphs/1: Failure mode 2 for EBS, instance store, and block storage performance occurs when the project team assumes that instance store automatically addresses every related layer. In reality, service availability does not replace proper access permissions, replication does not replace backups, encryption does not automatically grant permission to use a key, and autoscaling cannot fix a stateful dependency. At BlueRetail, map the blast radius of instance store, marking what AWS is responsible for and what the application team must still configure or test. Then select a metric, log, or event that can provide an early warning. Analyzing failure modes first ensures that the answer is valid not only while the system is healthy, but also provides a recovery path when an AZ, connection, credential, or operational process fails.
saa-09-ebs-instance-store./sections/3/paragraphs/2: Failure mode 3 for EBS, instance store, and block storage performance occurs when the project team assumes that IOPS automatically addresses every related layer. In reality, service availability does not replace proper access permissions, replication does not replace backups, encryption does not automatically grant permission to use a key, and autoscaling cannot fix a stateful dependency. At BlueRetail, map the blast radius of IOPS, marking what AWS is responsible for and what the application team must still configure or test. Then select a metric, log, or event that can provide an early warning. Analyzing failure modes first ensures that the answer is valid not only while the system is healthy, but also provides a recovery path when an AZ, connection, credential, or operational process fails.
saa-09-ebs-instance-store./sections/4/title: Apply the decision to the BlueRetail case
saa-09-ebs-instance-store./sections/4/paragraphs/0: BlueRetail has stores, an e-commerce website, internal applications, and an operations team working across multiple accounts. In Lesson 9, the image-processing cluster needs a very fast scratch disk, but the output data must persist after an instance is replaced. The first step is to separate the stakeholders: users need a stable experience, security needs boundaries and an audit trail, finance needs cost attribution, and the product team needs to make changes quickly. A design optimized for only one stakeholder creates operational debt for another.
saa-09-ebs-instance-store./sections/4/paragraphs/1: The architecture team documents three options centered on EBS volume, instance store, and IOPS. Each option is scored against the same criteria: satisfaction of mandatory requirements, blast radius, recovery path, operational burden, scalability, and cost per unit of consumption. throughput serves as the final validation criterion. If an option wins only because the team is familiar with it, the decision lacks sufficient evidence and the constraints must be revisited.
saa-09-ebs-instance-store./sections/4/paragraphs/2: The conclusion for EBS, instance store, and block storage performance is written in four sentences: which structure was selected; which requirements it satisfies; which trade-off is accepted; and which metric or review will trigger reassessment. This format helps developers, security, and operations understand the same decision. In exam questions, the four sentences also help eliminate distractors: an option that fails a mandatory requirement is eliminated first, even if it offers many attractive features.
saa-09-ebs-instance-store./sections/5/title: Selecting the right action in SAA-C03 questions
saa-09-ebs-instance-store./sections/5/paragraphs/0: Read an SAA-C03 question in this order: the assigned role, the current state, the required outcome, the constraints, and the verb in the final sentence. For EBS, instance store, and block storage performance, mark every detail related to the EBS volume and instance store. Then classify each answer as fully satisfying the requirements, partially satisfying them, introducing additional risk, or being out of scope. Do not choose an answer merely because it names the correct service; AWS tests your ability to combine multiple requirements into the best-fitting solution.
saa-09-ebs-instance-store./sections/5/paragraphs/1: Absolute phrases such as “no data loss,” “no downtime,” or “all traffic must remain private” require careful evaluation. They may require a completely different design from phrases such as “minimize,” “acceptable,” or “prioritize cost.” In Lesson 9, use IOPS to evaluate the required assurance level and throughput to determine whether the operations team can sustain the option. An answer that provides the right feature but the wrong assurance level is still a distractor.
saa-09-ebs-instance-store./sections/5/paragraphs/2: In Lesson 9—EBS, instance store, and block storage performance—objective SAA-C03 3.1 indicates the required depth of understanding. For design, you must balance quality attributes; for determine, you must interpret the facts and select a cause or option; for improve, you must preserve existing constraints while reducing risk through an appropriate change. End each answer with evidence: a metric, log, policy evaluation, recovery test, or cost report. Evidence turns an answer from a guess into a defensible architectural decision.
