saa-11-auto-scaling-policies./sources/2/label: GitHub topic inventory used to verify coverage—Lesson 11

saa-12-stateless-session-design./summary: Understand stateless design and session management through requirements, constraints, trade-offs, and an architectural decision that can be explained to the BlueRetail team.
saa-12-stateless-session-design./opening/0: BlueRetail is changing its architecture, and users are being logged out when auto scaling replaces an instance because sessions are stored in local memory. A diagram with many AWS icons is not necessarily a good design: what matters is which requirement each component addresses, which failure modes remain, and whether the operations team can manage it. Lesson 12 begins with this scenario and turns stateless design and session management into a sequence of evidence-based decisions rather than a list of features to memorize.
saa-12-stateless-session-design./opening/1: In SAA-C03, questions often present several technically viable options, but only one best matches keywords such as “least operational effort,” “no data loss,” “low cost,” “multiple accounts,” or “no use of the public internet.” Stateless design and session management should be evaluated from four perspectives: stateless compute, session store, load distribution, and horizontal scaling. Each perspective eliminates a group of answers that conflict with the requirements or introduce unacceptable responsibilities.
saa-12-stateless-session-design./opening/2: The scope of Lesson 12 — Stateless Design and Session Management is aligned with objective SAA-C03 2.1 in the current AWS Exam Guide. The GitHub topic inventory is used only to verify coverage and content organization; all explanations, cases, and diagrams here are newly written, while the technical conclusions are validated against AWS Documentation and the Well-Architected Framework. After this lesson, you should be able to explain not only “what to choose,” but also “why not to choose the other options.”
saa-12-stateless-session-design./sections/0/title: Thinking model: start with requirements, not services
saa-12-stateless-session-design./sections/0/paragraphs/0: Treat an AWS architecture as a contract between the business, application, and platform. The business defines what must remain operational and the acceptable level of loss; the application describes traffic, data, and dependencies; the platform translates those requirements into boundaries, services, policies, and observability mechanisms. For stateless design and session management, stateless compute is the entry point, the session store determines how the system responds, and load distribution and horizontal scaling determine long-term operability. Moving directly from a requirement to a service name often causes one of these four layers to be overlooked.
saa-12-stateless-session-design./sections/0/paragraphs/1: A compact model that works for every lesson is R-C-D-E: Requirement, Constraint, Decision, Evidence. A requirement describes the outcome; a constraint limits the scope through factors such as Region, budget, or skills; a decision is the selected architectural structure; and evidence is a metric, log, test, or document confirming that the decision remains valid. By applying R-C-D-E to stateless design and session management, learners can see that the same service may be right for one workload but wrong for another. This skill matters more than memorizing the entire AWS catalog.
saa-12-stateless-session-design./sections/0/paragraphs/2: You have understood Lesson 12 when you can trace a request or operational event from beginning to end. Which boundary does the request cross, which identity is evaluated, where is the data retained, which component can fail, and which mechanism provides recovery? If the narrative breaks at stateless compute or the session store, the diagram still contains an unverified assumption. Record that assumption next to the diagram before selecting another service.
saa-12-stateless-session-design./sections/1/title: Four concepts that must work together
saa-12-stateless-session-design./sections/1/paragraphs/0: stateless compute is the 1st building block in a stateless design and session management decision, but its value becomes clear only when considered alongside business requirements. For SAA-C03, learners should not stop at “what is this service used for?” Identify the input that stateless compute receives, the state or policies it manages, the failure modes it addresses, and the resulting operational cost. At BlueRetail, users are logged out when auto scaling replaces an instance because sessions are stored in local memory. Connecting stateless compute to the session store reveals where the responsibility boundary lies and why two similarly named options can produce different outcomes. A strong solution must trace the technical choice back to latency, availability, security, data, or the operations team’s capabilities.
saa-12-stateless-session-design./sections/1/paragraphs/1: session store is the 2nd building block in a stateless design and session management decision, but its value becomes clear only when considered alongside business requirements. For SAA-C03, learners should not stop at “what is this service used for?” Identify the input that the session store receives, the state or policies it manages, the failure modes it addresses, and the resulting operational cost. At BlueRetail, users are logged out when auto scaling replaces an instance because sessions are stored in local memory. Connecting the session store to load distribution reveals where the responsibility boundary lies and why two similarly named options can produce different outcomes. A strong solution must trace the technical choice back to latency, availability, security, data, or the operations team’s capabilities.
saa-12-stateless-session-design./sections/1/paragraphs/2: load distribution is the 3rd building block in a stateless design and session management decision, but its value becomes clear only when considered alongside business requirements. For SAA-C03, learners should not stop at “what is this service used for?” Identify the input that load distribution receives, the state or policies it manages, the failure modes it addresses, and the resulting operational cost. At BlueRetail, users are logged out when auto scaling replaces an instance because sessions are stored in local memory. Connecting load distribution to horizontal scaling reveals where the responsibility boundary lies and why two similarly named options can produce different outcomes. A strong solution must trace the technical choice back to latency, availability, security, data, or the operations team’s capabilities.
saa-12-stateless-session-design./sections/1/paragraphs/3: horizontal scaling is the 4th building block in a stateless design and session management decision, but its value becomes clear only when considered alongside business requirements. For SAA-C03, learners should not stop at “what is this service used for?” Identify the input that horizontal scaling receives, the state or policies it manages, the failure modes it addresses, and the resulting operational cost. At BlueRetail, users are logged out when auto scaling replaces an instance because sessions are stored in local memory. Connecting horizontal scaling to stateless compute reveals where the responsibility boundary lies and why two similarly named options can produce different outcomes. A strong solution must trace the technical choice back to latency, availability, security, data, or the operations team’s capabilities.
saa-12-stateless-session-design./sections/2/title: Make decisions step by step and document the trade-offs
saa-12-stateless-session-design./sections/2/paragraphs/0: A decision involving stateless compute should be documented as a chain of reasoning, not as a standalone service name. First, confirm which requirements are mandatory, then check quota, Region, consistency, network path, and responsibility-model constraints. Next, compare stateless compute with load distribution using the same criteria; do not compare one feature of option A with the total cost of option B. Finally, state the remaining trade-off and how it will be observed after deployment. In Lesson 12, this approach helps BlueRetail understand why the selected option is good enough in the current context and which signals would indicate that the architecture needs to be reconsidered.
saa-12-stateless-session-design./sections/2/paragraphs/1: A decision involving a session store should be documented as a chain of reasoning, not as a standalone service name. First, confirm which requirements are mandatory, then check quota, Region, consistency, network path, and responsibility-model constraints. Next, compare the session store with horizontal scaling using the same criteria; do not compare one feature of option A with the total cost of option B. Finally, state the remaining trade-off and how it will be observed after deployment. In Lesson 12, this approach helps BlueRetail understand why the selected option is good enough in the current context and which signals would indicate that the architecture needs to be reconsidered.
saa-12-stateless-session-design./sections/2/paragraphs/2: A decision involving load distribution should be documented as a chain of reasoning, not as a standalone service name. First, confirm which requirements are mandatory, then check quota, Region, consistency, network path, and responsibility-model constraints. Next, compare load distribution with stateless compute using the same criteria; do not compare one feature of option A with the total cost of option B. Finally, state the remaining trade-off and how it will be observed after deployment. In Lesson 12, this approach helps BlueRetail understand why the selected option is good enough in the current context and which signals would indicate that the architecture needs to be reconsidered.
saa-12-stateless-session-design./sections/2/paragraphs/3: A decision involving horizontal scaling should be documented as a chain of reasoning, not as a standalone service name. First, confirm which requirements are mandatory, then check quota, Region, consistency, network path, and responsibility-model constraints. Next, compare horizontal scaling with the session store using the same criteria; do not compare one feature of option A with the total cost of option B. Finally, state the remaining trade-off and how it will be observed after deployment. In Lesson 12, this approach helps BlueRetail understand why the selected option is good enough in the current context and which signals would indicate that the architecture needs to be reconsidered.
saa-12-stateless-session-design./sections/3/title: Design for failure before it occurs
saa-12-stateless-session-design./sections/3/paragraphs/0: Failure mode 1 in stateless design and session management occurs when the project team assumes that stateless compute automatically addresses every related layer. In practice, service availability does not replace correct access permissions, replication does not replace backup, encryption does not automatically grant permission to use a key, and autoscaling cannot fix a stateful dependency. At BlueRetail, map the blast radius of stateless compute and mark what AWS is responsible for and what the application team must still configure or test. Then select a metric, log, or event that can provide an early warning. Analyzing the failure mode first ensures that the answer is valid not only while the system is healthy but also provides a recovery path when an AZ, connection, credential, or operational process fails.
saa-12-stateless-session-design./sections/3/paragraphs/1: Failure mode 2 in stateless design and session management occurs when the project team assumes that the session store automatically addresses every related layer. In practice, service availability does not replace correct access permissions, replication does not replace backup, encryption does not automatically grant permission to use a key, and autoscaling cannot fix a stateful dependency. At BlueRetail, map the blast radius of the session store and mark what AWS is responsible for and what the application team must still configure or test. Then select a metric, log, or event that can provide an early warning. Analyzing the failure mode first ensures that the answer is valid not only while the system is healthy but also provides a recovery path when an AZ, connection, credential, or operational process fails.
saa-12-stateless-session-design./sections/3/paragraphs/2: Failure mode 3 in stateless design and session management occurs when the project team assumes that load distribution automatically addresses every related layer. In practice, service availability does not replace correct access permissions, replication does not replace backup, encryption does not automatically grant permission to use a key, and autoscaling cannot fix a stateful dependency. At BlueRetail, map the blast radius of load distribution and mark what AWS is responsible for and what the application team must still configure or test. Then select a metric, log, or event that can provide an early warning. Analyzing the failure mode first ensures that the answer is valid not only while the system is healthy but also provides a recovery path when an AZ, connection, credential, or operational process fails.
saa-12-stateless-session-design./sections/4/title: Apply the decision to the BlueRetail case
saa-12-stateless-session-design./sections/4/paragraphs/0: BlueRetail has physical stores, an e-commerce website, internal applications, and an operations team working across multiple accounts. In Lesson 12, users are logged out when Auto Scaling replaces an instance because sessions are stored in local memory. The first step is to separate the stakeholders: users need a stable experience, security needs boundaries and an audit trail, finance needs cost attribution, and the product team needs to make changes quickly. A design optimized for only one stakeholder creates operational debt for another.
saa-12-stateless-session-design./sections/4/paragraphs/1: The architecture team documents three options centered on stateless compute, the session store, and load distribution. Each option is scored against the same criteria: mandatory requirement coverage, blast radius, recovery path, operational burden, scalability, and cost per unit of usage. horizontal scaling serves as the final validation condition. If an option wins only because the team is familiar with it, the decision lacks sufficient evidence and the constraints must be revisited.
saa-12-stateless-session-design./sections/4/paragraphs/2: The conclusion for stateless design and session management is written in four sentences: which structure is selected; which requirements it meets; which trade-off is accepted; and which metric or review will trigger reassessment. This format helps developers, security, and operations understand the same decision. In an exam question, the four sentences also help eliminate distractors: an option that fails a mandatory requirement is eliminated first, even if it offers many attractive features.
saa-12-stateless-session-design./sections/5/title: Choosing the right action in SAA-C03 questions
saa-12-stateless-session-design./sections/5/paragraphs/0: Read an SAA-C03 question in this order: the assigned role, the current state, the mandatory outcome, the constraints, and the verb in the final sentence. For stateless design and session management, mark every detail related to stateless compute and the session store. Then classify the answers as fully satisfying the requirements, partially satisfying them, introducing additional risk, or being out of scope. Do not choose an answer merely because it mentions the correct service; AWS tests your ability to combine multiple requirements into the most appropriate solution.
saa-12-stateless-session-design./sections/5/paragraphs/1: Absolute phrases such as “no data loss,” “no downtime,” or “all traffic must remain private” require careful evaluation. They may call for a completely different design from phrases such as “minimize,” “acceptable,” or “prioritize cost.” In Lesson 12, use load distribution to evaluate the level of assurance and horizontal scaling to determine whether the operations team can sustain the option. An answer that provides the correct feature but the wrong assurance level is still a distractor.
saa-12-stateless-session-design./sections/5/paragraphs/2: In Lesson 12 — Stateless Design and Session Management, objective SAA-C03 2.1 indicates the required depth. For design, you must balance quality attributes; for determine, you must interpret the facts and select a cause or option; for improve, you must preserve the existing constraints and reduce risk through an appropriate change. End each answer with evidence: a metric, log, policy evaluation, recovery test, or cost report. Evidence turns an answer from a guess into a defensible architectural decision.
saa-12-stateless-session-design./visuals/0/title: Decision map: Stateless design and session management
saa-12-stateless-session-design./visuals/0/caption: An original diagram connecting requirements to stateless compute, session store, load distribution, and horizontal scaling in the BlueRetail case.
saa-12-stateless-session-design./decisionWalkthroughs/0/title: Stateless design and session management decision analysis
saa-12-stateless-session-design./decisionWalkthroughs/0/businessRequirem: BlueRetail must address the situation in which “users are logged out when Auto Scaling replaces an instance because sessions are stored in local memory” while still being able to explain the impact on security, resilience, performance, and cost.
saa-12-stateless-session-design./decisionWalkthroughs/0/technicalConstra: The design must clarify the stateless compute boundary and the responsibility of the session store.
The option must include a failure mode and recovery path related to load distribution.
The operations team must use real data to observe and reassess horizontal scaling.
saa-12-stateless-session-design./decisionWalkthroughs/0/candidates/0/fit: A good fit when the requirement prioritizes the stateless compute perspective in step 1.
saa-12-stateless-session-design./decisionWalkthroughs/0/candidates/0/lim: Does not address the session store by itself, so the remaining responsibilities must be evaluated.
saa-12-stateless-session-design./decisionWalkthroughs/0/candidates/1/fit: A good fit when the requirement prioritizes the session store perspective in step 2.
saa-12-stateless-session-design./decisionWalkthroughs/0/candidates/1/lim: Does not address load distribution by itself, so the remaining responsibilities must be evaluated.
saa-12-stateless-session-design./decisionWalkthroughs/0/candidates/2/fit: A good fit when the requirement prioritizes the load distribution perspective in step 3.
saa-12-stateless-session-design./decisionWalkthroughs/0/candidates/2/lim: Does not address horizontal scaling by itself, so the remaining responsibilities must be evaluated.
saa-12-stateless-session-design./decisionWalkthroughs/0/decision: Select an option that combines stateless compute with a session store, using load distribution as a guardrail and horizontal scaling as operational evidence.
saa-12-stateless-session-design./decisionWalkthroughs/0/reasoning/0: Eliminate options that fail mandatory requirements before comparing costs.
saa-12-stateless-session-design./decisionWalkthroughs/0/reasoning/1: Compare options using the same quality attributes and time horizon.
saa-12-stateless-session-design./decisionWalkthroughs/0/reasoning/2: Clearly document the trade-off, blast radius, and responsibilities that remain with the application team.
saa-12-stateless-session-design./decisionWalkthroughs/0/reasoning/3: Define the metric or review that would trigger reconsideration of the decision.
saa-12-stateless-session-design./comparison/0/bestFit: Prioritize stateless compute when the primary requirement is addressed in step 1 of the decision.
saa-12-stateless-session-design./comparison/0/blueRetailUse: BlueRetail uses stateless compute to address users being logged out when auto scaling replaces an instance because sessions are stored in local memory.
saa-12-stateless-session-design./comparison/0/tradeOff: A session store must be added or validated; do not treat this as a solution for every layer.
saa-12-stateless-session-design./comparison/1/bestFit: Prioritize the session store when the primary requirement is addressed in step 2 of the decision.
saa-12-stateless-session-design./comparison/1/blueRetailUse: BlueRetail uses a session store to address users being logged out when auto scaling replaces an instance because sessions are stored in local memory.
saa-12-stateless-session-design./comparison/1/tradeOff: Load distribution must be added or validated; do not treat this as a solution for every layer.
saa-12-stateless-session-design./comparison/2/bestFit: Prioritize load distribution when the primary requirement is addressed in step 3 of the decision.
saa-12-stateless-session-design./comparison/2/blueRetailUse: BlueRetail uses load distribution to address users being logged out when auto scaling replaces an instance because sessions are stored in local memory.
saa-12-stateless-session-design./comparison/2/tradeOff: Horizontal scaling must be added or validated; do not treat this as a solution for every layer.
saa-12-stateless-session-design./commonMistakes/0/wrong: Seeing the keyword stateless compute and immediately selecting an answer with the same service or feature name.
saa-12-stateless-session-design./commonMistakes/0/better: Evaluate stateless compute against the requirement, constraints, and session store before making a selection.
saa-12-stateless-session-design./commonMistakes/0/reason: In Lesson 12, a choice is correct only when the entire chain of responsibilities and failure modes is addressed.
saa-12-stateless-session-design./commonMistakes/1/wrong: Seeing the keyword session store and immediately selecting an answer with the same service or feature name.
saa-12-stateless-session-design./commonMistakes/1/better: Evaluate the session store against the requirement, constraints, and load distribution before making a selection.
saa-12-stateless-session-design./commonMistakes/1/reason: In Lesson 12, a choice is correct only when the entire chain of responsibilities and failure modes is addressed.
saa-12-stateless-session-design./commonMistakes/2/wrong: Seeing the keyword load distribution and immediately selecting an answer with the same service or feature name.
saa-12-stateless-session-design./commonMistakes/2/better: Evaluate load distribution against the requirement, constraints, and horizontal scaling before making a selection.
saa-12-stateless-session-design./commonMistakes/2/reason: In Lesson 12, a choice is correct only when the entire chain of responsibilities and failure modes is addressed.
saa-12-stateless-session-design./examFocus/0: Read the verb in objective SAA-C03 2.1 to determine the required response depth.
saa-12-stateless-session-design./examFocus/1: Distinguish mandatory requirements from background details related to stateless compute.
saa-12-stateless-session-design./examFocus/2: Eliminate answers that shift risk from the session store to load distribution without actually addressing it.
saa-12-stateless-session-design./examFocus/3: Select an option with an appropriate operational burden and evidence provided through horizontal scaling.
saa-12-stateless-session-design./activity/title: Architecture review: Stateless design and session management
saa-12-stateless-session-design./activity/context: BlueRetail is preparing to approve a change because users are logged out when auto scaling replaces an instance because sessions are stored in local memory. You must review the architecture before the team implements it or incurs a cost commitment.
saa-12-stateless-session-design./activity/learnerRole: You are the Solutions Architect responsible for a workload, translating requirements into a decision and clearly explaining the trade-off to stakeholders.
saa-12-stateless-session-design./activity/currentArchitecture: Users → access layer → workload → data store; the review points are stateless compute → session store → load distribution → horizontal scaling.
saa-12-stateless-session-design./activity/requirements/0: The solution must correctly address the requirement related to stateless compute.
saa-12-stateless-session-design./activity/requirements/1: The recovery path must show the role of the session store.
saa-12-stateless-session-design./activity/requirements/2: Security and operations must be able to observe load distribution without relying on assumptions.
saa-12-stateless-session-design./activity/constraints/0: Do not add a service merely to mask a requirement that has not been clearly defined.
saa-12-stateless-session-design./activity/constraints/1: The horizontal scaling trade-off must be accepted by stakeholders.
saa-12-stateless-session-design./activity/constraints/2: Do not change the storage schema or account boundary without an architectural reason.
saa-12-stateless-session-design./activity/tasks/0: Rewrite the requirement as measurable criteria.
saa-12-stateless-session-design./activity/tasks/1: Compare at least two options based on stateless compute, the session store, and load distribution.
saa-12-stateless-session-design./activity/tasks/2: Select an option, document the trade-off, and propose post-deployment evidence.
saa-12-stateless-session-design./activity/reasoningSteps/0: Separate the business outcome, technical constraints, and unconfirmed assumptions.
saa-12-stateless-session-design./activity/reasoningSteps/1: Diagram the request and data flow, including the trust boundary and blast radius.
saa-12-stateless-session-design./activity/reasoningSteps/2: Eliminate options that fail mandatory requirements before optimizing.
saa-12-stateless-session-design./activity/reasoningSteps/3: Compare the remaining options across reliability, security, performance, cost, and operations.
saa-12-stateless-session-design./activity/reasoningSteps/4: Document the decision, trade-off, recovery path, and review metric.
saa-12-stateless-session-design./activity/deliverables/0: An architecture diagram showing boundaries and data-flow directions.
saa-12-stateless-session-design./activity/deliverables/1: A decision matrix with at least two options.
saa-12-stateless-session-design./activity/deliverables/2: A short architecture decision record containing the trade-off and evidence.
saa-12-stateless-session-design./activity/rubric/0/evidence: The decision can be traced back to stateless compute and introduces no assumptions beyond the scenario.
saa-12-stateless-session-design./activity/rubric/0/importance: Prevents selecting a service based on preference or a single keyword.
saa-12-stateless-session-design./activity/rubric/1/evidence: Compares the session store, load distribution, and horizontal scaling using the same criteria.
saa-12-stateless-session-design./activity/rubric/1/importance: AWS architecture always involves balance; no option is absolutely best.
saa-12-stateless-session-design./activity/rubric/2/evidence: The submission identifies a failure mode, recovery path, metric, or validation test.
saa-12-stateless-session-design./activity/rubric/2/importance: A design is trustworthy only when it can be operated and validated after deployment.
saa-12-stateless-session-design./activity/sampleAnalysis/0: The primary requirement is rewritten around stateless compute; the session store is a constraint that directly affects the choice.
saa-12-stateless-session-design./activity/sampleAnalysis/1: An option that prioritizes load distribution is eliminated if it increases the operational burden of horizontal scaling without adding value.
saa-12-stateless-session-design./activity/sampleAnalysis/2: The proposed architecture combines stateless compute with a session store and clearly documents the blast radius, recovery path, and review metric.
saa-12-stateless-session-design./activity/sampleAnalysis/3: The remaining trade-off is accepted because it fits the situation in which users are logged out when auto scaling replaces an instance because sessions are stored in local memory.
saa-12-stateless-session-design./activity/commonMistakes/0: Listing services without connecting them to the requirement.
saa-12-stateless-session-design./activity/commonMistakes/1: Describing only the happy path and ignoring failure modes.
saa-12-stateless-session-design./activity/commonMistakes/2: Saying “AWS manages it” without identifying the customer’s responsibilities.

saa-13-compute-selection./title: Choosing EC2, Lambda, ECS, EKS, or Fargate
saa-13-compute-selection./shortTitle: Choosing EC2, Lambda, ECS, EKS, or Fargate
saa-13-compute-selection./summary: Learn how to choose EC2, Lambda, ECS, EKS, or Fargate based on requirements, constraints, trade-offs, and an architectural decision that can be explained to the BlueRetail team.
saa-13-compute-selection./opening/0: BlueRetail is changing its architecture, and five product teams are choosing compute services out of habit even though their processing durations, control requirements, and operational skills differ greatly. A diagram with many AWS icons is not necessarily a good design: what matters is which requirement each component addresses, which failure modes remain, and whether the operations team can manage it. Lesson 13 begins with this scenario and turns the choice among EC2, Lambda, ECS, EKS, and Fargate into an evidence-based decision process rather than a list of features to memorize.
saa-13-compute-selection./opening/1: In SAA-C03, questions often present several technically viable options, but only one best matches keywords such as “least operational effort,” “no data loss,” “low cost,” “multiple accounts,” or “no use of the public internet.” The choice among EC2, Lambda, ECS, EKS, and Fargate should be evaluated from four perspectives: execution model, operational control, scaling unit, and runtime constraint. Each perspective eliminates a group of answers that conflict with the requirements or introduce unacceptable responsibilities.
saa-13-compute-selection./opening/2: The scope of Lesson 13—Choosing EC2, Lambda, ECS, EKS, or Fargate—is aligned with objective SAA-C03 3.2 in the current AWS Exam Guide. The GitHub topic repository is used only to verify coverage and content structure; all explanations, cases, and diagrams here are newly written, while the technical conclusions are validated against AWS Documentation and the Well-Architected Framework. After this lesson, you should be able to explain not only “what to choose,” but also “why the other options were not chosen.”
saa-13-compute-selection./sections/0/title: Mental model: Start with requirements, not services
saa-13-compute-selection./sections/0/paragraphs/0: Treat an AWS architecture as a contract among business, application, and platform teams. The business defines what must remain operational and what level of loss is acceptable; the application describes traffic, data, and dependencies; and the platform translates those requirements into boundaries, services, policies, and observability mechanisms. When choosing EC2, Lambda, ECS, EKS, or Fargate, the execution model is the entry point, operational control determines how the system responds, and the scaling unit and runtime constraint determine long-term operability. Going directly from a requirement to a service name often causes one of these four layers to be overlooked.
saa-13-compute-selection./sections/0/paragraphs/1: A compact model that works for every lesson is R-C-D-E: Requirement, Constraint, Decision, Evidence. A requirement describes the outcome; a constraint limits the scope through factors such as Region, budget, or skills; a decision is the selected architectural structure; and evidence is a metric, log, test, or document confirming that the decision remains valid. By applying R-C-D-E to choosing EC2, Lambda, ECS, EKS, or Fargate, learners can see that the same service may be right for one workload but wrong for another. This skill matters more than memorizing the entire AWS catalog.
saa-13-compute-selection./sections/0/paragraphs/2: You have understood Lesson 13 when you can trace a request or operational event from beginning to end. Which boundary does the request cross, which identity is evaluated, where is the data retained, which component can fail, and what mechanism provides recovery? If the story breaks at the execution model or operational control, the diagram still contains an unverified assumption. Record that assumption next to the diagram before selecting another service.
saa-13-compute-selection./sections/1/title: Four concepts that must be connected
saa-13-compute-selection./sections/1/paragraphs/0: The execution model is the first part of deciding among EC2, Lambda, ECS, EKS, and Fargate, but its value emerges only when it is considered alongside business requirements. For SAA-C03, learners should not stop at “What is this service used for?” Identify the input received by the execution model, the state or policy it manages, the failure mode it addresses, and the resulting operational cost. At BlueRetail, five product teams are choosing compute services out of habit even though their processing durations, control requirements, and operational skills differ greatly. Connecting the execution model to operational control reveals where the responsibility boundary lies and why two similarly named options can produce different outcomes. A good solution must trace the technical choice back to latency, availability, security, data, or the operations team’s capabilities.
saa-13-compute-selection./sections/1/paragraphs/1: Operational control is the second part of deciding among EC2, Lambda, ECS, EKS, and Fargate, but its value emerges only when it is considered alongside business requirements. For SAA-C03, learners should not stop at “What is this service used for?” Identify the input received by operational control, the state or policy it manages, the failure mode it addresses, and the resulting operational cost. At BlueRetail, five product teams are choosing compute services out of habit even though their processing durations, control requirements, and operational skills differ greatly. Connecting operational control to the scaling unit reveals where the responsibility boundary lies and why two similarly named options can produce different outcomes. A good solution must trace the technical choice back to latency, availability, security, data, or the operations team’s capabilities.
saa-13-compute-selection./sections/1/paragraphs/2: The scaling unit is the third part of deciding among EC2, Lambda, ECS, EKS, and Fargate, but its value emerges only when it is considered alongside business requirements. For SAA-C03, learners should not stop at “What is this service used for?” Identify the input received by the scaling unit, the state or policy it manages, the failure mode it addresses, and the resulting operational cost. At BlueRetail, five product teams are choosing compute services out of habit even though their processing durations, control requirements, and operational skills differ greatly. Connecting the scaling unit to the runtime constraint reveals where the responsibility boundary lies and why two similarly named options can produce different outcomes. A good solution must trace the technical choice back to latency, availability, security, data, or the operations team’s capabilities.
saa-13-compute-selection./sections/1/paragraphs/3: The runtime constraint is the fourth part of deciding among EC2, Lambda, ECS, EKS, and Fargate, but its value emerges only when it is considered alongside business requirements. For SAA-C03, learners should not stop at “What is this service used for?” Identify the input received by the runtime constraint, the state or policy it manages, the failure mode it addresses, and the resulting operational cost. At BlueRetail, five product teams are choosing compute services out of habit even though their processing durations, control requirements, and operational skills differ greatly. Connecting the runtime constraint to the execution model reveals where the responsibility boundary lies and why two similarly named options can produce different outcomes. A good solution must trace the technical choice back to latency, availability, security, data, or the operations team’s capabilities.
saa-13-compute-selection./sections/2/title: Make decisions step by step and document the trade-offs
saa-13-compute-selection./sections/2/paragraphs/0: A decision concerning the execution model should be documented as a chain of reasoning, not as a service name in isolation. First, confirm which requirements are mandatory, then examine quota limits, Region, consistency, network path, and the responsibility model. Next, compare the execution model with the scaling unit using the same criteria; do not compare one feature of option A with the total cost of option B. Finally, state the remaining trade-off and how it will be observed after deployment. In Lesson 13, this approach helps BlueRetail understand why the selected option is good enough in the current context and which signals would indicate that the architecture needs to be reconsidered.
saa-13-compute-selection./sections/2/paragraphs/1: A decision concerning operational control should be documented as a chain of reasoning, not as a service name in isolation. First, confirm which requirements are mandatory, then examine quota limits, Region, consistency, network path, and the responsibility model. Next, compare operational control with the runtime constraint using the same criteria; do not compare one feature of option A with the total cost of option B. Finally, state the remaining trade-off and how it will be observed after deployment. In Lesson 13, this approach helps BlueRetail understand why the selected option is good enough in the current context and which signals would indicate that the architecture needs to be reconsidered.
saa-13-compute-selection./sections/2/paragraphs/2: A decision concerning the scaling unit should be documented as a chain of reasoning, not as a service name in isolation. First, confirm which requirements are mandatory, then examine quota limits, Region, consistency, network path, and the responsibility model. Next, compare the scaling unit with the execution model using the same criteria; do not compare one feature of option A with the total cost of option B. Finally, state the remaining trade-off and how it will be observed after deployment. In Lesson 13, this approach helps BlueRetail understand why the selected option is good enough in the current context and which signals would indicate that the architecture needs to be reconsidered.
saa-13-compute-selection./sections/2/paragraphs/3: A decision concerning the runtime constraint should be documented as a chain of reasoning, not as a service name in isolation. First, confirm which requirements are mandatory, then examine quota limits, Region, consistency, network path, and the responsibility model. Next, compare the runtime constraint with operational control using the same criteria; do not compare one feature of option A with the total cost of option B. Finally, state the remaining trade-off and how it will be observed after deployment. In Lesson 13, this approach helps BlueRetail understand why the selected option is good enough in the current context and which signals would indicate that the architecture needs to be reconsidered.
saa-13-compute-selection./sections/3/title: Design for failure before it occurs
saa-13-compute-selection./sections/3/paragraphs/0: Failure mode 1 when choosing EC2, Lambda, ECS, EKS, or Fargate occurs when the project team assumes that the execution model automatically addresses every related layer. In practice, service availability does not replace correct access permissions, replication does not replace backup, encryption does not automatically grant permission to use a key, and autoscaling cannot fix a stateful dependency. At BlueRetail, diagram the blast radius of the execution model and mark what AWS is responsible for and what the application team must still configure or test. Then select a metric, log, or event that can provide an early warning. Analyzing failure modes first ensures that the answer is valid not only while the system is healthy, but also provides a recovery path when an AZ, connection, credential, or operational process fails.
saa-13-compute-selection./sections/3/paragraphs/1: Failure mode 2 when choosing EC2, Lambda, ECS, EKS, or Fargate occurs when the project team assumes that operational control automatically addresses every related layer. In practice, service availability does not replace correct access permissions, replication does not replace backup, encryption does not automatically grant permission to use a key, and autoscaling cannot fix a stateful dependency. At BlueRetail, diagram the blast radius of operational control and mark what AWS is responsible for and what the application team must still configure or test. Then select a metric, log, or event that can provide an early warning. Analyzing failure modes first ensures that the answer is valid not only while the system is healthy, but also provides a recovery path when an AZ, connection, credential, or operational process fails.
saa-13-compute-selection./sections/3/paragraphs/2: Failure mode 3 when choosing EC2, Lambda, ECS, EKS, or Fargate occurs when the project team assumes that the scaling unit automatically addresses every related layer. In practice, service availability does not replace correct access permissions, replication does not replace backup, encryption does not automatically grant permission to use a key, and autoscaling cannot fix a stateful dependency. At BlueRetail, diagram the blast radius of the scaling unit and mark what AWS is responsible for and what the application team must still configure or test. Then select a metric, log, or event that can provide an early warning. Analyzing failure modes first ensures that the answer is valid not only while the system is healthy, but also provides a recovery path when an AZ, connection, credential, or operational process fails.
saa-13-compute-selection./sections/4/title: Apply the decision to the BlueRetail case
saa-13-compute-selection./sections/4/paragraphs/0: BlueRetail has physical stores, an e-commerce website, internal applications, and an operations team working across multiple accounts. In Lesson 13, five product teams are choosing compute services out of habit even though their processing durations, control requirements, and operational skills differ greatly. The first step is to separate the stakeholders: users need a stable experience, security needs boundaries and an audit trail, finance needs cost attribution, and product teams need to make changes quickly. A design optimized for only one stakeholder creates operational debt for another.
saa-13-compute-selection./sections/4/paragraphs/1: The architecture team documents three options centered on the execution model, operational control, and scaling unit. Each option is scored using the same matrix: fulfillment of mandatory requirements, blast radius, recovery path, operational burden, scalability, and cost per unit of consumption. The runtime constraint serves as the final validation condition. If an option wins only because the team is familiar with it, the decision lacks sufficient evidence and the team must revisit the constraints.
saa-13-compute-selection./sections/4/paragraphs/2: The conclusion for choosing EC2, Lambda, ECS, EKS, or Fargate is written in four sentences: which structure is selected, which requirements it satisfies, which trade-off is accepted, and which metric or review will trigger reassessment. This format helps developers, security, and operations understand the same decision. In exam questions, the four sentences also help eliminate distractors: an option that does not meet a mandatory requirement is eliminated first, even if it offers many appealing features.
saa-13-compute-selection./sections/5/title: Reasoning about actions in SAA-C03 questions
saa-13-compute-selection./sections/5/paragraphs/0: Read an SAA-C03 question in this order: the assigned role, current state, required outcome, constraints, and the verb at the end of the question. When choosing EC2, Lambda, ECS, EKS, or Fargate, mark every detail related to the execution model and operational control. Then classify each answer as fully meeting the requirements, partially meeting them, introducing additional risk, or being out of scope. Do not select an answer merely because it mentions the correct service; AWS tests your ability to combine multiple requirements into the most appropriate solution.
saa-13-compute-selection./sections/5/paragraphs/1: Absolute phrases such as “no data loss,” “no downtime,” or “all traffic must remain private” require careful examination. They may demand designs completely different from those indicated by “minimize,” “acceptable,” or “prioritize cost.” In Lesson 13, use the scaling unit to verify the required assurance level and the runtime constraint to determine whether the operations team can sustain the option. An answer with the right feature but the wrong assurance level is still a distractor.
saa-13-compute-selection./sections/5/paragraphs/2: In Lesson 13—Choosing EC2, Lambda, ECS, EKS, or Fargate—objective SAA-C03 3.2 indicates the required depth of understanding. For design, you must balance quality attributes; for determine, you must interpret the facts and select a cause or option; for improve, you must preserve existing constraints while reducing risk through an appropriate change. End each answer with evidence: a metric, log, policy evaluation, recovery test, or cost report. Evidence turns an answer from a guess into a defensible architectural decision.

saa-13-compute-selection./activity/title: Architecture review: Choosing EC2, Lambda, ECS, EKS, or Fargate
saa-13-compute-selection./activity/context: BlueRetail is preparing to approve a change because five product teams are choosing compute services out of habit even though their processing durations, control requirements, and operational skills differ greatly. You must review the architecture before the team deploys it or incurs a cost commitment.
saa-13-compute-selection./activity/learnerRole: You are the Solutions Architect responsible for a workload, translating requirements into a decision and clearly communicating the trade-offs to stakeholders.
saa-13-compute-selection./activity/currentArchitecture: Users → access layer → workload → data store; the review points are execution model → operational control → scaling unit → runtime constraint.
saa-13-compute-selection./activity/requirements/0: The solution must correctly address the requirement related to the execution model.
saa-13-compute-selection./activity/requirements/1: The recovery path must demonstrate the role of operational control.
saa-13-compute-selection./activity/requirements/2: Security and operations teams must be able to observe the scaling unit without relying on assumptions.
saa-13-compute-selection./activity/constraints/0: Do not add a service merely to conceal a requirement that has not been clearly defined.
saa-13-compute-selection./activity/constraints/1: Stakeholders must accept the trade-off involving the runtime constraint.
saa-13-compute-selection./activity/constraints/2: Do not change the storage schema or account boundary without an architectural justification.
saa-13-compute-selection./activity/tasks/0: Rewrite the requirement as measurable criteria.
saa-13-compute-selection./activity/tasks/1: Compare at least two options based on the execution model, operational control, and scaling unit.
saa-13-compute-selection./activity/tasks/2: Select an option, document the trade-off, and propose post-deployment evidence.
saa-13-compute-selection./activity/reasoningSteps/0: Separate the business outcome, technical constraints, and unconfirmed assumptions.
saa-13-compute-selection./activity/reasoningSteps/1: Diagram the request and data flows, including trust boundaries and the blast radius.
saa-13-compute-selection./activity/reasoningSteps/2: Eliminate options that fail to meet mandatory requirements before optimizing.
saa-13-compute-selection./activity/reasoningSteps/3: Compare the remaining options across reliability, security, performance, cost, and operations.
saa-13-compute-selection./activity/reasoningSteps/4: Document the decision, trade-off, recovery path, and review metric.
saa-13-compute-selection./activity/deliverables/0: An architecture diagram showing boundaries and data-flow directions.
saa-13-compute-selection./activity/deliverables/1: A decision matrix containing at least two options.
saa-13-compute-selection./activity/deliverables/2: A brief architecture decision record containing trade-offs and evidence.
saa-13-compute-selection./activity/rubric/0/evidence: The decision can be traced back to the execution model and does not introduce assumptions beyond the scenario.
saa-13-compute-selection./activity/rubric/0/importance: Prevents selecting services based on preference or a single keyword.
saa-13-compute-selection./activity/rubric/1/evidence: Operational control, the scaling unit, and the runtime constraint are compared using the same criteria.
saa-13-compute-selection./activity/rubric/1/importance: AWS architecture always involves balance; no option is absolutely best.
saa-13-compute-selection./activity/rubric/2/evidence: The submission identifies a failure mode, recovery path, and validating metric or test.
saa-13-compute-selection./activity/rubric/2/importance: A design is trustworthy only when it can be operated and validated after deployment.
saa-13-compute-selection./activity/sampleAnalysis/0: The primary requirement is rewritten around the execution model; operational control is a constraint that directly affects the choice.
saa-13-compute-selection./activity/sampleAnalysis/1: An option that prioritizes the scaling unit is eliminated if it increases the operational burden of the runtime constraint without adding value.
saa-13-compute-selection./activity/sampleAnalysis/2: The proposed architecture combines the execution model and operational control, with a clearly documented blast radius, recovery path, and review metric.
saa-13-compute-selection./activity/sampleAnalysis/3: The remaining trade-off is accepted because it fits the situation in which five product teams are choosing compute services out of habit even though their processing durations, control requirements, and operational skills differ greatly.
saa-13-compute-selection./activity/commonMistakes/0: Listing services without connecting them to requirements.
saa-13-compute-selection./activity/commonMistakes/1: Describing only the happy path and ignoring failure modes.
saa-13-compute-selection./activity/commonMistakes/2: Saying “AWS manages it” without identifying the customer’s responsibilities.
saa-13-compute-selection./activity/reflectionQuestions/0: Which detail in the scenario eliminated the most options?
saa-13-compute-selection./activity/reflectionQuestions/1: Which metric or event would cause you to reconsider the decision?
saa-13-compute-selection./activity/reflectionQuestions/2: If the budget or RTO changes, which part of the architecture must change first?
saa-13-compute-selection./recap/0: Choosing EC2, Lambda, ECS, EKS, or Fargate must begin with requirements and constraints.
saa-13-compute-selection./recap/1: The execution model, operational control, scaling unit, and runtime constraint form a chain of responsibilities.
saa-13-compute-selection./recap/2: Every decision needs a trade-off, recovery path, and operational evidence.
saa-13-compute-selection./recap/3: In exam questions, eliminate options that do not meet mandatory requirements before comparing optimizations.

saa-13-compute-selection./selfCheck/3: Which metric, log, or test would you use to validate the decision?

saa-13-compute-selection./sources/2/label: GitHub topic inventory used to verify coverage—Lesson 13

saa-14-vpc-addressing-subnets./summary: Understand VPC, CIDR, and subnet design through requirements, constraints, trade-offs, and an architectural decision that can be explained to the BlueRetail team.
saa-14-vpc-addressing-subnets./opening/0: BlueRetail is changing its architecture and expanding its network across multiple AZs, but its address ranges overlap, and the concept of a public subnet is being misunderstood as whether an instance has a public IP. A diagram with many AWS icons is not necessarily a good design: what matters is which requirement each component addresses, which failure modes remain, and whether the operations team can manage it. Lesson 14 starts with this scenario and turns VPC, CIDR, and subnet design into a sequence of evidence-based decisions rather than a list of features to memorize.
saa-14-vpc-addressing-subnets./opening/1: In SAA-C03, questions often present several technically viable options, but only one best matches keywords such as “least operational effort,” “no data loss,” “low cost,” “multiple accounts,” or “no use of the public internet.” VPC, CIDR, and subnet design should be evaluated from four perspectives: CIDR plan, public subnet, private subnet, and route boundary. Each perspective eliminates a group of answers if they conflict with the requirements or introduce unacceptable responsibilities.
saa-14-vpc-addressing-subnets./opening/2: The scope of Lesson 14—VPC, CIDR, and subnet design—is aligned with objective SAA-C03 3.4 in the current AWS Exam Guide. The GitHub topic inventory is used only to verify coverage and content organization; all explanations, cases, and diagrams here are newly written, while the technical conclusions are validated against AWS Documentation and the Well-Architected Framework. After this lesson, you should be able to explain not only “what to choose,” but also “why the other options should not be chosen.”
saa-14-vpc-addressing-subnets./sections/0/title: Mental model: start with requirements, not services
saa-14-vpc-addressing-subnets./sections/0/paragraphs/0: Treat an AWS architecture as a contract among business, application, and platform teams. The business defines what must continue operating and what level of loss is acceptable; the application describes traffic, data, and dependencies; the platform translates those requirements into boundaries, services, policies, and observability mechanisms. For VPC, CIDR, and subnet design, the CIDR plan is the entry point, the public subnet indicates how the system responds, and the private subnet and route boundary determine long-term operability. Moving directly from a requirement to a service name often causes one of these four layers to be overlooked.
saa-14-vpc-addressing-subnets./sections/0/paragraphs/1: A compact model that works for any problem is R-C-D-E: Requirement, Constraint, Decision, Evidence. A requirement describes the outcome; a constraint limits the scope through factors such as Region, budget, or skills; a decision is the selected architectural structure; and evidence is a metric, log, test, or document confirming that the decision remains valid. Applying R-C-D-E to VPC, CIDR, and subnet design shows learners that the same service can be right for one workload and wrong for another. This skill is more important than memorizing the entire AWS catalog.
saa-14-vpc-addressing-subnets./sections/0/paragraphs/2: You have understood Lesson 14 when you can trace a request or operational event from beginning to end. Which boundaries does the request cross, which identities are evaluated, where is the data retained, which components can fail, and which mechanisms provide recovery? If the story breaks at the CIDR plan or public subnet, the diagram still contains an unverified assumption. Record that assumption beside the diagram before selecting another service.
saa-14-vpc-addressing-subnets./sections/1/title: Four concepts that must be connected
saa-14-vpc-addressing-subnets./sections/1/paragraphs/0: The CIDR plan is the first component in a VPC, CIDR, and subnet design decision, but its value becomes clear only when considered alongside business requirements. For SAA-C03, learners should not stop at asking, “What is this service used for?” Identify the input the CIDR plan receives, the state or policy it manages, the failure modes it addresses, and the operational costs it introduces. At BlueRetail, the network is expanding across multiple AZs, but address ranges overlap, and a public subnet is being defined according to whether an instance has a public IP. Connecting the CIDR plan to the public subnet clarifies where responsibility boundaries lie and why two similarly named options can produce different outcomes. A good solution must trace the technical choice back to latency, availability, security, data, or the operations team's capabilities.
saa-14-vpc-addressing-subnets./sections/1/paragraphs/1: The public subnet is the second component in a VPC, CIDR, and subnet design decision, but its value becomes clear only when considered alongside business requirements. For SAA-C03, learners should not stop at asking, “What is this service used for?” Identify the input the public subnet receives, the state or policy it manages, the failure modes it addresses, and the operational costs it introduces. At BlueRetail, the network is expanding across multiple AZs, but address ranges overlap, and a public subnet is being defined according to whether an instance has a public IP. Connecting the public subnet to the private subnet clarifies where responsibility boundaries lie and why two similarly named options can produce different outcomes. A good solution must trace the technical choice back to latency, availability, security, data, or the operations team's capabilities.
saa-14-vpc-addressing-subnets./sections/1/paragraphs/2: The private subnet is the third component in a VPC, CIDR, and subnet design decision, but its value becomes clear only when considered alongside business requirements. For SAA-C03, learners should not stop at asking, “What is this service used for?” Identify the input the private subnet receives, the state or policy it manages, the failure modes it addresses, and the operational costs it introduces. At BlueRetail, the network is expanding across multiple AZs, but address ranges overlap, and a public subnet is being defined according to whether an instance has a public IP. Connecting the private subnet to the route boundary clarifies where responsibility boundaries lie and why two similarly named options can produce different outcomes. A good solution must trace the technical choice back to latency, availability, security, data, or the operations team's capabilities.
saa-14-vpc-addressing-subnets./sections/1/paragraphs/3: The route boundary is the fourth component in a VPC, CIDR, and subnet design decision, but its value becomes clear only when considered alongside business requirements. For SAA-C03, learners should not stop at asking, “What is this service used for?” Identify the input the route boundary receives, the state or policy it manages, the failure modes it addresses, and the operational costs it introduces. At BlueRetail, the network is expanding across multiple AZs, but address ranges overlap, and a public subnet is being defined according to whether an instance has a public IP. Connecting the route boundary to the CIDR plan clarifies where responsibility boundaries lie and why two similarly named options can produce different outcomes. A good solution must trace the technical choice back to latency, availability, security, data, or the operations team's capabilities.
saa-14-vpc-addressing-subnets./sections/2/title: Make decisions step by step and document trade-offs
saa-14-vpc-addressing-subnets./sections/2/paragraphs/0: A decision involving the CIDR plan should be documented as a chain of reasoning, not as a standalone service name. First, confirm which requirements are mandatory, then examine quota limits, Region, consistency, network path, and the responsibility model. Next, compare the CIDR plan with the private subnet using the same criteria; do not compare one feature of option A with the total cost of option B. Finally, state the remaining trade-off and how it will be observed after deployment. In Lesson 14, this approach helps BlueRetail understand why the selected option is good enough in the current context and which signals would trigger an architectural review.
saa-14-vpc-addressing-subnets./sections/2/paragraphs/1: A decision involving the public subnet should be documented as a chain of reasoning, not as a standalone service name. First, confirm which requirements are mandatory, then examine quota limits, Region, consistency, network path, and the responsibility model. Next, compare the public subnet with the route boundary using the same criteria; do not compare one feature of option A with the total cost of option B. Finally, state the remaining trade-off and how it will be observed after deployment. In Lesson 14, this approach helps BlueRetail understand why the selected option is good enough in the current context and which signals would trigger an architectural review.
saa-14-vpc-addressing-subnets./sections/2/paragraphs/2: A decision involving the private subnet should be documented as a chain of reasoning, not as a standalone service name. First, confirm which requirements are mandatory, then examine quota limits, Region, consistency, network path, and the responsibility model. Next, compare the private subnet with the CIDR plan using the same criteria; do not compare one feature of option A with the total cost of option B. Finally, state the remaining trade-off and how it will be observed after deployment. In Lesson 14, this approach helps BlueRetail understand why the selected option is good enough in the current context and which signals would trigger an architectural review.
saa-14-vpc-addressing-subnets./sections/2/paragraphs/3: A decision involving the route boundary should be documented as a chain of reasoning, not as a standalone service name. First, confirm which requirements are mandatory, then examine quota limits, Region, consistency, network path, and the responsibility model. Next, compare the route boundary with the public subnet using the same criteria; do not compare one feature of option A with the total cost of option B. Finally, state the remaining trade-off and how it will be observed after deployment. In Lesson 14, this approach helps BlueRetail understand why the selected option is good enough in the current context and which signals would trigger an architectural review.
saa-14-vpc-addressing-subnets./sections/3/title: Design for failures before they occur
saa-14-vpc-addressing-subnets./sections/3/paragraphs/0: Failure mode 1 in VPC, CIDR, and subnet design occurs when the project team assumes that the CIDR plan automatically addresses every related layer. In practice, service availability does not replace correct access permissions, replication does not replace backups, encryption does not automatically grant permission to use a key, and autoscaling does not fix a stateful dependency. At BlueRetail, map the blast radius of the CIDR plan, marking what AWS is responsible for and what the application team must still configure or test. Then select a metric, log, or event that can provide an early warning. Analyzing failure modes first ensures that the answer is not only correct when the system is healthy, but also provides a recovery path when an AZ, connection, credential, or operational process fails.
saa-14-vpc-addressing-subnets./sections/3/paragraphs/1: Failure mode 2 in VPC, CIDR, and subnet design occurs when the project team assumes that the public subnet automatically addresses every related layer. In practice, service availability does not replace correct access permissions, replication does not replace backups, encryption does not automatically grant permission to use a key, and autoscaling does not fix a stateful dependency. At BlueRetail, map the blast radius of the public subnet, marking what AWS is responsible for and what the application team must still configure or test. Then select a metric, log, or event that can provide an early warning. Analyzing failure modes first ensures that the answer is not only correct when the system is healthy, but also provides a recovery path when an AZ, connection, credential, or operational process fails.
saa-14-vpc-addressing-subnets./sections/3/paragraphs/2: Failure mode 3 in VPC, CIDR, and subnet design occurs when the project team assumes that the private subnet automatically addresses every related layer. In practice, service availability does not replace correct access permissions, replication does not replace backups, encryption does not automatically grant permission to use a key, and autoscaling does not fix a stateful dependency. At BlueRetail, map the blast radius of the private subnet, marking what AWS is responsible for and what the application team must still configure or test. Then select a metric, log, or event that can provide an early warning. Analyzing failure modes first ensures that the answer is not only correct when the system is healthy, but also provides a recovery path when an AZ, connection, credential, or operational process fails.
saa-14-vpc-addressing-subnets./sections/4/title: Apply the decision to the BlueRetail case
saa-14-vpc-addressing-subnets./sections/4/paragraphs/0: BlueRetail has stores, an e-commerce website, internal applications, and an operations team working across multiple accounts. In Lesson 14, the network is expanding across multiple AZs, but address ranges overlap, and a public subnet is being defined according to whether an instance has a public IP. The first step is to separate the stakeholders: users need a stable experience, security needs boundaries and an audit trail, finance needs cost attribution, and the product team needs to make changes quickly. A design optimized for only one stakeholder creates operational debt for another.
saa-14-vpc-addressing-subnets./sections/4/paragraphs/1: The architecture team documents three options centered on the CIDR plan, public subnet, and private subnet. Each option is scored using the same matrix: fulfillment of mandatory requirements, blast radius, recovery path, operational burden, scalability, and cost per unit of usage. The route boundary serves as the final validation condition. If an option wins only because the team is familiar with it, the decision lacks sufficient evidence and the constraints must be revisited.
saa-14-vpc-addressing-subnets./sections/4/paragraphs/2: The conclusion for VPC, CIDR, and subnet design is written in four sentences: which structure is selected; which requirements it fulfills; which trade-off is accepted; and which metric or review will trigger reevaluation. This format helps developers, security, and operations understand the same decision. In exam questions, these four sentences also help eliminate distractors: an option that fails a mandatory requirement is rejected first, even if it offers many attractive features.
saa-14-vpc-addressing-subnets./sections/5/title: Choosing an action in SAA-C03 questions
saa-14-vpc-addressing-subnets./sections/5/paragraphs/0: Read an SAA-C03 question in this order: the assigned role, current state, mandatory outcome, constraints, and the verb in the final sentence. For VPC, CIDR, and subnet design, mark every detail related to the CIDR plan and public subnet. Then classify the answers as fully meeting the requirements, partially meeting them, introducing additional risk, or being out of scope. Do not choose an answer merely because it mentions the correct service; AWS tests your ability to combine multiple requirements into the most appropriate solution.
saa-14-vpc-addressing-subnets./sections/5/paragraphs/1: Absolute phrases such as “no data loss,” “no downtime,” or “all traffic must remain private” require careful examination. They may require a completely different design from phrases such as “minimize,” “acceptable,” or “prioritize cost.” In Lesson 14, use the private subnet to evaluate the assurance level and the route boundary to determine whether the operations team can maintain the option. An answer that has the right feature but the wrong assurance level is still a distractor.
saa-14-vpc-addressing-subnets./sections/5/paragraphs/2: In Lesson 14—VPC, CIDR, and subnet design—objective SAA-C03 3.4 indicates the required depth. For design, you must balance quality attributes; for determine, you must interpret the facts and select a cause or option; for improve, you must preserve existing constraints while reducing risk through an appropriate change. End each answer with evidence: a metric, log, policy evaluation, recovery test, or cost report. Evidence turns an answer from a guess into a defensible architectural decision.
saa-14-vpc-addressing-subnets./visuals/0/title: Decision map: VPC, CIDR, and subnet design
saa-14-vpc-addressing-subnets./visuals/0/caption: An original diagram connecting requirements to the CIDR plan, public subnet, private subnet, and route boundary in the BlueRetail case.
saa-14-vpc-addressing-subnets./decisionWalkthroughs/0/title: Analyzing the VPC, CIDR, and subnet design decision
saa-14-vpc-addressing-subnets./decisionWalkthroughs/0/businessRequirem: BlueRetail must address the scenario in which “the network is expanding across multiple AZs, but address ranges overlap, and a public subnet is being defined according to whether an instance has a public IP,” while still being able to explain the effects on security, resilience, performance, and cost.
saa-14-vpc-addressing-subnets./decisionWalkthroughs/0/technicalConstra: The design must clarify the boundary of the CIDR plan and the responsibility of the public subnet.
The option must include a failure mode and recovery path related to the private subnet.
The operations team must use real data to observe and reevaluate the route boundary.
saa-14-vpc-addressing-subnets./decisionWalkthroughs/0/candidates/0/fit: A good fit when the requirement prioritizes the CIDR plan perspective in step 1.
saa-14-vpc-addressing-subnets./decisionWalkthroughs/0/candidates/0/lim: Does not address the public subnet by itself; remaining responsibilities must be examined.
saa-14-vpc-addressing-subnets./decisionWalkthroughs/0/candidates/1/fit: A good fit when the requirement prioritizes the public subnet perspective in step 2.
saa-14-vpc-addressing-subnets./decisionWalkthroughs/0/candidates/1/lim: Does not address the private subnet by itself; remaining responsibilities must be examined.
saa-14-vpc-addressing-subnets./decisionWalkthroughs/0/candidates/2/fit: A good fit when the requirement prioritizes the private subnet perspective in step 3.
saa-14-vpc-addressing-subnets./decisionWalkthroughs/0/candidates/2/lim: Does not address the route boundary by itself; remaining responsibilities must be examined.
saa-14-vpc-addressing-subnets./decisionWalkthroughs/0/decision: Select an option that combines the CIDR plan with the public subnet, uses the private subnet as a guardrail, and uses the route boundary as operational evidence.
saa-14-vpc-addressing-subnets./decisionWalkthroughs/0/reasoning/0: Eliminate options that fail mandatory requirements before comparing costs.
saa-14-vpc-addressing-subnets./decisionWalkthroughs/0/reasoning/1: Compare the options using the same quality attributes and time horizon.
saa-14-vpc-addressing-subnets./decisionWalkthroughs/0/reasoning/2: Clearly document the trade-off, blast radius, and responsibilities that remain with the application team.
saa-14-vpc-addressing-subnets./decisionWalkthroughs/0/reasoning/3: Identify the metric or review that would trigger reevaluation of the decision.
saa-14-vpc-addressing-subnets./comparison/0/bestFit: Prioritize the CIDR plan when the primary requirement is associated with step 1 of the decision.
saa-14-vpc-addressing-subnets./comparison/0/blueRetailUse: BlueRetail uses the CIDR plan to address a situation in which the network is expanding across multiple AZs, but address ranges overlap, and a public subnet is being defined according to whether an instance has a public IP.
saa-14-vpc-addressing-subnets./comparison/0/tradeOff: The public subnet must be added or examined; do not treat this as a solution for every layer.
saa-14-vpc-addressing-subnets./comparison/1/bestFit: Prioritize the public subnet when the primary requirement is associated with step 2 of the decision.
saa-14-vpc-addressing-subnets./comparison/1/blueRetailUse: BlueRetail uses the public subnet to address a situation in which the network is expanding across multiple AZs, but address ranges overlap, and a public subnet is being defined according to whether an instance has a public IP.
saa-14-vpc-addressing-subnets./comparison/1/tradeOff: The private subnet must be added or examined; do not treat this as a solution for every layer.
saa-14-vpc-addressing-subnets./comparison/2/bestFit: Prioritize the private subnet when the primary requirement is associated with step 3 of the decision.
saa-14-vpc-addressing-subnets./comparison/2/blueRetailUse: BlueRetail uses the private subnet to address a situation in which the network is expanding across multiple AZs, but address ranges overlap, and a public subnet is being defined according to whether an instance has a public IP.
saa-14-vpc-addressing-subnets./comparison/2/tradeOff: The route boundary must be added or examined; do not treat this as a solution for every layer.
saa-14-vpc-addressing-subnets./commonMistakes/0/wrong: Seeing the keyword CIDR plan and immediately choosing the answer with the same service or feature name.
saa-14-vpc-addressing-subnets./commonMistakes/0/better: Evaluate the CIDR plan against the requirements, constraints, and public subnet before choosing.
saa-14-vpc-addressing-subnets./commonMistakes/0/reason: In Lesson 14, a choice is correct only when the entire chain of responsibilities and failure modes is addressed.
