saa-31-rds-engine-selection./title: RDS engines and relational workload models
saa-31-rds-engine-selection./shortTitle: RDS engines and relational workload models
saa-31-rds-engine-selection./summary: Understand RDS engines and relational workload models through requirements, constraints, trade-offs, and an architectural decision that can be explained to the BlueRetail team.
saa-31-rds-engine-selection./opening/0: BlueRetail is changing its architecture, and its order system requires transactions and SQL, but the team wants to reduce patching, backup, and failover work. A diagram with many AWS icons is not necessarily a good design: what matters is which requirement each component addresses, which failure modes remain, and whether the operations team can manage it effectively. Lesson 31 begins with this scenario and turns RDS engines and relational workload models into a sequence of evidence-based decisions rather than a list of features to memorize.
saa-31-rds-engine-selection./opening/1: In SAA-C03, questions often present several technically viable options, but only one is the best fit for keywords such as “least operational effort,” “no data loss,” “low cost,” “multiple accounts,” or “no public internet.” RDS engines and relational workload models should be evaluated from four perspectives: relational model, managed database, connection pattern, and engine compatibility. Each perspective eliminates a group of answers if they conflict with the requirements or introduce unacceptable responsibilities.
saa-31-rds-engine-selection./opening/2: The scope of Lesson 31—RDS engines and relational workload models—is aligned with objective SAA-C03 3.3 in the current AWS Exam Guide. The GitHub topic repository is used only to verify coverage and content organization; all explanations, cases, and diagrams here are newly written, while the technical conclusions are validated against AWS Documentation and the Well-Architected Framework. After this lesson, you should be able to explain not only “what to choose,” but also “why the other options were not chosen.”
saa-31-rds-engine-selection./sections/0/title: Mental model: start with requirements, not services
saa-31-rds-engine-selection./sections/0/paragraphs/0: Treat an AWS architecture as a contract among business, application, and platform teams. The business defines what must remain operational and what level of loss is acceptable; the application defines traffic, data, and dependencies; and the platform translates those requirements into boundaries, services, policies, and observability mechanisms. For RDS engines and relational workload models, the relational model is the entry point, the managed database perspective explains how the system responds, and the connection pattern and engine compatibility determine long-term operability. Moving directly from a requirement to a service name often causes one of these four layers to be overlooked.
saa-31-rds-engine-selection./sections/0/paragraphs/1: A compact model that works for every lesson is R-C-D-E: Requirement, Constraint, Decision, Evidence. A requirement describes the outcome; a constraint limits the scope through factors such as Region, budget, or skills; a decision is the selected architectural structure; and evidence is a metric, log, test, or document confirming that the decision remains valid. Applying R-C-D-E to RDS engines and relational workload models shows learners that the same service can be correct for one workload and wrong for another. This skill is more important than memorizing the entire AWS catalog.
saa-31-rds-engine-selection./sections/0/paragraphs/2: You have understood Lesson 31 when you can trace a request or operational event from beginning to end. Which boundary does the request cross, which identity is evaluated, where is the data retained, which component can fail, and which mechanism provides recovery? If the story breaks at the relational model or managed database layer, the diagram still contains an unverified assumption. Record that assumption beside the diagram before selecting another service.
saa-31-rds-engine-selection./sections/1/title: Four concepts that must be connected
saa-31-rds-engine-selection./sections/1/paragraphs/0: The relational model is the first element in decisions about RDS engines and relational workload models, but its value becomes clear only when considered alongside business requirements. For SAA-C03, learners should not stop at “What is this service used for?” Identify the input received by the relational model, the state or policy it manages, the failure mode it addresses, and the operational cost it introduces. At BlueRetail, the order system requires transactions and SQL, but the team wants to reduce patching, backup, and failover work. Connecting the relational model to the managed database perspective reveals where the responsibility boundary lies and why two similarly named options can produce different outcomes. A good solution must be traceable from the technical choice back to latency, availability, security, data, or the capabilities of the operations team.
saa-31-rds-engine-selection./sections/1/paragraphs/1: The managed database perspective is the second element in decisions about RDS engines and relational workload models, but its value becomes clear only when considered alongside business requirements. For SAA-C03, learners should not stop at “What is this service used for?” Identify the input received by the managed database, the state or policy it manages, the failure mode it addresses, and the operational cost it introduces. At BlueRetail, the order system requires transactions and SQL, but the team wants to reduce patching, backup, and failover work. Connecting the managed database perspective to the connection pattern reveals where the responsibility boundary lies and why two similarly named options can produce different outcomes. A good solution must be traceable from the technical choice back to latency, availability, security, data, or the capabilities of the operations team.
saa-31-rds-engine-selection./sections/1/paragraphs/2: The connection pattern is the third element in decisions about RDS engines and relational workload models, but its value becomes clear only when considered alongside business requirements. For SAA-C03, learners should not stop at “What is this service used for?” Identify the input received by the connection pattern, the state or policy it manages, the failure mode it addresses, and the operational cost it introduces. At BlueRetail, the order system requires transactions and SQL, but the team wants to reduce patching, backup, and failover work. Connecting the connection pattern to engine compatibility reveals where the responsibility boundary lies and why two similarly named options can produce different outcomes. A good solution must be traceable from the technical choice back to latency, availability, security, data, or the capabilities of the operations team.
saa-31-rds-engine-selection./sections/1/paragraphs/3: Engine compatibility is the fourth element in decisions about RDS engines and relational workload models, but its value becomes clear only when considered alongside business requirements. For SAA-C03, learners should not stop at “What is this service used for?” Identify the input received by engine compatibility, the state or policy it manages, the failure mode it addresses, and the operational cost it introduces. At BlueRetail, the order system requires transactions and SQL, but the team wants to reduce patching, backup, and failover work. Connecting engine compatibility to the relational model reveals where the responsibility boundary lies and why two similarly named options can produce different outcomes. A good solution must be traceable from the technical choice back to latency, availability, security, data, or the capabilities of the operations team.
saa-31-rds-engine-selection./sections/2/title: Make decisions step by step and document the trade-offs
saa-31-rds-engine-selection./sections/2/paragraphs/0: A decision involving the relational model should be documented as a chain of reasoning, not as an isolated service name. First, confirm which requirements are mandatory, then check quota limits, Region, consistency, network path, and the responsibility model. Next, compare the relational model and connection pattern using the same criteria; do not compare one feature of option A with the total cost of option B. Finally, state the remaining trade-off and how it will be observed after deployment. In Lesson 31, this approach helps BlueRetail understand why the selected option is good enough in the current context and which signals would trigger an architectural review.
saa-31-rds-engine-selection./sections/2/paragraphs/1: A decision involving a managed database should be documented as a chain of reasoning, not as an isolated service name. First, confirm which requirements are mandatory, then check quota limits, Region, consistency, network path, and the responsibility model. Next, compare the managed database and engine compatibility using the same criteria; do not compare one feature of option A with the total cost of option B. Finally, state the remaining trade-off and how it will be observed after deployment. In Lesson 31, this approach helps BlueRetail understand why the selected option is good enough in the current context and which signals would trigger an architectural review.
saa-31-rds-engine-selection./sections/2/paragraphs/2: A decision involving a connection pattern should be documented as a chain of reasoning, not as an isolated service name. First, confirm which requirements are mandatory, then check quota limits, Region, consistency, network path, and the responsibility model. Next, compare the connection pattern and relational model using the same criteria; do not compare one feature of option A with the total cost of option B. Finally, state the remaining trade-off and how it will be observed after deployment. In Lesson 31, this approach helps BlueRetail understand why the selected option is good enough in the current context and which signals would trigger an architectural review.
saa-31-rds-engine-selection./sections/2/paragraphs/3: A decision involving engine compatibility should be documented as a chain of reasoning, not as an isolated service name. First, confirm which requirements are mandatory, then check quota limits, Region, consistency, network path, and the responsibility model. Next, compare engine compatibility and the managed database using the same criteria; do not compare one feature of option A with the total cost of option B. Finally, state the remaining trade-off and how it will be observed after deployment. In Lesson 31, this approach helps BlueRetail understand why the selected option is good enough in the current context and which signals would trigger an architectural review.
saa-31-rds-engine-selection./sections/3/title: Design for failure before it occurs
saa-31-rds-engine-selection./sections/3/paragraphs/0: Failure mode 1 for RDS engines and relational workload models occurs when the project team assumes that the relational model automatically addresses every related layer. In reality, service availability does not replace correct access permissions, replication does not replace backup, encryption does not automatically grant permission to use a key, and autoscaling cannot fix a stateful dependency. At BlueRetail, draw the blast radius of the relational model, marking the portion for which AWS is responsible and the portion that the application team must still configure or test. Then select a metric, log, or event that can provide an early warning. Analyzing the failure mode first ensures that the answer is valid not only when the system is healthy but also provides a recovery path when an AZ, connection, credential, or operational process fails.
saa-31-rds-engine-selection./sections/3/paragraphs/1: Failure mode 2 for RDS engines and relational workload models occurs when the project team assumes that the managed database automatically addresses every related layer. In reality, service availability does not replace correct access permissions, replication does not replace backup, encryption does not automatically grant permission to use a key, and autoscaling cannot fix a stateful dependency. At BlueRetail, draw the blast radius of the managed database, marking the portion for which AWS is responsible and the portion that the application team must still configure or test. Then select a metric, log, or event that can provide an early warning. Analyzing the failure mode first ensures that the answer is valid not only when the system is healthy but also provides a recovery path when an AZ, connection, credential, or operational process fails.
saa-31-rds-engine-selection./sections/3/paragraphs/2: Failure mode 3 for RDS engines and relational workload models occurs when the project team assumes that the connection pattern automatically addresses every related layer. In reality, service availability does not replace correct access permissions, replication does not replace backup, encryption does not automatically grant permission to use a key, and autoscaling cannot fix a stateful dependency. At BlueRetail, draw the blast radius of the connection pattern, marking the portion for which AWS is responsible and the portion that the application team must still configure or test. Then select a metric, log, or event that can provide an early warning. Analyzing the failure mode first ensures that the answer is valid not only when the system is healthy but also provides a recovery path when an AZ, connection, credential, or operational process fails.
saa-31-rds-engine-selection./sections/4/title: Apply the decision to the BlueRetail case
saa-31-rds-engine-selection./sections/4/paragraphs/0: BlueRetail has physical stores, an e-commerce website, internal applications, and an operations team working across multiple accounts. In Lesson 31, the order system requires transactions and SQL, but the team wants to reduce patching, backup, and failover work. The first step is to separate the stakeholders: users need a stable experience, security needs boundaries and an audit trail, finance needs cost attribution, and the product team needs to make changes quickly. A design optimized for only one stakeholder creates operational debt for another.
saa-31-rds-engine-selection./sections/4/paragraphs/1: The architecture team documents three options centered on the relational model, managed database, and connection pattern. Each option is scored using the same matrix: fulfillment of mandatory requirements, blast radius, recovery path, operational burden, scalability, and cost per unit of usage. Engine compatibility serves as the final validation condition. If an option wins only because the team is familiar with it, the decision lacks sufficient evidence and the constraints must be revisited.
saa-31-rds-engine-selection./sections/4/paragraphs/2: The conclusion for RDS engines and relational workload models is written in four sentences: which structure is selected; which requirements it satisfies; which trade-off is accepted; and which metric or review will trigger a reassessment. This format helps developers, security, and operations understand the same decision. In exam questions, these four sentences also help eliminate distractors: an option that does not meet a mandatory requirement is eliminated first, even if it offers many attractive features.
saa-31-rds-engine-selection./sections/5/title: Choosing the right action in SAA-C03 questions
saa-31-rds-engine-selection./sections/5/paragraphs/0: Read an SAA-C03 question in this order: the assigned role, current state, mandatory outcome, constraints, and the verb at the end of the question. For RDS engines and relational workload models, mark every detail related to the relational model and managed database. Then classify each answer as fully satisfying the requirements, partially satisfying them, introducing additional risk, or being out of scope. Do not select an answer merely because it mentions the correct service; AWS tests your ability to combine multiple requirements into the most appropriate solution.
saa-31-rds-engine-selection./sections/5/paragraphs/1: Absolute phrases such as “no data loss,” “no downtime,” or “all traffic must remain private” require careful examination. They can demand a substantially different design from phrases such as “minimize,” “acceptable,” or “prioritize cost.” In Lesson 31, use the connection pattern to evaluate the level of assurance and engine compatibility to determine whether the operations team can sustain the option. An answer with the correct feature but the wrong assurance level is still a distractor.
saa-31-rds-engine-selection./sections/5/paragraphs/2: In Lesson 31—RDS engines and relational workload models—objective SAA-C03 3.3 indicates the required depth. For design, you must balance quality attributes; for determine, you must interpret the facts and select a cause or option; for improve, you must preserve the existing constraints while reducing risk through an appropriate change. End each answer with evidence: a metric, log, policy evaluation, recovery test, or cost report. Evidence turns an answer from a guess into a defensible architectural decision.

saa-31-rds-engine-selection./activity/title: Architecture review: RDS engines and relational workload models
saa-31-rds-engine-selection./activity/context: BlueRetail is preparing to approve a change because the order system requires transactions and SQL, but the team wants to reduce patching, backup, and failover work. You must review the architecture before the team deploys it or incurs a cost commitment.
saa-31-rds-engine-selection./activity/learnerRole: You are the Solutions Architect responsible for a workload, translating requirements into a decision and clearly explaining the trade-offs to stakeholders.
saa-31-rds-engine-selection./activity/currentArchitecture: Users → access layer → workload → data store; the review points are relational model → managed database → connection pattern → engine compatibility.
saa-31-rds-engine-selection./activity/requirements/0: The solution must correctly address the requirement related to the relational model.
saa-31-rds-engine-selection./activity/requirements/1: The recovery path must demonstrate the role of the managed database.
saa-31-rds-engine-selection./activity/requirements/2: Security and operations must be able to observe the connection pattern without relying on assumptions.
saa-31-rds-engine-selection./activity/constraints/0: Do not add a service merely to mask a requirement that has not been clearly defined.
saa-31-rds-engine-selection./activity/constraints/1: Stakeholders must accept the engine compatibility trade-off.
saa-31-rds-engine-selection./activity/constraints/2: Do not change the storage schema or account boundary without an architectural reason.
saa-31-rds-engine-selection./activity/tasks/0: Rewrite the requirement as measurable criteria.
saa-31-rds-engine-selection./activity/tasks/1: Compare at least two options based on the relational model, managed database, and connection pattern.
saa-31-rds-engine-selection./activity/tasks/2: Select an option, document the trade-off, and propose post-deployment evidence.
saa-31-rds-engine-selection./activity/reasoningSteps/0: Separate the business outcome, technical constraints, and unconfirmed assumptions.
saa-31-rds-engine-selection./activity/reasoningSteps/1: Draw the request and data flow, including trust boundaries and blast radius.

saa-31-rds-engine-selection./sources/2/label: GitHub topic inventory used to verify coverage—Lesson 31

saa-32-rds-multiaz-read-replica./summary: Understand RDS Multi-AZ and read replicas through requirements, constraints, trade-offs, and an architectural decision that can be explained to the BlueRetail team.
saa-32-rds-multiaz-read-replica./opening/0: BlueRetail is changing its architecture, and the team is using a read replica to claim high availability even though the application has no failover mechanism. A diagram with many AWS icons is not necessarily a good design. What matters is which requirement each component addresses, which failure modes remain, and whether the operations team can manage it. Lesson 32 starts with this scenario and turns RDS Multi-AZ and read replicas into a sequence of evidence-based decisions rather than a list of features to memorize.
saa-32-rds-multiaz-read-replica./opening/1: In SAA-C03, questions often present several technically feasible options, but only one best matches keywords such as “least operational effort,” “no data loss,” “low cost,” “multiple accounts,” or “no public internet.” RDS Multi-AZ and read replicas should be evaluated from four perspectives: synchronous standby, read replica, failover endpoint, and replication lag. Each perspective eliminates a group of answers that conflict with the requirements or introduce unacceptable responsibilities.
saa-32-rds-multiaz-read-replica./opening/2: The scope of Lesson 32 — RDS Multi-AZ and read replicas is aligned with objective SAA-C03 2.2 in the current AWS Exam Guide. The GitHub topic repository is used only to verify coverage and content organization. All explanations, cases, and diagrams here are original, while the technical conclusions have been validated against AWS Documentation and the Well-Architected Framework. After this lesson, you should be able to explain not only what to choose, but also why the other options should not be chosen.
saa-32-rds-multiaz-read-replica./sections/0/title: Mental model: start with requirements, not services
saa-32-rds-multiaz-read-replica./sections/0/paragraphs/0: Think of an AWS architecture as a contract among the business, application, and platform. The business defines what must remain operational and what level of loss is acceptable; the application defines traffic, data, and dependencies; and the platform translates those requirements into boundaries, services, policies, and observability mechanisms. For RDS Multi-AZ and read replicas, synchronous standby is the entry point, read replica shows how the system responds, and the failover endpoint and replication lag determine long-term operability. Moving directly from a requirement to a service name often causes one of these four layers to be overlooked.
saa-32-rds-multiaz-read-replica./sections/0/paragraphs/1: A compact model that works for every lesson is R-C-D-E: Requirement, Constraint, Decision, Evidence. A requirement describes the outcome; a constraint limits the scope through factors such as Region, budget, or skills; a decision is the selected architectural structure; and evidence consists of metrics, logs, tests, or documentation confirming that the decision remains valid. By applying R-C-D-E to RDS Multi-AZ and read replicas, learners can see that the same service may be appropriate for one workload but unsuitable for another. This skill is more important than memorizing the entire AWS catalog.
saa-32-rds-multiaz-read-replica./sections/0/paragraphs/2: You have understood Lesson 32 when you can trace a request or operational event from beginning to end. Which boundary does the request cross, which identity is evaluated, where is the data stored, which component can fail, and which mechanism provides recovery? If the story breaks at the synchronous standby or read replica, the diagram still contains an unverified assumption. Record that assumption beside the diagram before selecting another service.
saa-32-rds-multiaz-read-replica./sections/1/title: Four concepts that must be connected
saa-32-rds-multiaz-read-replica./sections/1/paragraphs/0: synchronous standby is the first building block in an RDS Multi-AZ and read replica decision, but its value becomes clear only when considered alongside the business requirements. For SAA-C03, learners should not stop at asking, “What is this service used for?” Identify the input that synchronous standby receives, the state or policy it manages, the failure mode it addresses, and the resulting operational cost. At BlueRetail, the team is using a read replica to claim high availability even though the application has no failover mechanism. Connecting synchronous standby with read replica reveals where the responsibility boundary lies and why two similarly named options produce different outcomes. A strong solution must trace the technical choice back to latency, availability, security, data, or the capabilities of the operations team.

saa-32-rds-multiaz-read-replica./sections/2/title: Make decisions step by step and document the trade-offs
saa-32-rds-multiaz-read-replica./sections/2/paragraphs/0: A decision involving synchronous standby should be documented as a chain of reasoning, not as a standalone service name. First, confirm which requirements must be satisfied. Then check quota limits, Region, consistency, network path, and the responsibility model. Next, compare synchronous standby with failover endpoint using the same criteria; do not compare one feature of option A with the total cost of option B. Finally, state the remaining trade-off and how it will be observed after deployment. In Lesson 32, this approach helps BlueRetail understand why the selected option is good enough in the current context and which signals would require the architecture to be reassessed.
saa-32-rds-multiaz-read-replica./sections/2/paragraphs/1: A decision involving read replica should be documented as a chain of reasoning, not as a standalone service name. First, confirm which requirements must be satisfied. Then check quota limits, Region, consistency, network path, and the responsibility model. Next, compare read replica with replication lag using the same criteria; do not compare one feature of option A with the total cost of option B. Finally, state the remaining trade-off and how it will be observed after deployment. Next, compare failover endpoint with synchronous standby using the same criteria; do not compare one feature of option A with the total cost of option B. saa-32-rds-multiaz-read-replica./sections/3/title: Design for failure before it occurs
saa-32-rds-multiaz-read-replica./sections/3/paragraphs/0: Failure mode 1 for RDS Multi-AZ and read replicas occurs when the project team assumes that synchronous standby automatically addresses every related layer. In reality, service availability does not replace correct access permissions, replication does not replace backups, encryption does not automatically grant permission to use a key, and autoscaling cannot fix a stateful dependency. At BlueRetail, map the blast radius of synchronous standby and mark which parts are the responsibility of AWS and which parts the application team must still configure or test. Then select a metric, log, or event that can provide an early warning. Analyzing the failure mode first ensures that the answer is valid not only while the system is healthy, but also provides a recovery path when an AZ, connection, credential, or operational process fails.
saa-32-rds-multiaz-read-replica./sections/3/paragraphs/1: Failure mode 2 for RDS Multi-AZ and read replicas occurs when the project team assumes that read replica automatically addresses every related layer. In reality, service availability does not replace correct access permissions, replication does not replace backups, encryption does not automatically grant permission to use a key, and autoscaling cannot fix a stateful dependency. At BlueRetail, map the blast radius of read replica and mark which parts are the responsibility of AWS and which parts the application team must still configure or test. Then select a metric, log, or event that can provide an early warning. Analyzing the failure mode first ensures that the answer is valid not only while the system is healthy, but also provides a recovery path when an AZ, connection, credential, or operational process fails.
saa-32-rds-multiaz-read-replica./sections/3/paragraphs/2: Failure mode 3 for RDS Multi-AZ and read replicas occurs when the project team assumes that failover endpoint automatically addresses every related layer. In reality, service availability does not replace correct access permissions, replication does not replace backups, encryption does not automatically grant permission to use a key, and autoscaling cannot fix a stateful dependency. At BlueRetail, map the blast radius of failover endpoint and mark which parts are the responsibility of AWS and which parts the application team must still configure or test. Then select a metric, log, or event that can provide an early warning. Analyzing the failure mode first ensures that the answer is valid not only while the system is healthy, but also provides a recovery path when an AZ, connection, credential, or operational process fails.
saa-32-rds-multiaz-read-replica./sections/4/title: Apply the decision to the BlueRetail case
saa-32-rds-multiaz-read-replica./sections/4/paragraphs/0: BlueRetail has stores, an e-commerce website, internal applications, and an operations team working across multiple accounts. In Lesson 32, the team is using a read replica to claim high availability even though the application has no failover mechanism. The first step is to separate the stakeholders: users need a stable experience, security needs boundaries and an audit trail, finance needs cost attribution, and the product team needs to make changes quickly. A design optimized for only one stakeholder creates operational debt for another.
saa-32-rds-multiaz-read-replica./sections/4/paragraphs/1: The architecture team documents three options centered on synchronous standby, read replica, and failover endpoint. Each option is evaluated using the same scorecard: mandatory requirement coverage, blast radius, recovery path, operational burden, scalability, and cost per unit of usage. replication lag serves as the final validation criterion. If an option wins only because the team is familiar with it, the decision lacks sufficient evidence and the constraints must be revisited.
saa-32-rds-multiaz-read-replica./sections/4/paragraphs/2: The conclusion for RDS Multi-AZ and read replicas is written in four sentences: which structure is selected; which requirements it satisfies; which trade-off is accepted; and which metric or review will trigger a reassessment. This format helps developers, security, and operations understand the same decision. In exam questions, the four sentences also help eliminate distractors: an option that does not meet a mandatory requirement is eliminated first, even if it offers many attractive features.
saa-32-rds-multiaz-read-replica./sections/5/title: Choosing the correct action in SAA-C03 questions
saa-32-rds-multiaz-read-replica./sections/5/paragraphs/0: Read an SAA-C03 question in this order: the assigned role, the current state, the required outcome, the constraints, and the verb at the end of the question. For RDS Multi-AZ and read replicas, mark every detail related to synchronous standby and read replica. Then classify each answer as fully satisfying the requirements, partially satisfying them, introducing additional risk, or being out of scope. Do not choose an answer merely because it mentions the correct service; AWS tests your ability to combine multiple requirements into the most appropriate solution.
saa-32-rds-multiaz-read-replica./sections/5/paragraphs/1: Absolute phrases such as “no data loss,” “no downtime,” or “all traffic must remain private” require careful scrutiny. They may require a very different design from phrases such as “minimize,” “acceptable,” or “prioritize cost.” In Lesson 32, use failover endpoint to evaluate the level of assurance and replication lag to determine whether the operations team can sustain the option. An answer that is correct in terms of features but provides the wrong level of assurance is still a distractor.
saa-32-rds-multiaz-read-replica./sections/5/paragraphs/2: In Lesson 32 — RDS Multi-AZ and read replicas, objective SAA-C03 2.2 indicates the required depth of understanding. With design, you must balance quality attributes; with determine, you must interpret the facts and select a cause or option; with improve, you must preserve existing constraints while reducing risk through an appropriate change. End each answer with evidence: a metric, log, policy evaluation, recovery test, or cost report. Evidence turns an answer from a guess into a defensible architectural decision.

saa-32-rds-multiaz-read-replica./activity/title: Architecture review: RDS Multi-AZ and read replicas
saa-32-rds-multiaz-read-replica./activity/context: BlueRetail is preparing to approve a change because the team is using a read replica to claim high availability even though the application has no failover mechanism. You must review the architecture before the team deploys it or incurs a cost commitment.
saa-32-rds-multiaz-read-replica./activity/learnerRole: You are the Solutions Architect responsible for a workload, translating requirements into decisions and clearly communicating trade-offs to stakeholders.
saa-32-rds-multiaz-read-replica./activity/currentArchitecture: Users → access layer → workload → data store; the review points are synchronous standby → read replica → failover endpoint → replication lag.
saa-32-rds-multiaz-read-replica./activity/requirements/0: The solution must correctly address the requirement related to synchronous standby.
saa-32-rds-multiaz-read-replica./activity/requirements/1: The recovery path must show the role of read replica.
saa-32-rds-multiaz-read-replica./activity/requirements/2: Security and operations must be able to observe failover endpoint without relying on assumptions.
saa-32-rds-multiaz-read-replica./activity/constraints/0: Do not add a service merely to conceal a requirement that has not been clearly defined.
saa-32-rds-multiaz-read-replica./activity/constraints/1: The replication lag trade-off must be accepted by the stakeholders.
saa-32-rds-multiaz-read-replica./activity/constraints/2: Do not change the storage schema or account boundary without an architectural justification.
saa-32-rds-multiaz-read-replica./activity/tasks/0: Rewrite the requirement as measurable criteria.
saa-32-rds-multiaz-read-replica./activity/tasks/1: Compare at least two options based on synchronous standby, read replica, and failover endpoint.
saa-32-rds-multiaz-read-replica./activity/tasks/2: Select an option, document the trade-off, and propose post-deployment evidence.
saa-32-rds-multiaz-read-replica./activity/reasoningSteps/0: Separate the business outcome, technical constraints, and unverified assumptions.
saa-32-rds-multiaz-read-replica./activity/reasoningSteps/1: Map the request and data flow, including trust boundaries and blast radius.
saa-32-rds-multiaz-read-replica./activity/reasoningSteps/2: Eliminate options that do not meet mandatory requirements before optimizing.
saa-32-rds-multiaz-read-replica./activity/reasoningSteps/3: Compare the remaining options across reliability, security, performance, cost, and operations.
saa-32-rds-multiaz-read-replica./activity/reasoningSteps/4: Document the decision, trade-off, recovery path, and review metric.
saa-32-rds-multiaz-read-replica./activity/deliverables/0: An architecture diagram showing boundaries and data flow direction.
saa-32-rds-multiaz-read-replica./activity/deliverables/1: A decision matrix comparing at least two options.
saa-32-rds-multiaz-read-replica./activity/deliverables/2: A concise architecture decision record containing the trade-off and evidence.
saa-32-rds-multiaz-read-replica./activity/rubric/0/evidence: The decision can be traced back to synchronous standby and introduces no assumptions beyond the scenario.
saa-32-rds-multiaz-read-replica./activity/rubric/0/importance: Prevents services from being selected based on preference or an isolated keyword.
saa-32-rds-multiaz-read-replica./activity/rubric/1/evidence: Compares read replica, failover endpoint, and replication lag using the same criteria.
saa-32-rds-multiaz-read-replica./activity/rubric/1/importance: AWS architecture always involves balance; no option is universally best.
saa-32-rds-multiaz-read-replica./activity/rubric/2/evidence: The submission identifies a failure mode, recovery path, and validation metric or test.
saa-32-rds-multiaz-read-replica./activity/rubric/2/importance: A design is trustworthy only if it can be operated and tested after deployment.
saa-32-rds-multiaz-read-replica./activity/sampleAnalysis/0: The primary requirement is rewritten around synchronous standby; read replica is a constraint that directly affects the choice.
saa-32-rds-multiaz-read-replica./activity/sampleAnalysis/1: The option that prioritizes failover endpoint is eliminated if it increases the operational burden associated with replication lag without adding value.
saa-32-rds-multiaz-read-replica./activity/sampleAnalysis/2: The proposed architecture combines synchronous standby and read replica, with the blast radius, recovery path, and review metric clearly documented.
saa-32-rds-multiaz-read-replica./activity/sampleAnalysis/3: The remaining trade-off is accepted because it fits the situation in which the team is using a read replica to claim high availability even though the application has no failover mechanism.
saa-32-rds-multiaz-read-replica./activity/commonMistakes/0: Listing services without connecting them to requirements.
saa-32-rds-multiaz-read-replica./activity/commonMistakes/1: Describing only the happy path and ignoring failure modes.
saa-32-rds-multiaz-read-replica./activity/commonMistakes/2: Saying “AWS manages it” without identifying the customer's responsibilities.
saa-32-rds-multiaz-read-replica./activity/reflectionQuestions/0: Which detail in the scenario eliminated the most options?
saa-32-rds-multiaz-read-replica./activity/reflectionQuestions/1: Which metric or event would cause you to reassess the decision?

saa-33-aurora-architecture./title: Aurora clusters, replicas, and global databases
saa-33-aurora-architecture./shortTitle: Aurora clusters, replicas, and global databases
saa-33-aurora-architecture./summary: Understand Aurora clusters, replicas, and global databases through requirements, constraints, trade-offs, and an architectural decision that can be explained to the BlueRetail team.
saa-33-aurora-architecture./opening/0: BlueRetail is changing its architecture, and its website must scale reads within the Region and reduce RPO when expanding into a second market. A diagram with many AWS icons is not necessarily a good design. What matters is which requirement each component addresses, which failure modes remain, and whether the operations team can manage it. Lesson 33 begins with this scenario and turns Aurora clusters, replicas, and global databases into a sequence of evidence-based decisions rather than a list of features to memorize.
saa-33-aurora-architecture./opening/1: In SAA-C03, questions often present several technically viable options, but only one best matches keywords such as “least operational effort,” “no data loss,” “low cost,” “multiple accounts,” or “no public internet.” Aurora clusters, replicas, and global databases should be evaluated from four perspectives: cluster volume, reader endpoint, Aurora replica, and global database. Each perspective eliminates a group of answers if they conflict with the requirements or introduce unacceptable responsibilities.
saa-33-aurora-architecture./opening/2: The scope of Lesson 33—Aurora clusters, replicas, and global databases—is aligned with objective SAA-C03 3.3 in the current AWS Exam Guide. The GitHub topic repository is used only to verify coverage and content organization. All explanations, cases, and diagrams here are original, while the technical conclusions are validated against AWS Documentation and the Well-Architected Framework. After this lesson, you should be able to explain not only what to choose, but also why the other options should not be chosen.
saa-33-aurora-architecture./sections/0/title: Mental model: start with requirements, not services
saa-33-aurora-architecture./sections/0/paragraphs/0: Treat an AWS architecture as a contract among the business, application, and platform. The business defines what must remain operational and the acceptable level of loss. The application describes traffic, data, and dependencies. The platform translates those requirements into boundaries, services, policies, and observability mechanisms. For Aurora clusters, replicas, and global databases, the cluster volume is the entry point, the reader endpoint indicates how the system responds, and Aurora replicas and global databases determine long-term operability. Going directly from a requirement to a service name often causes one of these four layers to be overlooked.
saa-33-aurora-architecture./sections/0/paragraphs/1: A compact model that works for every lesson is R-C-D-E: Requirement, Constraint, Decision, Evidence. A requirement describes the outcome. A constraint limits the scope through factors such as Region, budget, or skills. A decision is the selected architectural structure. Evidence is a metric, log, test, or document confirming that the decision remains valid. Applying R-C-D-E to Aurora clusters, replicas, and global databases shows learners that the same service can be correct for one workload and wrong for another. This skill is more important than memorizing the entire AWS catalog.
saa-33-aurora-architecture./sections/0/paragraphs/2: You have understood Lesson 33 when you can trace a request or operational event from beginning to end. Which boundary does the request cross? Which identity is evaluated? Where is the data stored? Which component can fail, and what mechanism provides recovery? If the story breaks at the cluster volume or reader endpoint, the diagram still contains an unverified assumption. Record that assumption next to the diagram before selecting another service.
saa-33-aurora-architecture./sections/1/title: Four concepts that must be connected
saa-33-aurora-architecture./sections/1/paragraphs/0: The cluster volume is the 1st building block in decisions about Aurora clusters, replicas, and global databases, but its value becomes apparent only when considered alongside business requirements. For SAA-C03, learners should not stop at asking, “What is this service used for?” Identify the inputs received by the cluster volume, the state or policies it manages, the failure modes it addresses, and the operational costs it incurs. At BlueRetail, the website must scale reads within the Region and reduce RPO when expanding into a second market. Connecting the cluster volume to the reader endpoint reveals where the responsibility boundary lies and why two similarly named options can produce different outcomes. A good solution must be traceable from the technical choice back to latency, availability, security, data, or the operations team’s capabilities.
saa-33-aurora-architecture./sections/1/paragraphs/1: The reader endpoint is the 2nd building block in decisions about Aurora clusters, replicas, and global databases, but its value becomes apparent only when considered alongside business requirements. For SAA-C03, learners should not stop at asking, “What is this service used for?” Identify the inputs received by the reader endpoint, the state or policies it manages, the failure modes it addresses, and the operational costs it incurs. At BlueRetail, the website must scale reads within the Region and reduce RPO when expanding into a second market. Connecting the reader endpoint to the Aurora replica reveals where the responsibility boundary lies and why two similarly named options can produce different outcomes. A good solution must be traceable from the technical choice back to latency, availability, security, data, or the operations team’s capabilities.
saa-33-aurora-architecture./sections/1/paragraphs/2: The Aurora replica is the 3rd building block in decisions about Aurora clusters, replicas, and global databases, but its value becomes apparent only when considered alongside business requirements. For SAA-C03, learners should not stop at asking, “What is this service used for?” Identify the inputs received by the Aurora replica, the state or policies it manages, the failure modes it addresses, and the operational costs it incurs. At BlueRetail, the website must scale reads within the Region and reduce RPO when expanding into a second market. Connecting the Aurora replica to the global database reveals where the responsibility boundary lies and why two similarly named options can produce different outcomes. A good solution must be traceable from the technical choice back to latency, availability, security, data, or the operations team’s capabilities.
saa-33-aurora-architecture./sections/1/paragraphs/3: The global database is the 4th building block in decisions about Aurora clusters, replicas, and global databases, but its value becomes apparent only when considered alongside business requirements. For SAA-C03, learners should not stop at asking, “What is this service used for?” Identify the inputs received by the global database, the state or policies it manages, the failure modes it addresses, and the operational costs it incurs. At BlueRetail, the website must scale reads within the Region and reduce RPO when expanding into a second market. Connecting the global database to the cluster volume reveals where the responsibility boundary lies and why two similarly named options can produce different outcomes. A good solution must be traceable from the technical choice back to latency, availability, security, data, or the operations team’s capabilities.
saa-33-aurora-architecture./sections/2/title: Make decisions step by step and document the trade-offs
saa-33-aurora-architecture./sections/2/paragraphs/0: A decision involving the cluster volume should be documented as a chain of reasoning, not as a standalone service name. First, confirm which requirements are mandatory. Then check quota limits, Region, consistency, network paths, and the responsibility model. Next, compare the cluster volume with an Aurora replica using the same criteria; do not compare one feature of option A with the total cost of option B. Finally, state the remaining trade-off and how it will be observed after deployment. In Lesson 33, this approach helps BlueRetail understand why the selected option is good enough for the current context and identify the signals that would require the architecture to be reconsidered.
saa-33-aurora-architecture./sections/2/paragraphs/1: A decision involving the reader endpoint should be documented as a chain of reasoning, not as a standalone service name. First, confirm which requirements are mandatory. Then check quota limits, Region, consistency, network paths, and the responsibility model. Next, compare the reader endpoint with the global database using the same criteria; do not compare one feature of option A with the total cost of option B. Finally, state the remaining trade-off and how it will be observed after deployment. In Lesson 33, this approach helps BlueRetail understand why the selected option is good enough for the current context and identify the signals that would require the architecture to be reconsidered.
saa-33-aurora-architecture./sections/2/paragraphs/2: A decision involving an Aurora replica should be documented as a chain of reasoning, not as a standalone service name. First, confirm which requirements are mandatory. Then check quota limits, Region, consistency, network paths, and the responsibility model. Next, compare the Aurora replica with the cluster volume using the same criteria; do not compare one feature of option A with the total cost of option B. Finally, state the remaining trade-off and how it will be observed after deployment. In Lesson 33, this approach helps BlueRetail understand why the selected option is good enough for the current context and identify the signals that would require the architecture to be reconsidered.
saa-33-aurora-architecture./sections/2/paragraphs/3: A decision involving the global database should be documented as a chain of reasoning, not as a standalone service name. First, confirm which requirements are mandatory. Then check quota limits, Region, consistency, network paths, and the responsibility model. Next, compare the global database with the reader endpoint using the same criteria; do not compare one feature of option A with the total cost of option B. Finally, state the remaining trade-off and how it will be observed after deployment. In Lesson 33, this approach helps BlueRetail understand why the selected option is good enough for the current context and identify the signals that would require the architecture to be reconsidered.
saa-33-aurora-architecture./sections/3/title: Design for failure before it occurs
saa-33-aurora-architecture./sections/3/paragraphs/0: Failure mode 1 for Aurora clusters, replicas, and global databases occurs when the project team assumes that the cluster volume automatically addresses every related layer. In reality, service availability does not replace correct access permissions, replication does not replace backups, encryption does not automatically grant permission to use a key, and autoscaling cannot fix a stateful dependency. At BlueRetail, map the cluster volume’s blast radius, identify the portion for which AWS is responsible, and identify what the application team must still configure or test. Then select a metric, log, or event that can provide an early warning. Analyzing failure modes first ensures that the answer is valid not only when the system is healthy but also provides a recovery path when an AZ, connection, credential, or operational process fails.
saa-33-aurora-architecture./sections/3/paragraphs/1: Failure mode 2 for Aurora clusters, replicas, and global databases occurs when the project team assumes that the reader endpoint automatically addresses every related layer. In reality, service availability does not replace correct access permissions, replication does not replace backups, encryption does not automatically grant permission to use a key, and autoscaling cannot fix a stateful dependency. At BlueRetail, map the reader endpoint’s blast radius, identify the portion for which AWS is responsible, and identify what the application team must still configure or test. Then select a metric, log, or event that can provide an early warning. Analyzing failure modes first ensures that the answer is valid not only when the system is healthy but also provides a recovery path when an AZ, connection, credential, or operational process fails.
saa-33-aurora-architecture./sections/3/paragraphs/2: Failure mode 3 for Aurora clusters, replicas, and global databases occurs when the project team assumes that the Aurora replica automatically addresses every related layer. In reality, service availability does not replace correct access permissions, replication does not replace backups, encryption does not automatically grant permission to use a key, and autoscaling cannot fix a stateful dependency. At BlueRetail, map the Aurora replica’s blast radius, identify the portion for which AWS is responsible, and identify what the application team must still configure or test. Then select a metric, log, or event that can provide an early warning. Analyzing failure modes first ensures that the answer is valid not only when the system is healthy but also provides a recovery path when an AZ, connection, credential, or operational process fails.
saa-33-aurora-architecture./sections/4/title: Apply the decision to the BlueRetail case
saa-33-aurora-architecture./sections/4/paragraphs/0: BlueRetail has stores, an e-commerce website, internal applications, and an operations team working across multiple accounts. In Lesson 33, the website must scale reads within the Region and reduce RPO when expanding into a second market. The first step is to separate the stakeholders: users need a stable experience, security needs boundaries and an audit trail, finance needs cost attribution, and the product team needs to make changes quickly. A design optimized for only one stakeholder creates operational debt for another.
saa-33-aurora-architecture./sections/4/paragraphs/1: The architecture team documents three options centered on the cluster volume, reader endpoint, and Aurora replica. Each option is scored using the same matrix: satisfaction of mandatory requirements, blast radius, recovery path, operational burden, scalability, and cost per unit of usage. The global database serves as the final validation criterion. If an option wins only because the team is familiar with it, the decision lacks sufficient evidence and the constraints must be revisited.
saa-33-aurora-architecture./sections/4/paragraphs/2: The conclusion for Aurora clusters, replicas, and global databases is written in four sentences: which structure is selected, which requirements it satisfies, which trade-offs are accepted, and which metric or review will trigger reassessment. This format helps developers, security, and operations understand the same decision. In exam questions, these four sentences also help eliminate distractors: an option that fails to meet a mandatory requirement is eliminated first, even if it offers many attractive features.
saa-33-aurora-architecture./sections/5/title: Selecting the right action in SAA-C03 questions
saa-33-aurora-architecture./sections/5/paragraphs/0: Read an SAA-C03 question in this order: the assigned role, the current state, the required outcome, the constraints, and the verb in the final sentence. For Aurora clusters, replicas, and global databases, mark every detail related to the cluster volume and reader endpoint. Then classify each answer as fully satisfying the requirements, partially satisfying them, introducing additional risk, or falling outside the scope. Do not choose an answer merely because it mentions the correct service. AWS tests your ability to combine multiple requirements into the most appropriate solution.
saa-33-aurora-architecture./sections/5/paragraphs/1: Absolute terms such as “no data loss,” “no downtime,” or “all traffic must remain private” require careful examination. They may require a very different design from terms such as “minimize,” “acceptable,” or “cost is the priority.” In Lesson 33, use the Aurora replica to validate the assurance level and the global database to determine whether the operations team can sustain the option. An answer that has the right feature but the wrong assurance level is still a distractor.
saa-33-aurora-architecture./sections/5/paragraphs/2: In Lesson 33—Aurora clusters, replicas, and global databases—objective SAA-C03 3.3 indicates the required depth. For design, you must balance quality attributes. For determine, you must interpret the facts and select the cause or option. For improve, you must preserve existing constraints while reducing risk through an appropriate change. End each answer with evidence: a metric, log, policy evaluation, recovery test, or cost report. Evidence turns an answer from a guess into a defensible architectural decision.
saa-33-aurora-architecture./visuals/0/title: Decision map: Aurora clusters, replicas, and global databases
saa-33-aurora-architecture./visuals/0/caption: An original diagram connecting requirements to the cluster volume, reader endpoint, Aurora replica, and global database in the BlueRetail case.
saa-33-aurora-architecture./decisionWalkthroughs/0/title: Aurora cluster, replica, and global database decision analysis
saa-33-aurora-architecture./decisionWalkthroughs/0/businessRequirem: BlueRetail must address the scenario in which “the website must scale reads within the Region and reduce RPO when expanding into a second market,” while still being able to explain the effects on security, resilience, performance, and cost.
saa-33-aurora-architecture./decisionWalkthroughs/0/technicalConstra: The design must clarify the cluster volume boundary and the responsibility of the reader endpoint.
The option must include a failure mode and recovery path involving the Aurora replica.
The operations team must observe and reassess the global database using real data.
saa-33-aurora-architecture./decisionWalkthroughs/0/candidates/0/fit: Appropriate when the requirement prioritizes the cluster volume perspective in step 1.
saa-33-aurora-architecture./decisionWalkthroughs/0/candidates/0/lim: Does not address the reader endpoint by itself, and the remaining responsibilities must be reviewed.
saa-33-aurora-architecture./decisionWalkthroughs/0/candidates/1/fit: Appropriate when the requirement prioritizes the reader endpoint perspective in step 2.
saa-33-aurora-architecture./decisionWalkthroughs/0/candidates/1/lim: Does not address the Aurora replica by itself, and the remaining responsibilities must be reviewed.
saa-33-aurora-architecture./decisionWalkthroughs/0/candidates/2/fit: Appropriate when the requirement prioritizes the Aurora replica perspective in step 3.

saa-33-aurora-architecture./decisionWalkthroughs/0/decision: Select an option that combines the cluster volume with the reader endpoint, uses the Aurora replica as a guardrail, and uses the global database as operational evidence.
saa-33-aurora-architecture./decisionWalkthroughs/0/reasoning/0: Eliminate options that do not satisfy mandatory requirements before comparing costs.

saa-33-aurora-architecture./activity/title: Architecture review: Aurora clusters, replicas, and global databases
saa-33-aurora-architecture./activity/context: BlueRetail is preparing to approve a change because the website must scale reads within the Region and reduce RPO when expanding into a second market. You must review the architecture before the team deploys it or incurs cost commitments.
saa-33-aurora-architecture./activity/learnerRole: You are the Solutions Architect responsible for a workload. You must translate requirements into a decision and clearly explain the trade-offs to stakeholders.
saa-33-aurora-architecture./activity/currentArchitecture: Users → access layer → workload → data store; the review points are cluster volume → reader endpoint → Aurora replica → global database.
saa-33-aurora-architecture./activity/requirements/0: The solution must correctly address the requirement related to the cluster volume.
saa-33-aurora-architecture./activity/requirements/1: The recovery path must demonstrate the role of the reader endpoint.
saa-33-aurora-architecture./activity/requirements/2: Security and operations must be able to observe the Aurora replica without relying on assumptions.
saa-33-aurora-architecture./activity/constraints/0: Do not add a service merely to conceal a requirement that has not been clearly defined.
saa-33-aurora-architecture./activity/constraints/1: The global database trade-off must be accepted by stakeholders.
saa-33-aurora-architecture./activity/constraints/2: Do not change the storage schema or account boundary without an architectural justification.
saa-33-aurora-architecture./activity/tasks/0: Rewrite the requirement as measurable criteria.
saa-33-aurora-architecture./activity/tasks/1: Compare at least two options based on the cluster volume, reader endpoint, and Aurora replica.
saa-33-aurora-architecture./activity/tasks/2: Select an option, document the trade-offs, and propose post-deployment evidence.
saa-33-aurora-architecture./activity/reasoningSteps/0: Separate the business outcome, technical constraints, and unvalidated assumptions.
saa-33-aurora-architecture./activity/reasoningSteps/1: Diagram the request and data flows, including trust boundaries and blast radii.
saa-33-aurora-architecture./activity/reasoningSteps/2: Eliminate options that fail to satisfy mandatory requirements before optimizing.
saa-33-aurora-architecture./activity/reasoningSteps/3: Compare the remaining options across reliability, security, performance, cost, and operations.
saa-33-aurora-architecture./activity/reasoningSteps/4: Document the decision, trade-offs, recovery path, and review metric.
saa-33-aurora-architecture./activity/deliverables/0: An architecture diagram showing boundaries and data direction.
saa-33-aurora-architecture./activity/deliverables/1: A decision matrix with at least two options.
saa-33-aurora-architecture./activity/deliverables/2: A concise architecture decision record containing trade-offs and evidence.
saa-33-aurora-architecture./activity/rubric/0/evidence: The decision can be traced back to the cluster volume and does not add assumptions beyond the scenario.
saa-33-aurora-architecture./activity/rubric/0/importance: Prevents services from being selected based on preference or an isolated keyword.
saa-33-aurora-architecture./activity/rubric/1/evidence: Compares the reader endpoint, Aurora replica, and global database using the same criteria.
saa-33-aurora-architecture./activity/rubric/1/importance: AWS architecture always involves balancing priorities; no option is absolutely best.
saa-33-aurora-architecture./activity/rubric/2/evidence: The submission identifies a failure mode, recovery path, and validating metric or test.
saa-33-aurora-architecture./activity/rubric/2/importance: A design is trustworthy only when it can be operated and validated after deployment.
saa-33-aurora-architecture./activity/sampleAnalysis/0: The main requirement is rewritten around the cluster volume; the reader endpoint is a constraint that directly affects the choice.
saa-33-aurora-architecture./activity/sampleAnalysis/1: The option prioritizing the Aurora replica is eliminated if it increases the operational burden of the global database without adding value.
saa-33-aurora-architecture./activity/sampleAnalysis/2: The proposed architecture combines the cluster volume and reader endpoint and clearly documents the blast radius, recovery path, and review metric.
saa-33-aurora-architecture./activity/sampleAnalysis/3: The remaining trade-off is accepted because it fits the scenario in which the website must scale reads within the Region and reduce RPO when expanding into a second market.
saa-33-aurora-architecture./activity/commonMistakes/0: Listing services without connecting them to the requirement.
saa-33-aurora-architecture./activity/commonMistakes/1: Describing only the happy path and ignoring failure modes.
saa-33-aurora-architecture./activity/commonMistakes/2: Saying that “AWS manages it” without identifying the customer’s responsibilities.
saa-33-aurora-architecture./activity/reflectionQuestions/0: Which detail in the scenario eliminated the most options?
saa-33-aurora-architecture./activity/reflectionQuestions/1: Which metric or event would cause you to reconsider the decision?
saa-33-aurora-architecture./activity/reflectionQuestions/2: If the budget or RTO changes, which part of the architecture must change first?
saa-33-aurora-architecture./recap/0: Aurora clusters, replicas, and global databases must begin with requirements and constraints.
saa-33-aurora-architecture./recap/1: The cluster volume, reader endpoint, Aurora replica, and global database form a chain of responsibilities.
saa-33-aurora-architecture./recap/2: Every decision needs trade-offs, a recovery path, and operational evidence.
saa-33-aurora-architecture./recap/3: In exam questions, eliminate options that fail to satisfy mandatory requirements before comparing optimizations.
saa-33-aurora-architecture./selfCheck/0: Can you explain the difference between the roles of the cluster volume and reader endpoint?
saa-33-aurora-architecture./selfCheck/1: Which failure mode involves the Aurora replica, and what is the corresponding recovery path?

saa-33-aurora-architecture./selfCheck/3: Which metric, log, or test would you use to validate the decision?
