saa-38-streaming-ingestion./title: Kinesis, MSK, and streaming data ingestion
saa-38-streaming-ingestion./shortTitle: Kinesis, MSK, and streaming data ingestion
saa-38-streaming-ingestion./summary: Understand Kinesis, MSK, and streaming data ingestion through requirements, constraints, trade-offs, and an architectural decision that can be explained to the BlueRetail team.
saa-38-streaming-ingestion./opening/0: BlueRetail is changing its architecture, and clickstream events must be processed in near real time, preserve per-user ordering, and allow multiple consumers to read independently. A diagram with many AWS icons is not necessarily a good design: what matters is which requirement each component addresses, which failure modes remain, and whether the operations team can manage it. Lesson 38 starts with this scenario and turns Kinesis, MSK, and streaming data ingestion into a sequence of evidence-based decisions rather than a list of features to memorize.
saa-38-streaming-ingestion./opening/1: In SAA-C03, questions often present several technically viable options, but only one best matches keywords such as “least operational overhead,” “no data loss,” “low cost,” “multiple accounts,” or “no use of the public internet.” Kinesis, MSK, and streaming data ingestion should be evaluated from four perspectives: stream shard, consumer model, event ordering, and retention window. Each perspective eliminates a group of answers if it conflicts with the requirements or introduces unacceptable responsibilities.
saa-38-streaming-ingestion./opening/2: The scope of Lesson 38 — Kinesis, MSK, and streaming data ingestion is aligned with objective SAA-C03 3.5 in the current AWS Exam Guide. The GitHub topic repository is used only to check coverage and content organization; all explanations, cases, and diagrams here are newly written, while the technical conclusions are validated against AWS Documentation and the Well-Architected Framework. After this lesson, you should be able to explain not only “what to choose,” but also “why the other options were not chosen.”
saa-38-streaming-ingestion./sections/0/title: Mental model: start with requirements, not services
saa-38-streaming-ingestion./sections/0/paragraphs/0: Treat an AWS architecture as a contract among business, application, and platform teams. The business defines what must remain operational and the acceptable level of loss; the application defines traffic, data, and dependencies; and the platform translates those requirements into boundaries, services, policies, and observability mechanisms. For Kinesis, MSK, and streaming data ingestion, the stream shard is the entry point, the consumer model determines how the system responds, and event ordering and the retention window determine long-term operability. Going directly from a requirement to a service name often causes one of these four layers to be overlooked.
saa-38-streaming-ingestion./sections/0/paragraphs/1: A simple model that works for every lesson is R-C-D-E: Requirement, Constraint, Decision, Evidence. A requirement describes the outcome; a constraint limits the scope through factors such as Region, budget, or skills; a decision is the selected architectural structure; and evidence is a metric, log, test, or document confirming that the decision remains valid. By applying R-C-D-E to Kinesis, MSK, and streaming data ingestion, learners can see that the same service may be right for one workload but wrong for another. This skill matters more than memorizing the entire AWS catalog.
saa-38-streaming-ingestion./sections/0/paragraphs/2: A sign that you understand Lesson 38 is that you can trace a request or operational event from beginning to end. Which boundary does the request cross, which identity is evaluated, where is the data retained, which component can fail, and what mechanism provides recovery? If the story breaks at the stream shard or consumer model, the diagram still contains an unverified assumption. Record that assumption next to the diagram before selecting another service.
saa-38-streaming-ingestion./sections/1/title: Four concepts that must be connected
saa-38-streaming-ingestion./sections/1/paragraphs/0: The stream shard is the first building block in decisions about Kinesis, MSK, and streaming data ingestion, but its value becomes clear only when considered alongside business requirements. For SAA-C03, learners should not stop at “what is this service used for?” Identify the input that the stream shard receives, the state or policy it manages, the failure mode it addresses, and the resulting operational cost. At BlueRetail, clickstream events must be processed in near real time, preserve per-user ordering, and allow multiple consumers to read independently. Connecting the stream shard to the consumer model shows where the responsibility boundary lies and why two similarly named options can produce different outcomes. A good solution must trace the technical choice back to latency, availability, security, data, or the operations team's capabilities.
saa-38-streaming-ingestion./sections/1/paragraphs/1: The consumer model is the second building block in decisions about Kinesis, MSK, and streaming data ingestion, but its value becomes clear only when considered alongside business requirements. For SAA-C03, learners should not stop at “what is this service used for?” Identify the input that the consumer model receives, the state or policy it manages, the failure mode it addresses, and the resulting operational cost. At BlueRetail, clickstream events must be processed in near real time, preserve per-user ordering, and allow multiple consumers to read independently. Connecting the consumer model to event ordering shows where the responsibility boundary lies and why two similarly named options can produce different outcomes. A good solution must trace the technical choice back to latency, availability, security, data, or the operations team's capabilities.
saa-38-streaming-ingestion./sections/1/paragraphs/2: Event ordering is the third building block in decisions about Kinesis, MSK, and streaming data ingestion, but its value becomes clear only when considered alongside business requirements. For SAA-C03, learners should not stop at “what is this service used for?” Identify the input that event ordering receives, the state or policy it manages, the failure mode it addresses, and the resulting operational cost. At BlueRetail, clickstream events must be processed in near real time, preserve per-user ordering, and allow multiple consumers to read independently. Connecting event ordering to the retention window shows where the responsibility boundary lies and why two similarly named options can produce different outcomes. A good solution must trace the technical choice back to latency, availability, security, data, or the operations team's capabilities.
saa-38-streaming-ingestion./sections/1/paragraphs/3: The retention window is the fourth building block in decisions about Kinesis, MSK, and streaming data ingestion, but its value becomes clear only when considered alongside business requirements. For SAA-C03, learners should not stop at “what is this service used for?” Identify the input that the retention window receives, the state or policy it manages, the failure mode it addresses, and the resulting operational cost. At BlueRetail, clickstream events must be processed in near real time, preserve per-user ordering, and allow multiple consumers to read independently. Connecting the retention window to the stream shard shows where the responsibility boundary lies and why two similarly named options can produce different outcomes. A good solution must trace the technical choice back to latency, availability, security, data, or the operations team's capabilities.
saa-38-streaming-ingestion./sections/2/title: Make decisions step by step and record the trade-offs
saa-38-streaming-ingestion./sections/2/paragraphs/0: A decision involving a stream shard should be documented as a chain of reasoning, not as an isolated service name. First, confirm which requirements are mandatory, then check quota limits, Region, consistency, network path, and the responsibility model. Next, compare the stream shard with event ordering using the same criteria; do not compare one feature of option A with the total cost of option B. Finally, state the remaining trade-off and how it will be observed after deployment. In Lesson 38, this approach helps BlueRetail understand why the selected option is good enough in the current context and which signals would trigger an architectural review.
saa-38-streaming-ingestion./sections/2/paragraphs/1: A decision involving a consumer model should be documented as a chain of reasoning, not as an isolated service name. First, confirm which requirements are mandatory, then check quota limits, Region, consistency, network path, and the responsibility model. Next, compare the consumer model with the retention window using the same criteria; do not compare one feature of option A with the total cost of option B. Finally, state the remaining trade-off and how it will be observed after deployment. In Lesson 38, this approach helps BlueRetail understand why the selected option is good enough in the current context and which signals would trigger an architectural review.
saa-38-streaming-ingestion./sections/2/paragraphs/2: A decision involving event ordering should be documented as a chain of reasoning, not as an isolated service name. First, confirm which requirements are mandatory, then check quota limits, Region, consistency, network path, and the responsibility model. Next, compare event ordering with the stream shard using the same criteria; do not compare one feature of option A with the total cost of option B. Finally, state the remaining trade-off and how it will be observed after deployment. In Lesson 38, this approach helps BlueRetail understand why the selected option is good enough in the current context and which signals would trigger an architectural review.
saa-38-streaming-ingestion./sections/2/paragraphs/3: A decision involving a retention window should be documented as a chain of reasoning, not as an isolated service name. First, confirm which requirements are mandatory, then check quota limits, Region, consistency, network path, and the responsibility model. Next, compare the retention window with the consumer model using the same criteria; do not compare one feature of option A with the total cost of option B. Finally, state the remaining trade-off and how it will be observed after deployment. In Lesson 38, this approach helps BlueRetail understand why the selected option is good enough in the current context and which signals would trigger an architectural review.
saa-38-streaming-ingestion./sections/3/title: Design for failure before it occurs
saa-38-streaming-ingestion./sections/3/paragraphs/0: Failure mode 1 for Kinesis, MSK, and streaming data ingestion occurs when the project team assumes that a stream shard automatically addresses every related layer. In reality, service availability does not replace proper access permissions, replication does not replace backup, encryption does not automatically grant permission to use a key, and autoscaling cannot fix a stateful dependency. At BlueRetail, draw the blast radius of the stream shard and mark what AWS is responsible for and what the application team must still configure or test. Then select a metric, log, or event that can provide early warning. Analyzing the failure mode first ensures that the answer is not only correct while the system is healthy, but also provides a recovery path when an AZ, connection, credential, or operational process fails.
saa-38-streaming-ingestion./sections/3/paragraphs/1: Failure mode 2 for Kinesis, MSK, and streaming data ingestion occurs when the project team assumes that the consumer model automatically addresses every related layer. In reality, service availability does not replace proper access permissions, replication does not replace backup, encryption does not automatically grant permission to use a key, and autoscaling cannot fix a stateful dependency. At BlueRetail, draw the blast radius of the consumer model and mark what AWS is responsible for and what the application team must still configure or test. Then select a metric, log, or event that can provide early warning. Analyzing the failure mode first ensures that the answer is not only correct while the system is healthy, but also provides a recovery path when an AZ, connection, credential, or operational process fails.
saa-38-streaming-ingestion./sections/3/paragraphs/2: Failure mode 3 for Kinesis, MSK, and streaming data ingestion occurs when the project team assumes that event ordering automatically addresses every related layer. In reality, service availability does not replace proper access permissions, replication does not replace backup, encryption does not automatically grant permission to use a key, and autoscaling cannot fix a stateful dependency. At BlueRetail, draw the blast radius of event ordering and mark what AWS is responsible for and what the application team must still configure or test. Then select a metric, log, or event that can provide early warning. Analyzing the failure mode first ensures that the answer is not only correct while the system is healthy, but also provides a recovery path when an AZ, connection, credential, or operational process fails.
saa-38-streaming-ingestion./sections/4/title: Apply the decision to the BlueRetail case
saa-38-streaming-ingestion./sections/4/paragraphs/0: BlueRetail has stores, an e-commerce website, internal applications, and an operations team working across multiple accounts. In Lesson 38, clickstream events must be processed in near real time, preserve per-user ordering, and allow multiple consumers to read independently. The first step is to separate the stakeholders: users need a stable experience, security needs boundaries and an audit trail, finance needs cost attribution, and the product team needs to make changes quickly. A design optimized for only one stakeholder creates operational debt for another.
saa-38-streaming-ingestion./sections/4/paragraphs/1: The architecture team documents three options centered on the stream shard, consumer model, and event ordering. Each option is scored using the same matrix: satisfaction of mandatory requirements, blast radius, recovery path, operational burden, scalability, and cost per unit of use. The retention window serves as the final validation condition. If an option wins only because the team is familiar with it, the decision lacks sufficient evidence and the constraints must be revisited.
saa-38-streaming-ingestion./sections/4/paragraphs/2: The conclusion for Kinesis, MSK, and streaming data ingestion is written in four sentences: which structure is selected; which requirements it satisfies; which trade-off is accepted; and which metric or review will trigger reassessment. This format helps developers, security, and operations understand the same decision. In exam questions, the four sentences also help eliminate distractors: an option that fails a mandatory requirement is eliminated first, even if it offers many attractive features.
saa-38-streaming-ingestion./sections/5/title: How to choose an action in SAA-C03 questions
saa-38-streaming-ingestion./sections/5/paragraphs/0: Read an SAA-C03 question in this order: the assigned role, current state, mandatory outcome, constraints, and the verb at the end of the question. For Kinesis, MSK, and streaming data ingestion, mark every detail related to the stream shard and consumer model. Then classify each answer as fully satisfying the requirements, partially satisfying them, introducing additional risk, or being out of scope. Do not choose an answer merely because it names the correct service; AWS tests your ability to combine multiple requirements into the best-fit solution.
saa-38-streaming-ingestion./sections/5/paragraphs/1: Absolute phrases such as “no data loss,” “no downtime,” or “all traffic must remain private” require careful evaluation. They may require a very different design from phrases such as “minimize,” “acceptable,” or “prioritize cost.” In Lesson 38, use event ordering to verify the required guarantee and the retention window to determine whether the operations team can sustain the option. An answer that provides the right feature but the wrong level of assurance is still a distractor.
saa-38-streaming-ingestion./sections/5/paragraphs/2: In Lesson 38 — Kinesis, MSK, and streaming data ingestion, objective SAA-C03 3.5 indicates the required depth. For design, you must balance quality attributes; for determine, you must interpret the facts and select a cause or option; for improve, you must preserve existing constraints and reduce risk through an appropriate change. End each answer with evidence: a metric, log, policy evaluation, recovery test, or cost report. Evidence turns an answer from a guess into a defensible architectural decision.

saa-38-streaming-ingestion./decisionWalkthroughs/0/technicalConstra: The design must clarify the stream shard boundary and the responsibilities of the consumer model.
The option must include a failure mode and recovery path related to event ordering.

saa-38-streaming-ingestion./activity/title: Architecture review: Kinesis, MSK, and streaming data ingestion
saa-38-streaming-ingestion./activity/context: BlueRetail is preparing to approve a change because clickstream events must be processed in near real time, preserve per-user ordering, and allow multiple consumers to read independently. You must review the architecture before the team deploys it or incurs cost commitments.
saa-38-streaming-ingestion./activity/learnerRole: You are the Solutions Architect responsible for a workload, translating requirements into a decision and clearly explaining the trade-offs to stakeholders.
saa-38-streaming-ingestion./activity/currentArchitecture: Users → access layer → workload → data store; the review points are stream shard → consumer model → event ordering → retention window.
saa-38-streaming-ingestion./activity/requirements/0: The solution must correctly address the requirement related to the stream shard.
saa-38-streaming-ingestion./activity/requirements/1: The recovery path must show the role of the consumer model.
saa-38-streaming-ingestion./activity/requirements/2: Security and operations must be able to observe event ordering without relying on assumptions.
saa-38-streaming-ingestion./activity/constraints/0: Do not add a service merely to conceal a requirement that has not been clearly defined.
saa-38-streaming-ingestion./activity/constraints/1: Stakeholders must accept the trade-off involving the retention window.
saa-38-streaming-ingestion./activity/constraints/2: Do not change the storage schema or account boundary without an architectural reason.
saa-38-streaming-ingestion./activity/tasks/0: Rewrite the requirement as measurable criteria.
saa-38-streaming-ingestion./activity/tasks/1: Compare at least two options based on the stream shard, consumer model, and event ordering.
saa-38-streaming-ingestion./activity/tasks/2: Select an option, document the trade-off, and propose post-deployment evidence.
saa-38-streaming-ingestion./activity/reasoningSteps/0: Separate the business outcome, technical constraints, and unvalidated assumptions.
saa-38-streaming-ingestion./activity/reasoningSteps/1: Draw the request/data flow with trust boundaries and blast radii.
saa-38-streaming-ingestion./activity/reasoningSteps/2: Eliminate options that fail mandatory requirements before optimizing.
saa-38-streaming-ingestion./activity/reasoningSteps/3: Compare the remaining options across reliability, security, performance, cost, and operations.
saa-38-streaming-ingestion./activity/reasoningSteps/4: Document the decision, trade-off, recovery path, and review metric.
saa-38-streaming-ingestion./activity/deliverables/0: An architecture diagram showing boundaries and data flow direction.
saa-38-streaming-ingestion./activity/deliverables/1: A decision matrix with at least two options.
saa-38-streaming-ingestion./activity/deliverables/2: A short architecture decision record containing the trade-off and evidence.
saa-38-streaming-ingestion./activity/rubric/0/evidence: The decision can be traced back to the stream shard and adds no assumptions beyond the scenario.
saa-38-streaming-ingestion./activity/rubric/0/importance: Prevents selecting services based on preference or isolated keywords.
saa-38-streaming-ingestion./activity/rubric/1/evidence: Compares the consumer model, event ordering, and retention window using the same criteria.
saa-38-streaming-ingestion./activity/rubric/1/importance: AWS architecture always involves balance; there is no universally best option.
saa-38-streaming-ingestion./activity/rubric/2/evidence: The submission identifies a failure mode, recovery path, and validation metric or test.
saa-38-streaming-ingestion./activity/rubric/2/importance: A design is trustworthy only if it can be operated and validated after deployment.
saa-38-streaming-ingestion./activity/sampleAnalysis/0: The primary requirement is rewritten around the stream shard; the consumer model is a constraint that directly affects the choice.
saa-38-streaming-ingestion./activity/sampleAnalysis/1: The option that prioritizes event ordering is eliminated if it increases the operational burden of the retention window without adding value.
saa-38-streaming-ingestion./activity/sampleAnalysis/2: The proposed architecture combines the stream shard and consumer model and clearly documents the blast radius, recovery path, and review metric.
saa-38-streaming-ingestion./activity/sampleAnalysis/3: The remaining trade-off is accepted because it fits the need to process clickstream events in near real time, preserve per-user ordering, and allow multiple consumers to read independently.
saa-38-streaming-ingestion./activity/commonMistakes/0: Listing services without connecting them to the requirement.
saa-38-streaming-ingestion./activity/commonMistakes/1: Describing only the happy path and ignoring failure modes.
saa-38-streaming-ingestion./activity/commonMistakes/2: Saying “AWS manages it” without identifying the customer's responsibilities.

saa-39-database-cost-selection./title: Choosing a database based on access patterns and cost
saa-39-database-cost-selection./shortTitle: Choosing a database based on access patterns and cost
saa-39-database-cost-selection./summary: Learn how to choose a database based on access patterns and cost by evaluating requirements, constraints, trade-offs, and an architectural decision that can be explained to the BlueRetail team.
saa-39-database-cost-selection./opening/0: BlueRetail is changing its architecture, and the project team has been choosing databases based on past experience, resulting in high costs for idle capacity and unused indexes. A diagram with many AWS icons is not necessarily a good design: what matters is which requirement each component addresses, which failure modes remain, and whether the operations team can manage it. Lesson 39 begins with this scenario and turns database selection based on access patterns and cost into an evidence-based decision sequence rather than a list of features to memorize.
saa-39-database-cost-selection./opening/1: In SAA-C03, questions often present several technically viable options, but only one is the best fit for keywords such as “least operational effort,” “no data loss,” “low cost,” “multiple accounts,” or “no public internet.” Choosing a database based on access patterns and cost requires examining four perspectives: access pattern, capacity utilization, serverless database, and operational overhead. Each perspective eliminates a group of answers that conflict with the requirements or introduce unacceptable responsibilities.
saa-39-database-cost-selection./opening/2: The scope of Lesson 39 — Choosing a database based on access patterns and cost is aligned with objective SAA-C03 4.3 in the current AWS Exam Guide. The GitHub topic repository is used only to verify coverage and content organization; all explanations, cases, and diagrams here are newly written, while the technical conclusions are cross-checked against AWS Documentation and the Well-Architected Framework. After this lesson, you should be able to explain not only “what to choose,” but also “why not to choose the other options.”
saa-39-database-cost-selection./sections/0/title: Mental model: start with requirements, not services
saa-39-database-cost-selection./sections/0/paragraphs/0: Think of an AWS architecture as a contract among business, application, and platform teams. The business defines what must continue operating and what level of loss is acceptable; the application describes traffic, data, and dependencies; the platform translates those requirements into boundaries, services, policies, and observability mechanisms. When choosing a database based on access patterns and cost, the access pattern is the entry point, capacity utilization indicates how the system responds, and serverless database and operational overhead determine long-term operability. Going directly from a requirement to a service name often causes one of these four layers to be overlooked.
saa-39-database-cost-selection./sections/0/paragraphs/1: A compact model that works for every lesson is R-C-D-E: Requirement, Constraint, Decision, Evidence. A requirement describes the outcome; a constraint limits the scope through factors such as Region, budget, or skills; a decision is the selected architectural structure; and evidence is a metric, log, test, or document confirming that the decision remains valid. By applying R-C-D-E to Choosing a database based on access patterns and cost, learners can see that the same service may be right for one workload but wrong for another. This skill is more important than memorizing the entire AWS catalog.
saa-39-database-cost-selection./sections/0/paragraphs/2: You understand Lesson 39 when you can trace a request or operational event from beginning to end. Which boundaries does the request cross, which identities are evaluated, where is the data retained, which components can fail, and which mechanisms provide recovery? If the story breaks at the access pattern or capacity utilization, the diagram still contains an unverified assumption. Record that assumption beside the diagram before selecting another service.
saa-39-database-cost-selection./sections/1/title: Four concepts that must be connected
saa-39-database-cost-selection./sections/1/paragraphs/0: access pattern is the first element in choosing a database based on access patterns and cost, but its value becomes clear only when considered alongside business requirements. For SAA-C03, learners should not stop at asking, “What is this service used for?” Identify the input received by the access pattern, the state or policy it manages, the failure mode it addresses, and the operational cost it introduces. At BlueRetail, the project team has been choosing databases based on past experience, resulting in high costs for idle capacity and unused indexes. Connecting access pattern with capacity utilization reveals where the responsibility boundary lies and why two similarly named options can produce different outcomes. A good solution must be traceable from the technical choice back to latency, availability, security, data, or the capabilities of the operations team.

For SAA-C03, learners should not stop at asking, “What is this service used for?” Identify the input received by capacity utilization, the state or policy it manages, the failure mode it addresses, and the operational cost it introduces. Connecting capacity utilization with serverless database reveals where the responsibility boundary lies and why two similarly named options can produce different outcomes. For SAA-C03, learners should not stop at asking, “What is this service used for?” Identify the input received by the serverless database, the state or policy it manages, the failure mode it addresses, and the operational cost it introduces. saa-39-database-cost-selection./sections/2/title: Make decisions step by step and record the trade-offs
saa-39-database-cost-selection./sections/2/paragraphs/0: A decision involving an access pattern should be documented as a chain of reasoning, not as a standalone service name. First, confirm which requirements are mandatory, then check quota limits, Region, consistency, network path, and the responsibility model. Next, compare access pattern with serverless database using the same criteria; do not compare one feature of option A with the total cost of option B. Finally, state the remaining trade-off and how it will be observed after deployment. In Lesson 39, this approach helps BlueRetail understand why the selected option is good enough in the current context and which signals should trigger an architectural review.
saa-39-database-cost-selection./sections/2/paragraphs/1: A decision involving capacity utilization should be documented as a chain of reasoning, not as a standalone service name. First, confirm which requirements are mandatory, then check quota limits, Region, consistency, network path, and the responsibility model. Next, compare capacity utilization with operational overhead using the same criteria; do not compare one feature of option A with the total cost of option B. Finally, state the remaining trade-off and how it will be observed after deployment. In Lesson 39, this approach helps BlueRetail understand why the selected option is good enough in the current context and which signals should trigger an architectural review.
saa-39-database-cost-selection./sections/2/paragraphs/2: A decision involving a serverless database should be documented as a chain of reasoning, not as a standalone service name. First, confirm which requirements are mandatory, then check quota limits, Region, consistency, network path, and the responsibility model. Next, compare serverless database with access pattern using the same criteria; do not compare one feature of option A with the total cost of option B. Finally, state the remaining trade-off and how it will be observed after deployment. In Lesson 39, this approach helps BlueRetail understand why the selected option is good enough in the current context and which signals should trigger an architectural review.
saa-39-database-cost-selection./sections/2/paragraphs/3: A decision involving operational overhead should be documented as a chain of reasoning, not as a standalone service name. First, confirm which requirements are mandatory, then check quota limits, Region, consistency, network path, and the responsibility model. Next, compare operational overhead with capacity utilization using the same criteria; do not compare one feature of option A with the total cost of option B. Finally, state the remaining trade-off and how it will be observed after deployment. In Lesson 39, this approach helps BlueRetail understand why the selected option is good enough in the current context and which signals should trigger an architectural review.
saa-39-database-cost-selection./sections/3/title: Design for failure before it occurs
saa-39-database-cost-selection./sections/3/paragraphs/0: Failure mode 1 in choosing a database based on access patterns and cost occurs when the project team assumes that the access pattern automatically addresses every related layer. In reality, service availability does not replace correct access permissions, replication does not replace backups, encryption does not automatically grant permission to use a key, and autoscaling cannot fix a stateful dependency. At BlueRetail, draw the blast radius of the access pattern and mark what AWS is responsible for and what the application team must still configure or test. Then select a metric, log, or event that can provide early warning. Analyzing the failure mode first ensures that the answer is valid not only when the system is healthy but also provides a recovery path when an AZ, connection, credential, or operational process fails.
saa-39-database-cost-selection./sections/3/paragraphs/1: Failure mode 2 in choosing a database based on access patterns and cost occurs when the project team assumes that capacity utilization automatically addresses every related layer. In reality, service availability does not replace correct access permissions, replication does not replace backups, encryption does not automatically grant permission to use a key, and autoscaling cannot fix a stateful dependency. At BlueRetail, draw the blast radius of capacity utilization and mark what AWS is responsible for and what the application team must still configure or test. Then select a metric, log, or event that can provide early warning. Analyzing the failure mode first ensures that the answer is valid not only when the system is healthy but also provides a recovery path when an AZ, connection, credential, or operational process fails.
saa-39-database-cost-selection./sections/3/paragraphs/2: Failure mode 3 in choosing a database based on access patterns and cost occurs when the project team assumes that the serverless database automatically addresses every related layer. In reality, service availability does not replace correct access permissions, replication does not replace backups, encryption does not automatically grant permission to use a key, and autoscaling cannot fix a stateful dependency. At BlueRetail, draw the blast radius of the serverless database and mark what AWS is responsible for and what the application team must still configure or test. Then select a metric, log, or event that can provide early warning. Analyzing the failure mode first ensures that the answer is valid not only when the system is healthy but also provides a recovery path when an AZ, connection, credential, or operational process fails.
saa-39-database-cost-selection./sections/4/title: Apply the decision to the BlueRetail case
saa-39-database-cost-selection./sections/4/paragraphs/0: BlueRetail has stores, an e-commerce website, internal applications, and an operations team working across multiple accounts. In Lesson 39, the project team has been choosing databases based on past experience, resulting in high costs for idle capacity and unused indexes. The first step is to separate the stakeholders: users need a stable experience, security needs boundaries and an audit trail, finance needs cost attribution, and the product team needs to make changes quickly. A design optimized for only one stakeholder creates operational debt for another.
saa-39-database-cost-selection./sections/4/paragraphs/1: The architecture team documents three options centered on access pattern, capacity utilization, and serverless database. Each option is scored against the same criteria: satisfaction of mandatory requirements, blast radius, recovery path, operational burden, scalability, and cost per unit of usage. operational overhead serves as the final validation criterion. If an option wins only because the team is familiar with it, the decision lacks sufficient evidence and the constraints must be revisited.
saa-39-database-cost-selection./sections/4/paragraphs/2: The conclusion for choosing a database based on access patterns and cost is written in four sentences: which structure is selected; which requirements it satisfies; which trade-off is accepted; and which metric or review will trigger reassessment. This format helps developers, security, and operations understand the same decision. In exam questions, these four sentences also help eliminate distractors: an option that fails a mandatory requirement is eliminated first, even if it offers many appealing features.
saa-39-database-cost-selection./sections/5/title: Reasoning about the required action in SAA-C03 questions
saa-39-database-cost-selection./sections/5/paragraphs/0: Read an SAA-C03 question in this order: assigned role, current state, required outcome, constraints, and the final verb in the question. When choosing a database based on access patterns and cost, mark every detail related to access pattern and capacity utilization. Then classify each answer as fully satisfying the requirements, partially satisfying them, introducing additional risk, or being out of scope. Do not select an answer merely because it mentions the correct service; AWS tests your ability to combine multiple requirements into the best-fit solution.
saa-39-database-cost-selection./sections/5/paragraphs/1: Absolute phrases such as “no data loss,” “no downtime,” or “all traffic must remain private” require careful evaluation. They may call for a very different design than phrases such as “minimize,” “acceptable,” or “prioritize cost.” In Lesson 39, use serverless database to assess the required assurance level and operational overhead to determine whether the operations team can sustain the option. An answer with the right feature but the wrong assurance level is still a distractor.
saa-39-database-cost-selection./sections/5/paragraphs/2: In Lesson 39 — Choosing a database based on access patterns and cost, objective SAA-C03 4.3 indicates the required depth. For design, you must balance quality attributes; for determine, you must interpret the facts and select a cause or option; for improve, you must preserve existing constraints while reducing risk through an appropriate change. End each answer with evidence: a metric, log, policy evaluation, recovery test, or cost report. Evidence turns an answer from a guess into a defensible architectural decision.

saa-39-database-cost-selection./activity/title: Architecture review: Choosing a database based on access patterns and cost
saa-39-database-cost-selection./activity/context: BlueRetail is preparing to approve a change because the project team has been choosing databases based on past experience, resulting in high costs for idle capacity and unused indexes. You must review the architecture before the team deploys it or incurs a cost commitment.
saa-39-database-cost-selection./activity/learnerRole: You are the Solutions Architect responsible for a workload, translating requirements into a decision and clearly communicating the trade-off to stakeholders.
saa-39-database-cost-selection./activity/currentArchitecture: Users → access layer → workload → data store; the review points are access pattern → capacity utilization → serverless database → operational overhead.
saa-39-database-cost-selection./activity/requirements/0: The solution must correctly address the requirement related to access pattern.
saa-39-database-cost-selection./activity/requirements/1: The recovery path must show the role of capacity utilization.
saa-39-database-cost-selection./activity/requirements/2: Security and operations must be able to observe the serverless database without relying on assumptions.
saa-39-database-cost-selection./activity/constraints/0: Do not add a service merely to conceal a requirement that has not been clearly defined.
saa-39-database-cost-selection./activity/constraints/1: The trade-off involving operational overhead must be accepted by stakeholders.
saa-39-database-cost-selection./activity/constraints/2: Do not change the storage schema or account boundary without an architectural reason.
saa-39-database-cost-selection./activity/tasks/0: Rewrite the requirement as measurable criteria.
saa-39-database-cost-selection./activity/tasks/1: Compare at least two options based on access pattern, capacity utilization, and serverless database.
saa-39-database-cost-selection./activity/tasks/2: Select an option, document the trade-off, and propose post-deployment evidence.
saa-39-database-cost-selection./activity/reasoningSteps/0: Separate the business outcome, technical constraints, and unverified assumptions.
saa-39-database-cost-selection./activity/reasoningSteps/1: Draw the request and data flows with the trust boundary and blast radius.
saa-39-database-cost-selection./activity/reasoningSteps/2: Eliminate options that fail mandatory requirements before optimizing.
saa-39-database-cost-selection./activity/reasoningSteps/3: Compare the remaining options across reliability, security, performance, cost, and operations.
saa-39-database-cost-selection./activity/reasoningSteps/4: Document the decision, trade-off, recovery path, and review metric.
saa-39-database-cost-selection./activity/deliverables/0: An architecture diagram showing boundaries and data flow directions.
saa-39-database-cost-selection./activity/deliverables/1: A decision matrix with at least two options.
saa-39-database-cost-selection./activity/deliverables/2: A brief architecture decision record containing the trade-off and evidence.
saa-39-database-cost-selection./activity/rubric/0/evidence: The decision is traceable to the access pattern and introduces no assumptions beyond the question.
saa-39-database-cost-selection./activity/rubric/0/importance: Prevents selecting a service based on preference or a single keyword.
saa-39-database-cost-selection./activity/rubric/1/evidence: Compares capacity utilization, serverless database, and operational overhead using the same criteria.
saa-39-database-cost-selection./activity/rubric/1/importance: AWS architecture always involves balance; there is no universally best option.
saa-39-database-cost-selection./activity/rubric/2/evidence: The submission identifies a failure mode, recovery path, and validating metric or test.
saa-39-database-cost-selection./activity/rubric/2/importance: A design is trustworthy only when it can be operated and validated after deployment.
saa-39-database-cost-selection./activity/sampleAnalysis/0: The primary requirement is rewritten around the access pattern; capacity utilization is a constraint that directly affects the choice.
saa-39-database-cost-selection./activity/sampleAnalysis/1: The option prioritizing serverless database is eliminated if it increases the operational burden of operational overhead without adding value.
saa-39-database-cost-selection./activity/sampleAnalysis/2: The proposed architecture combines access pattern and capacity utilization and clearly documents the blast radius, recovery path, and review metric.
saa-39-database-cost-selection./activity/sampleAnalysis/3: The remaining trade-off is accepted because it fits the situation in which the project team has been choosing databases based on past experience, resulting in high costs for idle capacity and unused indexes.
saa-39-database-cost-selection./activity/commonMistakes/0: Listing services without connecting them to requirements.
saa-39-database-cost-selection./activity/commonMistakes/1: Describing only the happy path and ignoring failure modes.
saa-39-database-cost-selection./activity/commonMistakes/2: Saying “AWS manages it” without identifying the customer's responsibilities.
saa-39-database-cost-selection./activity/reflectionQuestions/0: Which detail in the question eliminated the most options?
saa-39-database-cost-selection./activity/reflectionQuestions/1: Which metric or event would cause you to reassess the decision?
saa-39-database-cost-selection./activity/reflectionQuestions/2: If the budget or RTO changes, which part of the architecture must change first?
saa-39-database-cost-selection./recap/0: Choosing a database based on access patterns and cost must begin with requirements and constraints.
saa-39-database-cost-selection./recap/1: access pattern, capacity utilization, serverless database, and operational overhead form a chain of responsibilities.
saa-39-database-cost-selection./recap/2: Every decision requires a trade-off, recovery path, and operational evidence.
saa-39-database-cost-selection./recap/3: In exam questions, eliminate options that fail mandatory requirements before comparing optimizations.
saa-39-database-cost-selection./selfCheck/0: Can you explain the difference between the roles of access pattern and capacity utilization?

saa-40-sqs-decoupling./title: SQS, visibility timeout, and dead-letter queues
saa-40-sqs-decoupling./shortTitle: SQS, visibility timeout, and dead-letter queues
saa-40-sqs-decoupling./summary: Understand SQS, visibility timeout, and dead-letter queues through requirements, constraints, trade-offs, and an architectural decision that can be explained to the BlueRetail team.
saa-40-sqs-decoupling./opening/0: BlueRetail is changing its architecture, and a slow payment service is causing the order API to time out and failed messages to be processed repeatedly. A diagram containing many AWS icons is not necessarily a good design: what matters is which requirement each component addresses, which failure modes remain, and whether the operations team can manage it. Lesson 40 starts with this scenario and turns SQS, visibility timeout, and dead-letter queues into an evidence-based sequence of decisions rather than a list of features to memorize.
saa-40-sqs-decoupling./opening/1: In SAA-C03, questions often present several technically viable options, but only one best matches keywords such as “least operational effort,” “no data loss,” “low cost,” “multiple accounts,” or “no public internet.” SQS, visibility timeout, and dead-letter queues should be evaluated from four perspectives: message queues, visibility timeout, dead-letter queues, and idempotency. Each perspective eliminates a group of answers that conflict with the requirements or introduce unacceptable responsibilities.
saa-40-sqs-decoupling./opening/2: The scope of Lesson 40—SQS, visibility timeout, and dead-letter queues—is aligned with objective SAA-C03 2.1 in the current AWS Exam Guide. The GitHub topic repository is used only to verify coverage and content structure; all explanations, cases, and diagrams here are original, while the technical conclusions are validated against AWS Documentation and the Well-Architected Framework. After this lesson, you should be able to explain not only “what to choose,” but also “why the other options were not chosen.”
saa-40-sqs-decoupling./sections/0/title: Mental model: start with requirements, not services
saa-40-sqs-decoupling./sections/0/paragraphs/0: Think of an AWS architecture as a contract among the business, application, and platform. The business defines what must remain operational and the acceptable level of loss; the application describes traffic, data, and dependencies; and the platform translates those requirements into boundaries, services, policies, and observability mechanisms. For SQS, visibility timeout, and dead-letter queues, the message queue is the entry point, visibility timeout determines how the system responds, and dead-letter queues and idempotency determine long-term operability. Moving directly from a requirement to a service name often causes one of these four layers to be overlooked.
saa-40-sqs-decoupling./sections/0/paragraphs/1: A compact model that works for every lesson is R-C-D-E: Requirement, Constraint, Decision, Evidence. A requirement describes an outcome; a constraint limits the scope through factors such as Region, budget, or skills; a decision is the selected architectural structure; and evidence consists of metrics, logs, tests, or documentation confirming that the decision remains valid. By applying R-C-D-E to SQS, visibility timeout, and dead-letter queues, learners can see that the same service may be correct for one workload but wrong for another. This skill is more important than memorizing the entire AWS catalog.
saa-40-sqs-decoupling./sections/0/paragraphs/2: You have understood Lesson 40 when you can trace a request or operational event from beginning to end. Which boundary does the request cross, which identity is evaluated, where is the data retained, which component can fail, and which mechanism provides recovery? If the story breaks at the message queue or visibility timeout, the diagram still contains an unverified assumption. Record that assumption beside the diagram before selecting another service.
saa-40-sqs-decoupling./sections/1/title: Four concepts that must be connected
saa-40-sqs-decoupling./sections/1/paragraphs/0: A message queue is the first component in a decision involving SQS, visibility timeout, and dead-letter queues, but its value becomes clear only when considered alongside business requirements. For SAA-C03, learners should not stop at “What is this service used for?” Identify the input received by the message queue, the state or policy it manages, the failure mode it handles, and the operational cost it introduces. At BlueRetail, a slow payment service causes the order API to time out and failed messages to be processed repeatedly. Connecting the message queue to visibility timeout reveals where the responsibility boundary lies and why two similarly named options can produce different outcomes. A strong solution must trace the technical choice back to latency, availability, security, data, or the capabilities of the operations team.
saa-40-sqs-decoupling./sections/1/paragraphs/1: Visibility timeout is the second component in a decision involving SQS, visibility timeout, and dead-letter queues, but its value becomes clear only when considered alongside business requirements. For SAA-C03, learners should not stop at “What is this service used for?” Identify the input received by visibility timeout, the state or policy it manages, the failure mode it handles, and the operational cost it introduces. At BlueRetail, a slow payment service causes the order API to time out and failed messages to be processed repeatedly. Connecting visibility timeout to the dead-letter queue reveals where the responsibility boundary lies and why two similarly named options can produce different outcomes. A strong solution must trace the technical choice back to latency, availability, security, data, or the capabilities of the operations team.
saa-40-sqs-decoupling./sections/1/paragraphs/2: A dead-letter queue is the third component in a decision involving SQS, visibility timeout, and dead-letter queues, but its value becomes clear only when considered alongside business requirements. For SAA-C03, learners should not stop at “What is this service used for?” Identify the input received by the dead-letter queue, the state or policy it manages, the failure mode it handles, and the operational cost it introduces. At BlueRetail, a slow payment service causes the order API to time out and failed messages to be processed repeatedly. Connecting the dead-letter queue to idempotency reveals where the responsibility boundary lies and why two similarly named options can produce different outcomes. A strong solution must trace the technical choice back to latency, availability, security, data, or the capabilities of the operations team.
saa-40-sqs-decoupling./sections/1/paragraphs/3: Idempotency is the fourth component in a decision involving SQS, visibility timeout, and dead-letter queues, but its value becomes clear only when considered alongside business requirements. For SAA-C03, learners should not stop at “What is this service used for?” Identify the input received by the idempotency mechanism, the state or policy it manages, the failure mode it handles, and the operational cost it introduces. At BlueRetail, a slow payment service causes the order API to time out and failed messages to be processed repeatedly. Connecting idempotency to the message queue reveals where the responsibility boundary lies and why two similarly named options can produce different outcomes. A strong solution must trace the technical choice back to latency, availability, security, data, or the capabilities of the operations team.
saa-40-sqs-decoupling./sections/2/title: Make decisions step by step and document the trade-offs
saa-40-sqs-decoupling./sections/2/paragraphs/0: A decision involving a message queue should be documented as a chain of reasoning, not as an isolated service name. First, confirm which requirements are mandatory, then check quota limits, Region, consistency, network path, and the responsibility model. Next, compare the message queue and dead-letter queue using the same criteria; do not compare one feature of option A with the total cost of option B. Finally, state the remaining trade-off and how it will be observed after implementation. In Lesson 40, this approach helps BlueRetail understand why the selected option is good enough for the current context and which signals would trigger an architectural review.
saa-40-sqs-decoupling./sections/2/paragraphs/1: A decision involving visibility timeout should be documented as a chain of reasoning, not as an isolated service name. First, confirm which requirements are mandatory, then check quota limits, Region, consistency, network path, and the responsibility model. Next, compare visibility timeout and idempotency using the same criteria; do not compare one feature of option A with the total cost of option B. Finally, state the remaining trade-off and how it will be observed after implementation. In Lesson 40, this approach helps BlueRetail understand why the selected option is good enough for the current context and which signals would trigger an architectural review.
saa-40-sqs-decoupling./sections/2/paragraphs/2: A decision involving a dead-letter queue should be documented as a chain of reasoning, not as an isolated service name. First, confirm which requirements are mandatory, then check quota limits, Region, consistency, network path, and the responsibility model. Next, compare the dead-letter queue and message queue using the same criteria; do not compare one feature of option A with the total cost of option B. Finally, state the remaining trade-off and how it will be observed after implementation. In Lesson 40, this approach helps BlueRetail understand why the selected option is good enough for the current context and which signals would trigger an architectural review.
saa-40-sqs-decoupling./sections/2/paragraphs/3: A decision involving idempotency should be documented as a chain of reasoning, not as an isolated service name. First, confirm which requirements are mandatory, then check quota limits, Region, consistency, network path, and the responsibility model. Next, compare idempotency and visibility timeout using the same criteria; do not compare one feature of option A with the total cost of option B. Finally, state the remaining trade-off and how it will be observed after implementation. In Lesson 40, this approach helps BlueRetail understand why the selected option is good enough for the current context and which signals would trigger an architectural review.
saa-40-sqs-decoupling./sections/3/title: Design for failure before it occurs
saa-40-sqs-decoupling./sections/3/paragraphs/0: Failure mode 1 for SQS, visibility timeout, and dead-letter queues occurs when the project team assumes that the message queue automatically addresses every related layer. In practice, service availability does not replace appropriate access permissions, replication does not replace backups, encryption does not automatically grant permission to use a key, and autoscaling cannot fix a stateful dependency. At BlueRetail, map the blast radius of the message queue, marking the areas for which AWS is responsible and those that the application team must still configure or test. Then select a metric, log, or event that can provide an early warning. Analyzing failure modes in advance ensures that the answer is valid not only while the system is healthy, but also provides a recovery path when an AZ, connection, credential, or operational process fails.
saa-40-sqs-decoupling./sections/3/paragraphs/1: Failure mode 2 for SQS, visibility timeout, and dead-letter queues occurs when the project team assumes that visibility timeout automatically addresses every related layer. In practice, service availability does not replace appropriate access permissions, replication does not replace backups, encryption does not automatically grant permission to use a key, and autoscaling cannot fix a stateful dependency. At BlueRetail, map the blast radius of visibility timeout, marking the areas for which AWS is responsible and those that the application team must still configure or test. Then select a metric, log, or event that can provide an early warning. Analyzing failure modes in advance ensures that the answer is valid not only while the system is healthy, but also provides a recovery path when an AZ, connection, credential, or operational process fails.
saa-40-sqs-decoupling./sections/3/paragraphs/2: Failure mode 3 for SQS, visibility timeout, and dead-letter queues occurs when the project team assumes that the dead-letter queue automatically addresses every related layer. In practice, service availability does not replace appropriate access permissions, replication does not replace backups, encryption does not automatically grant permission to use a key, and autoscaling cannot fix a stateful dependency. At BlueRetail, map the blast radius of the dead-letter queue, marking the areas for which AWS is responsible and those that the application team must still configure or test. Then select a metric, log, or event that can provide an early warning. Analyzing failure modes in advance ensures that the answer is valid not only while the system is healthy, but also provides a recovery path when an AZ, connection, credential, or operational process fails.
saa-40-sqs-decoupling./sections/4/title: Apply the decision to the BlueRetail case
saa-40-sqs-decoupling./sections/4/paragraphs/0: BlueRetail has stores, an e-commerce website, internal applications, and an operations team working across multiple accounts. In Lesson 40, a slow payment service causes the order API to time out and failed messages to be processed repeatedly. The first step is to separate the stakeholders: users need a stable experience, security needs boundaries and an audit trail, finance needs cost attribution, and the product team needs to make changes quickly. A design optimized for only one stakeholder creates operational debt for the others.
saa-40-sqs-decoupling./sections/4/paragraphs/1: The architecture team documents three options involving the message queue, visibility timeout, and dead-letter queue. Each option is scored using the same matrix: satisfaction of mandatory requirements, blast radius, recovery path, operational burden, scalability, and cost per unit of usage. Idempotency serves as the final validation condition. If an option wins only because the team is familiar with it, the decision lacks sufficient evidence and the constraints must be revisited.
saa-40-sqs-decoupling./sections/4/paragraphs/2: The conclusion for SQS, visibility timeout, and dead-letter queues is written in four sentences: which structure was selected; which requirements it satisfies; which trade-off is accepted; and which metric or review will trigger a reassessment. This format helps developers, security, and operations understand the same decision. In exam questions, the four sentences also help eliminate distractors: an option that fails a mandatory requirement is eliminated first, even if it offers many appealing features.
saa-40-sqs-decoupling./sections/5/title: Choosing the correct action in SAA-C03 questions
saa-40-sqs-decoupling./sections/5/paragraphs/0: Read an SAA-C03 question in this order: the assigned role, the current state, the required outcome, the constraints, and the verb in the final sentence. For SQS, visibility timeout, and dead-letter queues, mark every detail related to the message queue and visibility timeout. Then classify each answer as fully satisfying the requirements, partially satisfying them, introducing additional risk, or being out of scope. Do not choose an answer merely because it mentions the correct service; AWS tests your ability to combine multiple requirements into the most appropriate solution.
saa-40-sqs-decoupling./sections/5/paragraphs/1: Absolute phrases such as “no data loss,” “no downtime,” or “all traffic must remain private” require careful evaluation. They may demand a very different design from phrases such as “minimize,” “acceptable,” or “prioritize cost.” In Lesson 40, use the dead-letter queue to evaluate the required assurance level and idempotency to determine whether the operations team can sustain the option. An answer that provides the right feature but the wrong assurance level is still a distractor.
saa-40-sqs-decoupling./sections/5/paragraphs/2: In Lesson 40—SQS, visibility timeout, and dead-letter queues—objective SAA-C03 2.1 indicates the required depth. For design, you must balance quality attributes; for determine, you must interpret the facts and select a cause or option; for improve, you must preserve existing constraints while reducing risk through an appropriate change. Conclude each answer with evidence: a metric, log, policy evaluation, recovery test, or cost report. Evidence turns an answer from a guess into a defensible architectural decision.

saa-40-sqs-decoupling./activity/title: Architecture review: SQS, visibility timeout, and dead-letter queues
saa-40-sqs-decoupling./activity/context: BlueRetail is preparing to approve a change because a slow payment service causes the order API to time out and failed messages to be processed repeatedly. You must review the architecture before the team implements it or incurs a cost commitment.
saa-40-sqs-decoupling./activity/learnerRole: You are the Solutions Architect responsible for a workload, translating requirements into decisions and clearly communicating trade-offs to stakeholders.
saa-40-sqs-decoupling./activity/currentArchitecture: Users → access layer → workload → data store; the review points are message queue → visibility timeout → dead-letter queue → idempotency.
saa-40-sqs-decoupling./activity/requirements/0: The solution must correctly address the requirement related to the message queue.
saa-40-sqs-decoupling./activity/requirements/1: The recovery path must demonstrate the role of visibility timeout.
saa-40-sqs-decoupling./activity/requirements/2: Security and operations must be able to observe the dead-letter queue without relying on assumptions.
saa-40-sqs-decoupling./activity/constraints/0: Do not add a service merely to conceal a requirement that has not been clearly defined.
saa-40-sqs-decoupling./activity/constraints/1: The idempotency trade-off must be accepted by stakeholders.
saa-40-sqs-decoupling./activity/constraints/2: Do not change the storage schema or account boundary without an architectural justification.
saa-40-sqs-decoupling./activity/tasks/0: Rewrite the requirement as measurable criteria.
saa-40-sqs-decoupling./activity/tasks/1: Compare at least two options based on the message queue, visibility timeout, and dead-letter queue.
saa-40-sqs-decoupling./activity/tasks/2: Select an option, document the trade-off, and propose post-implementation evidence.
saa-40-sqs-decoupling./activity/reasoningSteps/0: Separate the business outcome, technical constraints, and unverified assumptions.
saa-40-sqs-decoupling./activity/reasoningSteps/1: Draw the request and data flow, including the trust boundary and blast radius.
saa-40-sqs-decoupling./activity/reasoningSteps/2: Eliminate options that fail mandatory requirements before optimizing.
saa-40-sqs-decoupling./activity/reasoningSteps/3: Compare the remaining options across reliability, security, performance, cost, and operations.
saa-40-sqs-decoupling./activity/reasoningSteps/4: Document the decision, trade-off, recovery path, and review metric.
saa-40-sqs-decoupling./activity/deliverables/0: An architecture diagram showing boundaries and data flow direction.
saa-40-sqs-decoupling./activity/deliverables/1: A decision matrix containing at least two options.
saa-40-sqs-decoupling./activity/deliverables/2: A brief architecture decision record containing the trade-off and evidence.
saa-40-sqs-decoupling./activity/rubric/0/evidence: The decision can be traced back to the message queue and introduces no assumptions beyond the scenario.
saa-40-sqs-decoupling./activity/rubric/0/importance: Prevents services from being selected based on preference or an isolated keyword.
saa-40-sqs-decoupling./activity/rubric/1/evidence: Compares visibility timeout, the dead-letter queue, and idempotency using the same criteria.
saa-40-sqs-decoupling./activity/rubric/1/importance: AWS architecture always involves balance; no option is universally best.
saa-40-sqs-decoupling./activity/rubric/2/evidence: The submission identifies a failure mode, recovery path, and validating metric or test.
saa-40-sqs-decoupling./activity/rubric/2/importance: A design is trustworthy only if it can be operated and validated after implementation.
saa-40-sqs-decoupling./activity/sampleAnalysis/0: The primary requirement is rewritten around the message queue; visibility timeout is a constraint that directly affects the choice.
saa-40-sqs-decoupling./activity/sampleAnalysis/1: The option prioritizing the dead-letter queue is eliminated if it increases the operational burden of idempotency without adding value.
saa-40-sqs-decoupling./activity/sampleAnalysis/2: The proposed architecture combines the message queue and visibility timeout and clearly documents the blast radius, recovery path, and review metric.
saa-40-sqs-decoupling./activity/sampleAnalysis/3: The remaining trade-off is accepted because it fits the situation in which a slow payment service causes the order API to time out and failed messages to be processed repeatedly.
saa-40-sqs-decoupling./activity/commonMistakes/0: Listing services without connecting them to the requirement.
saa-40-sqs-decoupling./activity/commonMistakes/1: Describing only the happy path and ignoring failure modes.
saa-40-sqs-decoupling./activity/commonMistakes/2: Saying that “AWS manages it” without identifying the customer's responsibilities.
