saa-41-sns-eventbridge./summary: Understand sns fan-out and eventbridge routing through requirements, constraints, trade-offs, and an architectural decision that can be clearly explained to the BlueRetail team.
saa-41-sns-eventbridge./opening/0: BlueRetail is changing its architecture, and a single order event must reach email, warehouse, analytics, and partner systems under different routing rules. A diagram filled with AWS icons is not necessarily a good design. What matters is which requirement each component addresses, which failure modes remain, and whether the operations team can manage it. Lesson 41 starts with this scenario and turns sns fan-out and eventbridge routing into a sequence of evidence-based decisions rather than a list of features to memorize.
saa-41-sns-eventbridge./opening/1: In SAA-C03, questions often present several technically feasible options, but only one best matches keywords such as “least operational effort,” “no data loss,” “low cost,” “multiple accounts,” or “no public internet.” SNS fan-out and EventBridge routing should be considered from four perspectives: publish-subscribe, fan-out, event bus, and event pattern. Each perspective eliminates a group of answers that conflict with the requirements or introduce unacceptable responsibilities.
saa-41-sns-eventbridge./opening/2: The scope of Lesson 41—SNS fan-out and EventBridge routing—is aligned with objective SAA-C03 2.1 in the current AWS Exam Guide. The GitHub topic repository is used only to verify coverage and content organization. All explanations, cases, and diagrams here are newly written, while the technical conclusions are validated against AWS Documentation and the Well-Architected Framework. After this lesson, you should be able to explain not only “what to choose,” but also “why the other options were not chosen.”
saa-41-sns-eventbridge./sections/0/title: Mental model: start with requirements, not services
saa-41-sns-eventbridge./sections/0/paragraphs/0: Treat an AWS architecture as a contract among business, application, and platform teams. The business defines what must remain operational and what level of loss is acceptable; the application defines traffic, data, and dependencies; and the platform translates those requirements into boundaries, services, policies, and observability mechanisms. For sns fan-out and eventbridge routing, publish-subscribe is the entry point, fan-out defines how the system responds, and the event bus and event pattern determine long-term operability. Going directly from a requirement to a service name often causes one of these four layers to be overlooked.
saa-41-sns-eventbridge./sections/0/paragraphs/1: A simple model that works for any lesson is R-C-D-E: Requirement, Constraint, Decision, Evidence. A requirement describes the outcome; a constraint limits the scope through factors such as Region, budget, or skills; a decision is the selected architectural structure; and evidence is a metric, log, test, or document confirming that the decision remains valid. Applying R-C-D-E to SNS fan-out and EventBridge routing shows learners that the same service can be right for one workload and wrong for another. This skill is more important than memorizing the entire AWS catalog.
saa-41-sns-eventbridge./sections/0/paragraphs/2: You have understood Lesson 41 when you can trace a request or operational event from beginning to end. Which boundary does the request cross, which identity is evaluated, where is the data retained, which component can fail, and what mechanism provides recovery? If the story breaks at publish-subscribe or fan-out, the diagram still contains an unverified assumption. Record that assumption beside the diagram before selecting another service.
saa-41-sns-eventbridge./sections/1/paragraphs/0: publish-subscribe is the first component in the sns fan-out and eventbridge routing decision, but its value appears only when considered alongside business requirements. For SAA-C03, learners should not stop at “what is this service used for?” Identify the input received by publish-subscribe, the state or policy it manages, the failure mode it addresses, and the operational cost it introduces. At BlueRetail, a single order event must reach email, warehouse, analytics, and partner systems under different routing rules. Connecting publish-subscribe to fan-out reveals where the responsibility boundary lies and why two similarly named options can produce different outcomes. A strong solution must trace the technical choice back to latency, availability, security, data, or the capabilities of the operations team.
saa-41-sns-eventbridge./sections/1/paragraphs/1: fan-out is the second component in the sns fan-out and eventbridge routing decision, but its value appears only when considered alongside business requirements. For SAA-C03, learners should not stop at “what is this service used for?” Identify the input received by fan-out, the state or policy it manages, the failure mode it addresses, and the operational cost it introduces. At BlueRetail, a single order event must reach email, warehouse, analytics, and partner systems under different routing rules. Connecting fan-out to the event bus reveals where the responsibility boundary lies and why two similarly named options can produce different outcomes. A strong solution must trace the technical choice back to latency, availability, security, data, or the capabilities of the operations team.
saa-41-sns-eventbridge./sections/1/paragraphs/2: event bus is the third component in the sns fan-out and eventbridge routing decision, but its value appears only when considered alongside business requirements. For SAA-C03, learners should not stop at “what is this service used for?” Identify the input received by the event bus, the state or policy it manages, the failure mode it addresses, and the operational cost it introduces. At BlueRetail, a single order event must reach email, warehouse, analytics, and partner systems under different routing rules. Connecting the event bus to the event pattern reveals where the responsibility boundary lies and why two similarly named options can produce different outcomes. A strong solution must trace the technical choice back to latency, availability, security, data, or the capabilities of the operations team.
saa-41-sns-eventbridge./sections/1/paragraphs/3: event pattern is the fourth component in the sns fan-out and eventbridge routing decision, but its value appears only when considered alongside business requirements. For SAA-C03, learners should not stop at “what is this service used for?” Identify the input received by the event pattern, the state or policy it manages, the failure mode it addresses, and the operational cost it introduces. At BlueRetail, a single order event must reach email, warehouse, analytics, and partner systems under different routing rules. Connecting the event pattern to publish-subscribe reveals where the responsibility boundary lies and why two similarly named options can produce different outcomes. A strong solution must trace the technical choice back to latency, availability, security, data, or the capabilities of the operations team.
saa-41-sns-eventbridge./sections/2/title: Make decisions step by step and document the trade-offs
saa-41-sns-eventbridge./sections/2/paragraphs/0: A decision involving publish-subscribe should be documented as a chain of reasoning, not as an isolated service name. First, confirm which requirements are mandatory, then check quota limits, Region, consistency, network path, and the responsibility model. Next, compare publish-subscribe with the event bus using the same criteria; do not compare one feature of option A with the total cost of option B. Finally, state the remaining trade-off and how it will be observed after deployment. In Lesson 41, this approach helps BlueRetail understand why the selected option is good enough in the current context and which signals would trigger an architectural review.
saa-41-sns-eventbridge./sections/2/paragraphs/1: A decision involving fan-out should be documented as a chain of reasoning, not as an isolated service name. First, confirm which requirements are mandatory, then check quota limits, Region, consistency, network path, and the responsibility model. Next, compare fan-out with the event pattern using the same criteria; do not compare one feature of option A with the total cost of option B. Finally, state the remaining trade-off and how it will be observed after deployment. In Lesson 41, this approach helps BlueRetail understand why the selected option is good enough in the current context and which signals would trigger an architectural review.
saa-41-sns-eventbridge./sections/2/paragraphs/2: A decision involving an event bus should be documented as a chain of reasoning, not as an isolated service name. First, confirm which requirements are mandatory, then check quota limits, Region, consistency, network path, and the responsibility model. Next, compare the event bus with publish-subscribe using the same criteria; do not compare one feature of option A with the total cost of option B. Finally, state the remaining trade-off and how it will be observed after deployment. In Lesson 41, this approach helps BlueRetail understand why the selected option is good enough in the current context and which signals would trigger an architectural review.
saa-41-sns-eventbridge./sections/2/paragraphs/3: A decision involving an event pattern should be documented as a chain of reasoning, not as an isolated service name. First, confirm which requirements are mandatory, then check quota limits, Region, consistency, network path, and the responsibility model. Next, compare the event pattern with fan-out using the same criteria; do not compare one feature of option A with the total cost of option B. Finally, state the remaining trade-off and how it will be observed after deployment. In Lesson 41, this approach helps BlueRetail understand why the selected option is good enough in the current context and which signals would trigger an architectural review.
saa-41-sns-eventbridge./sections/3/title: Design for failure before it occurs
saa-41-sns-eventbridge./sections/3/paragraphs/0: Failure mode 1 for sns fan-out and eventbridge routing occurs when the project team assumes that publish-subscribe automatically handles every related layer. In reality, service availability does not replace correct access permissions, replication does not replace backup, encryption does not automatically grant permission to use a key, and autoscaling cannot fix a stateful dependency. At BlueRetail, map the blast radius of publish-subscribe and mark what AWS is responsible for and what the application team must still configure or test. Then select a metric, log, or event that can provide an early warning. Analyzing the failure mode first ensures that the answer is valid not only while the system is healthy, but also provides a recovery path when an AZ, connection, credential, or operational process fails.
saa-41-sns-eventbridge./sections/3/paragraphs/1: Failure mode 2 for sns fan-out and eventbridge routing occurs when the project team assumes that fan-out automatically handles every related layer. In reality, service availability does not replace correct access permissions, replication does not replace backup, encryption does not automatically grant permission to use a key, and autoscaling cannot fix a stateful dependency. At BlueRetail, map the blast radius of fan-out and mark what AWS is responsible for and what the application team must still configure or test. Then select a metric, log, or event that can provide an early warning. Analyzing the failure mode first ensures that the answer is valid not only while the system is healthy, but also provides a recovery path when an AZ, connection, credential, or operational process fails.
saa-41-sns-eventbridge./sections/3/paragraphs/2: Failure mode 3 for sns fan-out and eventbridge routing occurs when the project team assumes that the event bus automatically handles every related layer. In reality, service availability does not replace correct access permissions, replication does not replace backup, encryption does not automatically grant permission to use a key, and autoscaling cannot fix a stateful dependency. At BlueRetail, map the blast radius of the event bus and mark what AWS is responsible for and what the application team must still configure or test. Then select a metric, log, or event that can provide an early warning. Analyzing the failure mode first ensures that the answer is valid not only while the system is healthy, but also provides a recovery path when an AZ, connection, credential, or operational process fails.
saa-41-sns-eventbridge./sections/4/title: Apply the decision to the BlueRetail case
saa-41-sns-eventbridge./sections/4/paragraphs/0: BlueRetail has stores, an e-commerce website, internal applications, and an operations team working across multiple accounts. In Lesson 41, a single order event must reach email, warehouse, analytics, and partner systems under different routing rules. The first step is to separate the stakeholders: users need a stable experience, security needs boundaries and an audit trail, finance needs cost attribution, and the product team needs to make changes quickly. A design optimized for only one stakeholder creates operational debt for another.
saa-41-sns-eventbridge./sections/4/paragraphs/1: The architecture team documents three options centered on publish-subscribe, fan-out, and the event bus. Each option is scored using the same matrix: fulfillment of mandatory requirements, blast radius, recovery path, operational burden, scalability, and cost per unit of usage. The event pattern serves as the final validation condition. If an option wins only because the team is familiar with it, the decision lacks sufficient evidence and the team must revisit the constraints.
saa-41-sns-eventbridge./sections/4/paragraphs/2: The conclusion for sns fan-out and eventbridge routing is written in four sentences: which structure is selected; which requirements it satisfies; which trade-off is accepted; and which metric or review will trigger reevaluation. This format helps developers, security, and operations understand the same decision. In exam questions, the four sentences also help eliminate distractors: an option that fails a mandatory requirement is eliminated first, even if it offers many attractive features.
saa-41-sns-eventbridge./sections/5/title: Choosing the right action in SAA-C03 questions
saa-41-sns-eventbridge./sections/5/paragraphs/0: Read an SAA-C03 question in this order: the assigned role, the current state, the required outcome, the constraints, and the verb in the final sentence. For sns fan-out and eventbridge routing, mark every detail related to publish-subscribe and fan-out. Then classify each answer as fully satisfying the requirements, partially satisfying them, introducing additional risk, or falling outside the scope. Do not select an answer merely because it names the correct service; AWS tests your ability to combine multiple requirements into the most appropriate solution.
saa-41-sns-eventbridge./sections/5/paragraphs/1: Absolute terms such as “no data loss,” “no downtime,” or “all traffic must remain private” require careful evaluation. They may demand a very different design from terms such as “minimize,” “acceptable,” or “prioritize cost.” In Lesson 41, use the event bus to evaluate the required assurance level and the event pattern to determine whether the operations team can maintain the option. An answer that provides the right feature but the wrong assurance level is still a distractor.
saa-41-sns-eventbridge./sections/5/paragraphs/2: In Lesson 41—SNS fan-out and EventBridge routing—objective SAA-C03 2.1 indicates the required depth. For design, you must balance quality attributes; for determine, you must interpret the facts and select a cause or option; for improve, you must preserve existing constraints while reducing risk through an appropriate change. End each answer with evidence: a metric, log, policy evaluation, recovery test, or cost report. Evidence turns an answer from a guess into a defensible architectural decision.

saa-41-sns-eventbridge./activity/title: Architecture review: SNS fan-out and EventBridge routing
saa-41-sns-eventbridge./activity/context: BlueRetail is preparing to approve a change because a single order event must reach email, warehouse, analytics, and partner systems under different routing rules. You must review the architecture before the team implements it or incurs cost commitments.
saa-41-sns-eventbridge./activity/learnerRole: You are the Solutions Architect responsible for a workload, translating requirements into a decision and clearly explaining the trade-offs to stakeholders.
saa-41-sns-eventbridge./activity/currentArchitecture: Users → access layer → workload → data store; the review points are publish-subscribe → fan-out → event bus → event pattern.
saa-41-sns-eventbridge./activity/requirements/0: The solution must correctly address the requirement related to publish-subscribe.
saa-41-sns-eventbridge./activity/requirements/1: The recovery path must show the role of fan-out.
saa-41-sns-eventbridge./activity/requirements/2: Security and operations must be able to observe the event bus without relying on assumptions.
saa-41-sns-eventbridge./activity/constraints/0: Do not add a service merely to hide a requirement that has not been clearly defined.
saa-41-sns-eventbridge./activity/constraints/1: The trade-off involving the event pattern must be accepted by stakeholders.
saa-41-sns-eventbridge./activity/constraints/2: Do not change the storage schema or account boundary without an architectural reason.
saa-41-sns-eventbridge./activity/tasks/0: Rewrite the requirement as measurable evaluation criteria.
saa-41-sns-eventbridge./activity/tasks/1: Compare at least two options based on publish-subscribe, fan-out, and the event bus.
saa-41-sns-eventbridge./activity/tasks/2: Select an option, document the trade-off, and propose post-deployment evidence.
saa-41-sns-eventbridge./activity/reasoningSteps/0: Separate the business outcome, technical constraints, and unverified assumptions.
saa-41-sns-eventbridge./activity/reasoningSteps/1: Draw the request and data flow together with trust boundaries and the blast radius.
saa-41-sns-eventbridge./activity/reasoningSteps/2: Eliminate options that fail mandatory requirements before optimizing.
saa-41-sns-eventbridge./activity/reasoningSteps/3: Compare the remaining options across reliability, security, performance, cost, and operations.
saa-41-sns-eventbridge./activity/reasoningSteps/4: Document the decision, trade-off, recovery path, and review metric.

saa-41-sns-eventbridge./activity/deliverables/2: A brief architecture decision record containing the trade-off and evidence.
saa-41-sns-eventbridge./activity/rubric/0/evidence: The decision can be traced back to publish-subscribe and introduces no assumptions beyond the question.
saa-41-sns-eventbridge./activity/rubric/0/importance: Prevents selecting services based on preference or an isolated keyword.
saa-41-sns-eventbridge./activity/rubric/1/evidence: Compares fan-out, the event bus, and the event pattern using the same criteria.
saa-41-sns-eventbridge./activity/rubric/1/importance: AWS architecture always involves balancing factors; there is no universally best option.
saa-41-sns-eventbridge./activity/rubric/2/evidence: The submission identifies a failure mode, recovery path, and validating metric or test.
saa-41-sns-eventbridge./activity/rubric/2/importance: A design is trustworthy only when it can be operated and validated after deployment.

saa-41-sns-eventbridge./activity/sampleAnalysis/2: The proposed architecture combines publish-subscribe and fan-out and clearly documents the blast radius, recovery path, and review metric.
saa-41-sns-eventbridge./activity/sampleAnalysis/3: The remaining trade-off is accepted because it fits the scenario in which a single order event must reach email, warehouse, analytics, and partner systems under different routing rules.

saa-42-step-functions-workflows./summary: Understand Step Functions and stateful workflows through requirements, constraints, trade-offs, and an architecture decision that can be explained to the BlueRetail team.
saa-42-step-functions-workflows./opening/0: BlueRetail is changing its architecture and its multistep refund process, which has timeouts and requires compensation if the inventory update succeeds but the payment fails. A diagram with many AWS icons is not necessarily a good design: what matters is which requirement each component addresses, which failure modes remain, and whether the operations team can manage it. Lesson 42 starts with this scenario and turns Step Functions and stateful workflows into a sequence of evidence-based decisions rather than a list of features to memorize.
saa-42-step-functions-workflows./opening/1: In SAA-C03, questions often present several technically feasible options, but only one best matches keywords such as “least operational effort,” “no data loss,” “low cost,” “multiple accounts,” or “no public internet.” Step Functions and stateful workflows should be assessed from four perspectives: state machine, retry policy, compensation, and workflow history. Each perspective eliminates a group of answers if it conflicts with a requirement or introduces unacceptable responsibilities.
saa-42-step-functions-workflows./opening/2: The scope of Lesson 42—Step Functions and stateful workflows—is aligned with objective SAA-C03 2.1 in the current AWS Exam Guide. The GitHub topic repository is used only to verify coverage and content organization; all explanations, cases, and diagrams here are newly written, while the technical conclusions are validated against AWS Documentation and the Well-Architected Framework. After this lesson, you should be able to explain not only “what to choose,” but also “why the other options should not be chosen.”
saa-42-step-functions-workflows./sections/0/title: Mental model: start with requirements, not services
saa-42-step-functions-workflows./sections/0/paragraphs/0: Think of an AWS architecture as a contract among business, application, and platform teams. The business defines what must remain operational and what level of loss is acceptable; the application describes traffic, data, and dependencies; the platform translates those requirements into boundaries, services, policies, and observability mechanisms. For Step Functions and stateful workflows, the state machine is the entry point, the retry policy defines how the system responds, and compensation and workflow history determine long-term operability. Going directly from a requirement to a service name often causes one of these four layers to be overlooked.
saa-42-step-functions-workflows./sections/0/paragraphs/1: A compact model that works for any lesson is R-C-D-E: Requirement, Constraint, Decision, Evidence. A requirement describes the outcome; a constraint limits the scope through factors such as Region, budget, or skills; a decision is the selected architecture; and evidence is a metric, log, test, or document confirming that the decision remains valid. By applying R-C-D-E to Step Functions and stateful workflows, learners can see that the same service may be right for one workload but wrong for another. This skill is more important than memorizing the entire AWS catalog.
saa-42-step-functions-workflows./sections/0/paragraphs/2: You have understood Lesson 42 when you can trace a request or operational event from beginning to end. Which boundary does the request cross, which identity is evaluated, where is the data retained, which component can fail, and which mechanism provides recovery? If the story breaks at the state machine or retry policy, the diagram still contains an unvalidated assumption. Record that assumption beside the diagram before selecting another service.
saa-42-step-functions-workflows./sections/1/title: Four concepts that must be connected
saa-42-step-functions-workflows./sections/1/paragraphs/0: The state machine is the first element in a Step Functions and stateful workflow decision, but its value becomes clear only when considered alongside the business requirement. For SAA-C03, learners should not stop at “What is this service used for?” Identify the input received by the state machine, the state or policy it manages, the failure mode it handles, and the resulting operational cost. At BlueRetail, the refund process has multiple steps and timeouts, and requires compensation if the inventory update succeeds but the payment fails. Connecting the state machine to the retry policy clarifies the responsibility boundary and explains why two similarly named options can produce different outcomes. A good solution must be traceable from the technical choice back to latency, availability, security, data, or the operations team’s capabilities.
saa-42-step-functions-workflows./sections/1/paragraphs/1: The retry policy is the second element in a Step Functions and stateful workflow decision, but its value becomes clear only when considered alongside the business requirement. For SAA-C03, learners should not stop at “What is this service used for?” Identify the input received by the retry policy, the state or policy it manages, the failure mode it handles, and the resulting operational cost. At BlueRetail, the refund process has multiple steps and timeouts, and requires compensation if the inventory update succeeds but the payment fails. Connecting the retry policy to compensation clarifies the responsibility boundary and explains why two similarly named options can produce different outcomes. A good solution must be traceable from the technical choice back to latency, availability, security, data, or the operations team’s capabilities.
saa-42-step-functions-workflows./sections/1/paragraphs/2: Compensation is the third element in a Step Functions and stateful workflow decision, but its value becomes clear only when considered alongside the business requirement. For SAA-C03, learners should not stop at “What is this service used for?” Identify the input received by the compensation mechanism, the state or policy it manages, the failure mode it handles, and the resulting operational cost. At BlueRetail, the refund process has multiple steps and timeouts, and requires compensation if the inventory update succeeds but the payment fails. Connecting compensation to workflow history clarifies the responsibility boundary and explains why two similarly named options can produce different outcomes. A good solution must be traceable from the technical choice back to latency, availability, security, data, or the operations team’s capabilities.
saa-42-step-functions-workflows./sections/1/paragraphs/3: Workflow history is the fourth element in a Step Functions and stateful workflow decision, but its value becomes clear only when considered alongside the business requirement. For SAA-C03, learners should not stop at “What is this service used for?” Identify the input received by workflow history, the state or policy it manages, the failure mode it handles, and the resulting operational cost. At BlueRetail, the refund process has multiple steps and timeouts, and requires compensation if the inventory update succeeds but the payment fails. Connecting workflow history to the state machine clarifies the responsibility boundary and explains why two similarly named options can produce different outcomes. A good solution must be traceable from the technical choice back to latency, availability, security, data, or the operations team’s capabilities.
saa-42-step-functions-workflows./sections/2/title: Make decisions step by step and document the trade-offs
saa-42-step-functions-workflows./sections/2/paragraphs/0: A decision involving a state machine should be documented as a chain of reasoning, not as an isolated service name. First, confirm which requirements are mandatory, then check quota limits, Region, consistency, network path, and the responsibility model. Next, compare the state machine with compensation using the same criteria; do not compare one feature of option A with the total cost of option B. Finally, state the remaining trade-off and how it will be observed after deployment. In Lesson 42, this approach helps BlueRetail understand why the selected option is good enough for the current context and which signals would trigger an architecture review.
saa-42-step-functions-workflows./sections/2/paragraphs/1: A decision involving a retry policy should be documented as a chain of reasoning, not as an isolated service name. First, confirm which requirements are mandatory, then check quota limits, Region, consistency, network path, and the responsibility model. Next, compare the retry policy with workflow history using the same criteria; do not compare one feature of option A with the total cost of option B. Finally, state the remaining trade-off and how it will be observed after deployment. In Lesson 42, this approach helps BlueRetail understand why the selected option is good enough for the current context and which signals would trigger an architecture review.
saa-42-step-functions-workflows./sections/2/paragraphs/2: A decision involving compensation should be documented as a chain of reasoning, not as an isolated service name. First, confirm which requirements are mandatory, then check quota limits, Region, consistency, network path, and the responsibility model. Next, compare compensation with the state machine using the same criteria; do not compare one feature of option A with the total cost of option B. Finally, state the remaining trade-off and how it will be observed after deployment. In Lesson 42, this approach helps BlueRetail understand why the selected option is good enough for the current context and which signals would trigger an architecture review.
saa-42-step-functions-workflows./sections/2/paragraphs/3: A decision involving workflow history should be documented as a chain of reasoning, not as an isolated service name. First, confirm which requirements are mandatory, then check quota limits, Region, consistency, network path, and the responsibility model. Next, compare workflow history with the retry policy using the same criteria; do not compare one feature of option A with the total cost of option B. Finally, state the remaining trade-off and how it will be observed after deployment. In Lesson 42, this approach helps BlueRetail understand why the selected option is good enough for the current context and which signals would trigger an architecture review.
saa-42-step-functions-workflows./sections/3/title: Design for failure before it occurs
saa-42-step-functions-workflows./sections/3/paragraphs/0: Failure mode 1 for Step Functions and stateful workflows occurs when the project team assumes that the state machine automatically addresses every related layer. In practice, service availability does not replace correct access permissions, replication does not replace backups, encryption does not automatically grant permission to use a key, and autoscaling does not fix a stateful dependency. At BlueRetail, draw the blast radius of the state machine and mark what AWS is responsible for and what the application team must still configure or test. Then select a metric, log, or event that can provide early warning. Analyzing failure modes first ensures that the answer is not only correct when the system is healthy, but also provides a recovery path when an AZ, connection, credential, or operational process fails.
saa-42-step-functions-workflows./sections/3/paragraphs/1: Failure mode 2 for Step Functions and stateful workflows occurs when the project team assumes that the retry policy automatically addresses every related layer. In practice, service availability does not replace correct access permissions, replication does not replace backups, encryption does not automatically grant permission to use a key, and autoscaling does not fix a stateful dependency. At BlueRetail, draw the blast radius of the retry policy and mark what AWS is responsible for and what the application team must still configure or test. Then select a metric, log, or event that can provide early warning. Analyzing failure modes first ensures that the answer is not only correct when the system is healthy, but also provides a recovery path when an AZ, connection, credential, or operational process fails.
saa-42-step-functions-workflows./sections/3/paragraphs/2: Failure mode 3 for Step Functions and stateful workflows occurs when the project team assumes that compensation automatically addresses every related layer. In practice, service availability does not replace correct access permissions, replication does not replace backups, encryption does not automatically grant permission to use a key, and autoscaling does not fix a stateful dependency. At BlueRetail, draw the blast radius of compensation and mark what AWS is responsible for and what the application team must still configure or test. Then select a metric, log, or event that can provide early warning. Analyzing failure modes first ensures that the answer is not only correct when the system is healthy, but also provides a recovery path when an AZ, connection, credential, or operational process fails.
saa-42-step-functions-workflows./sections/4/title: Apply the decision to the BlueRetail case
saa-42-step-functions-workflows./sections/4/paragraphs/0: BlueRetail has physical stores, an ecommerce website, internal applications, and an operations team working across multiple accounts. In Lesson 42, the refund process has multiple steps and timeouts, and requires compensation if the inventory update succeeds but the payment fails. The first step is to separate the stakeholders: users need a stable experience, security needs boundaries and an audit trail, finance needs cost attribution, and the product team needs to make changes quickly. A design optimized for only one stakeholder will create operational debt for another.
saa-42-step-functions-workflows./sections/4/paragraphs/1: The architecture team documents three options centered on the state machine, retry policy, and compensation. Each option is scored using the same table: satisfaction of mandatory requirements, blast radius, recovery path, operational burden, scalability, and cost per unit of usage. Workflow history serves as the final validation criterion. If an option wins only because the team is familiar with it, the decision lacks sufficient evidence and the constraints must be revisited.
saa-42-step-functions-workflows./sections/4/paragraphs/2: The conclusion for Step Functions and stateful workflows is written in four sentences: which structure is selected; which requirements it satisfies; which trade-off is accepted; and which metric or review will trigger reevaluation. This format helps developers, security, and operations understand the same decision. In exam questions, the four sentences also help eliminate distractors: an option that does not satisfy a mandatory requirement is eliminated first, even if it offers many attractive features.
saa-42-step-functions-workflows./sections/5/title: Choosing the right action in SAA-C03 questions
saa-42-step-functions-workflows./sections/5/paragraphs/0: Read an SAA-C03 question in this order: the assigned role, current state, mandatory outcome, constraints, and the verb at the end of the question. For Step Functions and stateful workflows, mark every detail related to the state machine and retry policy. Then classify the answers as fully satisfying the requirements, partially satisfying them, introducing additional risk, or being out of scope. Do not choose an answer merely because it mentions the correct service; AWS tests your ability to combine multiple requirements into the most appropriate solution.
saa-42-step-functions-workflows./sections/5/paragraphs/1: Absolute terms such as “no data loss,” “no downtime,” or “all traffic must remain private” require careful evaluation. They may require a substantially different design from terms such as “minimize,” “acceptable,” or “prioritize cost.” In Lesson 42, use compensation to verify the assurance level and workflow history to determine whether the operations team can sustain the option. An answer that is functionally correct but provides the wrong assurance level is still a distractor.
saa-42-step-functions-workflows./sections/5/paragraphs/2: In Lesson 42—Step Functions and stateful workflows—objective SAA-C03 2.1 indicates the required depth. For design, you must balance quality attributes; for determine, you must interpret the facts and select a cause or option; for improve, you must preserve existing constraints and reduce risk through an appropriate change. End each answer with evidence: a metric, log, policy evaluation, recovery test, or cost report. Evidence turns an answer from a guess into a defensible architecture decision.

saa-42-step-functions-workflows./activity/title: Architecture review: Step Functions and stateful workflows
saa-42-step-functions-workflows./activity/context: BlueRetail is preparing to approve a change because the refund process has multiple steps and timeouts, and requires compensation if the inventory update succeeds but the payment fails. You must review the architecture before the team implements it or incurs a cost commitment.
saa-42-step-functions-workflows./activity/learnerRole: You are the Solutions Architect responsible for a workload, translating requirements into decisions and clearly communicating trade-offs to stakeholders.
saa-42-step-functions-workflows./activity/currentArchitecture: User → access layer → workload → data store; the review points are state machine → retry policy → compensation → workflow history.
saa-42-step-functions-workflows./activity/requirements/0: The solution must correctly address the requirement related to the state machine.
saa-42-step-functions-workflows./activity/requirements/1: The recovery path must show the role of the retry policy.
saa-42-step-functions-workflows./activity/requirements/2: Security and operations must be able to observe compensation without relying on assumptions.
saa-42-step-functions-workflows./activity/constraints/0: Do not add a service merely to conceal a requirement that has not been clearly defined.
saa-42-step-functions-workflows./activity/constraints/1: Stakeholders must accept the workflow history trade-off.
saa-42-step-functions-workflows./activity/constraints/2: Do not change the storage schema or account boundary without an architectural reason.
saa-42-step-functions-workflows./activity/tasks/0: Rewrite the requirement as measurable criteria.
saa-42-step-functions-workflows./activity/tasks/1: Compare at least two options based on the state machine, retry policy, and compensation.
saa-42-step-functions-workflows./activity/tasks/2: Select an option, document the trade-off, and propose post-deployment evidence.
saa-42-step-functions-workflows./activity/reasoningSteps/0: Separate the business outcome, technical constraints, and unvalidated assumptions.
saa-42-step-functions-workflows./activity/reasoningSteps/1: Draw the request and data flow, including the trust boundary and blast radius.
saa-42-step-functions-workflows./activity/reasoningSteps/2: Eliminate options that fail mandatory requirements before optimizing.
saa-42-step-functions-workflows./activity/reasoningSteps/3: Compare the remaining options across reliability, security, performance, cost, and operations.
saa-42-step-functions-workflows./activity/reasoningSteps/4: Document the decision, trade-off, recovery path, and review metric.
saa-42-step-functions-workflows./activity/deliverables/0: An architecture diagram showing boundaries and data flow direction.
saa-42-step-functions-workflows./activity/deliverables/1: A decision matrix with at least two options.
saa-42-step-functions-workflows./activity/deliverables/2: A concise architecture decision record containing trade-offs and evidence.
saa-42-step-functions-workflows./activity/rubric/0/evidence: The decision can be traced back to the state machine and introduces no assumptions beyond the question.
saa-42-step-functions-workflows./activity/rubric/0/importance: Prevents selecting a service based on preference or a single keyword.
saa-42-step-functions-workflows./activity/rubric/1/evidence: Compares the retry policy, compensation, and workflow history using the same criteria.
saa-42-step-functions-workflows./activity/rubric/1/importance: AWS architecture always involves balance; there is no universally best option.
saa-42-step-functions-workflows./activity/rubric/2/evidence: The submission identifies a failure mode, recovery path, and validating metric or test.
saa-42-step-functions-workflows./activity/rubric/2/importance: A design is trustworthy only when it can be operated and validated after deployment.
saa-42-step-functions-workflows./activity/sampleAnalysis/0: The primary requirement is rewritten around the state machine; the retry policy is a constraint that directly affects the choice.
saa-42-step-functions-workflows./activity/sampleAnalysis/1: An option that prioritizes compensation is eliminated if it increases the operational burden of workflow history without adding value.
saa-42-step-functions-workflows./activity/sampleAnalysis/2: The proposed architecture combines the state machine and retry policy, and clearly documents the blast radius, recovery path, and review metric.
saa-42-step-functions-workflows./activity/sampleAnalysis/3: The remaining trade-off is accepted because it fits a multistep refund process with timeouts that requires compensation if the inventory update succeeds but the payment fails.

saa-43-lambda-design./title: Lambda concurrency, retries, and event sources
saa-43-lambda-design./shortTitle: Lambda concurrency, retries, and event sources
saa-43-lambda-design./summary: Understand Lambda concurrency, retries, and event sources through requirements, constraints, trade-offs, and an architectural decision that can be explained to the BlueRetail team.
saa-43-lambda-design./opening/0: BlueRetail is changing its architecture, and its event-processing function is experiencing unexpected load spikes and exhausting connections to the backend database. A diagram with many AWS icons is not necessarily a good design: what matters is which requirement each component addresses, which failure modes remain, and whether the operations team can manage it. Lesson 43 starts with this scenario and turns Lambda concurrency, retries, and event sources into a sequence of evidence-based decisions rather than a list of features to memorize.
saa-43-lambda-design./opening/1: In SAA-C03, questions often present several technically viable options, but only one best matches keywords such as “least operational effort,” “no data loss,” “low cost,” “multiple accounts,” or “no use of the public internet.” Lambda concurrency, retries, and event sources should be examined from four perspectives: concurrency, event source mapping, retry behavior, and execution duration. Each perspective eliminates a group of answers that conflict with the requirements or introduce unacceptable responsibilities.
saa-43-lambda-design./opening/2: The scope of Lesson 43—Lambda concurrency, retries, and event sources—is aligned with objective SAA-C03 3.2 in the current AWS Exam Guide. The GitHub topic repository is used only to check coverage and content structure; all explanations, cases, and diagrams here are original, while the technical conclusions are validated against AWS Documentation and the Well-Architected Framework. After this lesson, you should be able to explain not only what to choose, but also why the other options should not be chosen.
saa-43-lambda-design./sections/0/title: Mental model: start with requirements, not services
saa-43-lambda-design./sections/0/paragraphs/0: Think of an AWS architecture as a contract among the business, application, and platform. The business defines what must remain operational and what level of loss is acceptable; the application describes traffic, data, and dependencies; and the platform translates those requirements into boundaries, services, policies, and observability mechanisms. For Lambda concurrency, retries, and event sources, concurrency is the entry point, event source mapping indicates how the system responds, and retry behavior and execution duration determine long-term operability. Moving directly from a requirement to a service name often causes one of these four layers to be overlooked.
saa-43-lambda-design./sections/0/paragraphs/1: A compact model that works for every lesson is R-C-D-E: Requirement, Constraint, Decision, Evidence. A requirement describes the outcome; a constraint limits the scope through factors such as Region, budget, or skills; a decision is the selected architectural structure; and evidence consists of metrics, logs, tests, or documentation confirming that the decision remains valid. By applying R-C-D-E to Lambda concurrency, retries, and event sources, learners can see that the same service may be appropriate for one workload but unsuitable for another. This skill is more important than memorizing the entire AWS catalog.
saa-43-lambda-design./sections/0/paragraphs/2: You have understood Lesson 43 when you can trace a request or operational event from beginning to end. Which boundary does the request cross, which identity is evaluated, where is the data retained, which component can fail, and which mechanism provides recovery? If the story breaks at concurrency or event source mapping, the diagram still contains an unverified assumption. Record that assumption next to the diagram before selecting another service.
saa-43-lambda-design./sections/1/title: Four concepts that must be connected
saa-43-lambda-design./sections/1/paragraphs/0: concurrency is the first element in decisions about Lambda concurrency, retries, and event sources, but its value becomes clear only when considered alongside business requirements. For SAA-C03, learners should not stop at asking, “What is this service used for?” Identify the input that concurrency receives, the state or policy it manages, the failure mode it addresses, and the resulting operational cost. At BlueRetail, the event-processing function is experiencing unexpected load spikes and exhausting connections to the backend database. Connecting concurrency with event source mapping clarifies the boundary of responsibility and explains why two similarly named options can produce different outcomes. A strong solution must trace the technical choice back to latency, availability, security, data, or the operations team’s capabilities.
saa-43-lambda-design./sections/1/paragraphs/1: event source mapping is the second element in decisions about Lambda concurrency, retries, and event sources, but its value becomes clear only when considered alongside business requirements. For SAA-C03, learners should not stop at asking, “What is this service used for?” Identify the input that event source mapping receives, the state or policy it manages, the failure mode it addresses, and the resulting operational cost. At BlueRetail, the event-processing function is experiencing unexpected load spikes and exhausting connections to the backend database. Connecting event source mapping with retry behavior clarifies the boundary of responsibility and explains why two similarly named options can produce different outcomes. A strong solution must trace the technical choice back to latency, availability, security, data, or the operations team’s capabilities.
saa-43-lambda-design./sections/1/paragraphs/2: retry behavior is the third element in decisions about Lambda concurrency, retries, and event sources, but its value becomes clear only when considered alongside business requirements. For SAA-C03, learners should not stop at asking, “What is this service used for?” Identify the input that retry behavior receives, the state or policy it manages, the failure mode it addresses, and the resulting operational cost. At BlueRetail, the event-processing function is experiencing unexpected load spikes and exhausting connections to the backend database. Connecting retry behavior with execution duration clarifies the boundary of responsibility and explains why two similarly named options can produce different outcomes. A strong solution must trace the technical choice back to latency, availability, security, data, or the operations team’s capabilities.
saa-43-lambda-design./sections/1/paragraphs/3: execution duration is the fourth element in decisions about Lambda concurrency, retries, and event sources, but its value becomes clear only when considered alongside business requirements. For SAA-C03, learners should not stop at asking, “What is this service used for?” Identify the input that execution duration receives, the state or policy it manages, the failure mode it addresses, and the resulting operational cost. At BlueRetail, the event-processing function is experiencing unexpected load spikes and exhausting connections to the backend database. Connecting execution duration with concurrency clarifies the boundary of responsibility and explains why two similarly named options can produce different outcomes. A strong solution must trace the technical choice back to latency, availability, security, data, or the operations team’s capabilities.
saa-43-lambda-design./sections/2/title: Make decisions step by step and document the trade-offs
saa-43-lambda-design./sections/2/paragraphs/0: A decision involving concurrency should be recorded as a chain of reasoning, not as a standalone service name. First, confirm which requirements are mandatory, then check quota limits, Region, consistency, network path, and the responsibility model. Next, compare concurrency with retry behavior using the same criteria; do not compare one feature of option A with the total cost of option B. Finally, state the remaining trade-off and how it will be observed after deployment. In Lesson 43, this approach helps BlueRetail understand why the selected option is good enough in the current context and which signals would trigger an architectural review.
saa-43-lambda-design./sections/2/paragraphs/1: A decision involving event source mapping should be recorded as a chain of reasoning, not as a standalone service name. First, confirm which requirements are mandatory, then check quota limits, Region, consistency, network path, and the responsibility model. Next, compare event source mapping with execution duration using the same criteria; do not compare one feature of option A with the total cost of option B. Finally, state the remaining trade-off and how it will be observed after deployment. In Lesson 43, this approach helps BlueRetail understand why the selected option is good enough in the current context and which signals would trigger an architectural review.
saa-43-lambda-design./sections/2/paragraphs/2: A decision involving retry behavior should be recorded as a chain of reasoning, not as a standalone service name. First, confirm which requirements are mandatory, then check quota limits, Region, consistency, network path, and the responsibility model. Next, compare retry behavior with concurrency using the same criteria; do not compare one feature of option A with the total cost of option B. Finally, state the remaining trade-off and how it will be observed after deployment. In Lesson 43, this approach helps BlueRetail understand why the selected option is good enough in the current context and which signals would trigger an architectural review.
saa-43-lambda-design./sections/2/paragraphs/3: A decision involving execution duration should be recorded as a chain of reasoning, not as a standalone service name. First, confirm which requirements are mandatory, then check quota limits, Region, consistency, network path, and the responsibility model. Next, compare execution duration with event source mapping using the same criteria; do not compare one feature of option A with the total cost of option B. Finally, state the remaining trade-off and how it will be observed after deployment. In Lesson 43, this approach helps BlueRetail understand why the selected option is good enough in the current context and which signals would trigger an architectural review.
saa-43-lambda-design./sections/3/title: Design for failure before it occurs
saa-43-lambda-design./sections/3/paragraphs/0: Failure mode 1 for Lambda concurrency, retries, and event sources occurs when the project team assumes that concurrency automatically addresses every related layer. In reality, service availability does not replace correct access permissions, replication does not replace backups, encryption does not automatically grant permission to use a key, and autoscaling cannot fix a stateful dependency. At BlueRetail, map the blast radius of concurrency, identify the part for which AWS is responsible, and identify what the application team must still configure or test. Then select a metric, log, or event that can provide an early warning. Analyzing the failure mode first ensures that the answer is valid not only when the system is healthy, but also provides a recovery path when an AZ, connection, credential, or operational process fails.
saa-43-lambda-design./sections/3/paragraphs/1: Failure mode 2 for Lambda concurrency, retries, and event sources occurs when the project team assumes that event source mapping automatically addresses every related layer. In reality, service availability does not replace correct access permissions, replication does not replace backups, encryption does not automatically grant permission to use a key, and autoscaling cannot fix a stateful dependency. At BlueRetail, map the blast radius of event source mapping, identify the part for which AWS is responsible, and identify what the application team must still configure or test. Then select a metric, log, or event that can provide an early warning. Analyzing the failure mode first ensures that the answer is valid not only when the system is healthy, but also provides a recovery path when an AZ, connection, credential, or operational process fails.
saa-43-lambda-design./sections/3/paragraphs/2: Failure mode 3 for Lambda concurrency, retries, and event sources occurs when the project team assumes that retry behavior automatically addresses every related layer. In reality, service availability does not replace correct access permissions, replication does not replace backups, encryption does not automatically grant permission to use a key, and autoscaling cannot fix a stateful dependency. At BlueRetail, map the blast radius of retry behavior, identify the part for which AWS is responsible, and identify what the application team must still configure or test. Then select a metric, log, or event that can provide an early warning. Analyzing the failure mode first ensures that the answer is valid not only when the system is healthy, but also provides a recovery path when an AZ, connection, credential, or operational process fails.
saa-43-lambda-design./sections/4/title: Apply the decision to the BlueRetail case
saa-43-lambda-design./sections/4/paragraphs/0: BlueRetail has retail stores, an e-commerce website, internal applications, and an operations team working across multiple accounts. In Lesson 43, the event-processing function is experiencing unexpected load spikes and exhausting connections to the backend database. The first step is to separate the stakeholders: users need a stable experience, security needs boundaries and an audit trail, finance needs cost attribution, and the product team needs to make changes quickly. A design optimized for only one stakeholder creates operational debt for another.
saa-43-lambda-design./sections/4/paragraphs/1: The architecture team documents three options centered on concurrency, event source mapping, and retry behavior. Each option is scored using the same matrix: fulfillment of mandatory requirements, blast radius, recovery path, operational burden, scalability, and cost per unit of use. execution duration serves as the final validation condition. If an option wins only because the team is familiar with it, the decision lacks sufficient evidence and the constraints must be revisited.
saa-43-lambda-design./sections/4/paragraphs/2: The conclusion for Lambda concurrency, retries, and event sources is written in four sentences: which structure is selected; which requirements it satisfies; which trade-off is accepted; and which metric or review will trigger reassessment. This format helps developers, security, and operations understand the same decision. In an exam question, the four sentences also help eliminate distractors: an option that fails a mandatory requirement is eliminated first, even if it offers many attractive features.
saa-43-lambda-design./sections/5/title: Choosing the right action in SAA-C03 questions
saa-43-lambda-design./sections/5/paragraphs/0: Read an SAA-C03 question in this order: the assigned role, the current state, the required outcome, the constraints, and the verb at the end of the question. For Lambda concurrency, retries, and event sources, mark every detail related to concurrency and event source mapping. Then classify the answers as fully meeting the requirements, partially meeting them, introducing additional risk, or being out of scope. Do not choose an answer merely because it mentions the correct service; AWS tests your ability to combine multiple requirements into the most appropriate solution.
saa-43-lambda-design./sections/5/paragraphs/1: Absolute terms such as “no data loss,” “no downtime,” or “all traffic must remain private” require careful scrutiny. They may require a completely different design from terms such as “minimize,” “acceptable,” or “prioritize cost.” In Lesson 43, use retry behavior to assess the level of assurance and execution duration to determine whether the operations team can sustain the option. An answer that is correct in terms of features but provides the wrong level of assurance is still a distractor.
saa-43-lambda-design./sections/5/paragraphs/2: For Lesson 43—Lambda concurrency, retries, and event sources—objective SAA-C03 3.2 indicates the required depth. For design, you must balance quality attributes; for determine, you must interpret the facts and select a cause or option; for improve, you must preserve the existing constraints and reduce risk through an appropriate change. Conclude each answer with evidence: a metric, log, policy evaluation, recovery test, or cost report. Evidence turns an answer from a guess into a defensible architectural decision.

saa-43-lambda-design./activity/title: Architecture review: Lambda concurrency, retries, and event sources
saa-43-lambda-design./activity/context: BlueRetail is preparing to approve a change because its event-processing function is experiencing unexpected load spikes and exhausting connections to the backend database. You must review the architecture before the team implements it or incurs cost commitments.
saa-43-lambda-design./activity/learnerRole: You are the Solutions Architect responsible for a workload, translating requirements into decisions and clearly explaining trade-offs to stakeholders.
saa-43-lambda-design./activity/currentArchitecture: Users → access layer → workload → data store; the review points are concurrency → event source mapping → retry behavior → execution duration.
saa-43-lambda-design./activity/requirements/0: The solution must correctly address the requirement related to concurrency.
saa-43-lambda-design./activity/requirements/1: The recovery path must demonstrate the role of event source mapping.
saa-43-lambda-design./activity/requirements/2: Security and operations must be able to observe retry behavior without relying on assumptions.
saa-43-lambda-design./activity/constraints/0: Do not add a service merely to conceal a requirement that has not been clearly defined.
saa-43-lambda-design./activity/constraints/1: The trade-off involving execution duration must be accepted by the stakeholders.
saa-43-lambda-design./activity/constraints/2: Do not change the storage schema or account boundary without an architectural justification.
saa-43-lambda-design./activity/tasks/0: Rewrite the requirement as measurable criteria.
saa-43-lambda-design./activity/tasks/1: Compare at least two options based on concurrency, event source mapping, and retry behavior.
saa-43-lambda-design./activity/tasks/2: Select an option, document the trade-off, and propose post-deployment evidence.
saa-43-lambda-design./activity/reasoningSteps/0: Separate the business outcome, technical constraints, and unverified assumptions.
saa-43-lambda-design./activity/reasoningSteps/1: Diagram the request and data flow, including the trust boundary and blast radius.
saa-43-lambda-design./activity/reasoningSteps/2: Eliminate options that fail mandatory requirements before optimizing.
saa-43-lambda-design./activity/reasoningSteps/3: Compare the remaining options across reliability, security, performance, cost, and operations.
saa-43-lambda-design./activity/reasoningSteps/4: Document the decision, trade-off, recovery path, and review metric.
saa-43-lambda-design./activity/deliverables/0: An architecture diagram showing boundaries and data-flow direction.
saa-43-lambda-design./activity/deliverables/1: A decision matrix with at least two options.
saa-43-lambda-design./activity/deliverables/2: A concise architecture decision record containing the trade-off and evidence.
saa-43-lambda-design./activity/rubric/0/evidence: The decision can be traced back to concurrency and does not introduce assumptions beyond the scenario.
saa-43-lambda-design./activity/rubric/0/importance: Prevents selecting services based on preference or isolated keywords.
saa-43-lambda-design./activity/rubric/1/evidence: Compares event source mapping, retry behavior, and execution duration using the same criteria.
saa-43-lambda-design./activity/rubric/1/importance: AWS architecture always involves balance; there is no universally best option.
saa-43-lambda-design./activity/rubric/2/evidence: The submission identifies a failure mode, recovery path, and validation metric or test.
saa-43-lambda-design./activity/rubric/2/importance: A design is trustworthy only when it can be operated and validated after deployment.
saa-43-lambda-design./activity/sampleAnalysis/0: The primary requirement is rewritten around concurrency; event source mapping is a constraint that directly affects the choice.
saa-43-lambda-design./activity/sampleAnalysis/1: An option that prioritizes retry behavior is eliminated if it increases the operational burden of execution duration without adding value.
saa-43-lambda-design./activity/sampleAnalysis/2: The proposed architecture combines concurrency and event source mapping and clearly documents the blast radius, recovery path, and review metric.
saa-43-lambda-design./activity/sampleAnalysis/3: The remaining trade-off is accepted because it fits the situation in which the event-processing function is experiencing unexpected load spikes and exhausting connections to the backend database.
saa-43-lambda-design./activity/commonMistakes/0: Listing services without connecting them to the requirement.
saa-43-lambda-design./activity/commonMistakes/1: Describing only the happy path and ignoring failure modes.
saa-43-lambda-design./activity/commonMistakes/2: Saying that “AWS manages it” without identifying the customer’s responsibilities.
saa-43-lambda-design./activity/reflectionQuestions/0: Which detail in the scenario eliminated the most options?
saa-43-lambda-design./activity/reflectionQuestions/1: Which metric or event would cause you to reassess the decision?
saa-43-lambda-design./activity/reflectionQuestions/2: If the budget or RTO changes, which part of the architecture must change first?
